- Benchmark baseline recorded with durability settings named. Abandoned-attempt code removed.

**Falsifiable ops-light claim:** one operator cycle — provision identity, issue + revoke credential, rotate a secret, verify the audit chain — through one documented workflow: no external service, no restart, no policy language.
**Plan-review release additions:** M3 requires the focused recovery gates AND every full/extended suite: U8.4, U11.4 and U11.9 `source-full` + checksum-matched `artifact-smoke`. The verification manifest must cover R1–R41/AE1–AE13 with zero stale/unowned ignored security cases. Failure artifacts must pass the unconditional safety gate before upload. U12.7's operator-authored explainer/second-reader evidence is a first-class release gate. Promotion uses the exact staged artifact digests that passed both-architecture smoke—never a rebuild.

**Exact-candidate evidence:** scheduled fuzz and controlled performance results must be fresh and green for the exact release commit/digest; PR smoke from another revision is insufficient. Native architecture × actual ext4/XFS/deployment coverage must match every public claim or the matrix is narrowed. Artifact-smoke runs against staged immutable bytes before promotion; post-promotion redownload proves those exact bytes, never a rebuild. The global manifest covers every numbered unit scenario as well as R/AE mappings and requires pure-logic unit coverage where applicable.

**Maintenance/recovery release checklist:** U10.7 real-prior migration (v0.1 synthetic N/A exactly once), U10.8 compaction, their state-bound signed+DR-rehearsed backup preconditions, all marker/atomic-install/pending-anchor fault cases, old-binary no-write refusal, and source-full/artifact-smoke stages are mandatory. Record-key rotation is explicit alongside metadata/audit rotations. M3 cannot close on an older abbreviated smoke list.

**M3 additions from plan-space audit:** the release gate includes backup/export list-show-resume owner recovery, reserve status-release-recreate on the claimed filesystems, reasoned identity/grant/credential authority changes, and historical rotation status that never labels a superseded target current. These commands and semantics must appear in generated help/docs, named unit/fault cases, source-full and applicable artifact-smoke stages, and the executed-case manifest with detailed secret-safe logs.
close_reason: M3: rotation-ready v0.1 — U7/U8/U11/U12 complete; release smoke local.

title: U0.1: Pin the client matrix with verified provenance (KTD14)
description:
**Build:** Resolve and pin, at execution time (KTD14): the latest stable HashiCorp `vault` CLI, the latest stable OpenBao `bao` CLI, and the two most recent fnox releases. One legacy Vault line is retained ONLY if it represents a documented deployed consumer or captures a materially different request contract — otherwise skip it.

**Provenance discipline:** verify every downloaded artifact checksum; record exact versions, artifact checksums, and download provenance. Pins are committed to CI and docs/operating.md so a client upgrade can never silently change what R2's config-only guarantee was tested against.

**Why:** compatibility is versioned evidence, not a permanent assumption. fnox's provider surface (`address`, `token`, `credential_command`) may change transport across releases; the guarantee holds for tested versions, not all future CLIs. The mount-discovery preflight is Vault-internal and may change per release — pinning is what makes the claim honest.

**Output:** research/compat/ notes + pinned-version manifest consumed by U11's CI and by the capture runs (sibling bead).

**Acceptance:** matrix of pinned versions exists with checksums + provenance; CI can fetch exactly these artifacts; the legacy-line decision (include or not) is recorded with its reason.
notes: Resolved 2026-07-16 from primary publisher feeds: Vault OSS 2.0.3, OpenBao 2.6.0, fnox 1.30.0 and 1.29.0 for Linux amd64. Independently downloaded all four exact archives; SHA-256 matched HashiCorp SHA256SUMS or GitHub publisher asset digests. Legacy Vault line skipped because no documented deployed consumer was supplied and no materially distinct request contract is yet evidenced. Added machine-readable manifest, checksum-refusing fetcher, static pin gate, manual-only workflow entry, and operating guidance. Verification: fetcher verified all four downloads; check-compat-pins, verify_runner, and complete 12-check local manifest (format, clippy, tests, docs, MSRV, harness, canary) passed. Automatic CI remained disabled.

title: U0.2: Recording stub + capture pinned client behavior (incl. bao rung proof)
description:
**Build:** A recording stub server plus capture runs for every pinned client (fnox ×2, vault, bao), using SYNTHETIC high-entropy canary credentials only (they must be scannable later — R25 canary discipline applies to fixtures too).

**The stub is a RESPONDING recorder, not a passive one** — a passive recorder cannot elicit the behavior this bead must capture: clients abort after the mounts preflight unless the stub returns a plausible KV v2 mount response, so later endpoints would never appear; retry/error behavior only exists if scripted failure responses exist; TLS-root supply is only observable if the stub serves TLS. Therefore: (a) serve minimally valid canned responses per endpoint (mounts preflight, KV read, seal-status, health, lookup-self, AppRole login — shapes cribbed from a live pinned `bao` dev server and themselves recorded as provenance); (b) serve TLS from a locally generated test CA so each client's root-supply mechanism (VAULT_CACERT / env / flag) is exercised and recorded; (c) run scripted fault passes per client (403, 404, 500, 503-sealed, connection reset, timeout) to capture retry and error handling.

**Capture per client:** method, path, query, headers, body shape, TLS configuration, subprocess execution (does this fnox version shell out to the `vault` CLI or speak HTTP itself? record the exact `vault` subcommands fnox invokes AND the HTTP requests those CLI versions emit), retry behavior, status/error handling.

**Path-encoding capture matrix (feeds G0's segment-character contract and KTD9's parser corpus):** for each pinned client, request secret paths containing spaces, UTF-8, percent-encoded characters (including %2F and %25), `.` and `..` segments, leading/trailing/double slashes, `+`, and an over-long segment; record the exact bytes each client puts on the wire (and where fnox/CLI reject client-side instead). The charter's threat model protects against authz bypass via ambiguous path encodings — G0 must not freeze a segment contract with zero recorded evidence behind it.

**Scripted AppRole pass:** run the pinned `vault`/`bao` CLI `write auth/approle/login role_id=… secret_id=…` against the stub, recording the request shape; record the live pinned `bao` dev server's login response as the response-shape provenance — U4.2/U4.4 assert their login envelope byte-shape against these fixtures.

**Named questions this bead must answer with evidence:**
1. Which endpoints does each client actually hit? (mount preflight? sys/seal-status? sys/health? auth/token/lookup-self?)
2. How are tokens and TLS roots supplied per client?
3. Can a fresh host run fnox WITHOUT the BUSL `vault` binary — in particular, does pointing fnox at the `bao` CLI work (BUSL exit-ladder rung 1: OpenBao's CLI is a vault-CLI fork; zero code — this capture proves or kills the rung)?
4. What path-segment characters do the pinned clients emit, and how are they encoded on the wire? (The resulting encoding table is a G0 freeze input.)

**Why:** the captured contract — not the KV v2 API docs — is the compatibility target (R1 declares the preflight and seal-status precisely because the CLI needs them). Everything U5 builds and U11 asserts starts from these recordings.

**Acceptance:** each pinned client's capture replays cleanly against the recorded set (deterministic enough to assert against); the four named questions have recorded answers, including the per-client path-encoding table and per-client fault-pass (retry/error) recordings.
**Plan-review capture harness:** provide an automated noninteractive scenario driver and unit-test the recorder endpoint state machine, TLS mode, scripted faults, retries, missing/out-of-order steps, and clean shutdown. Logs expose client/version/scenario/step/status/duration/retry/fixture path and redacted reproducer only—never headers, bodies, tokens, cookies, or TLS private material. Raw captures contain synthetic canaries only, live in a mode-0700 gitignored tmpfs/temp directory, and are never CI artifacts. An unconditional success/failure/panic/signal cleanup unlinks them after normalization; no secure-erasure claim is made for CoW filesystems or SSDs. Interrupted runs identify bounded safe cleanup paths. Client stdout/stderr is privately captured without tee and only normalized, sentinel-clean typed summaries may reach the console.
notes:
Intermediate investigation (2026-07-16): Verified downloaded client binaries report Vault 2.0.3, OpenBao 2.6.0, fnox 1.30.0 and 1.29.0. Inspected primary fnox v1.30.0 tag bec836c77fbd473b634e35646c2879564e168a0f: crates/fnox-core/src/providers/vault.rs hardcodes tokio Command::new("vault") and invokes `vault kv get -field=<field> <path>`; it does not speak Vault HTTP directly. Therefore BUSL rung 1 requires Bao exposed under executable name `vault` via a PATH-local symlink/wrapper; provider address/token still flow through VAULT_ADDR/VAULT_TOKEN. Runtime capture remains required for both pinned fnox versions and must record the resulting Bao HTTP traffic. Docker, OpenSSL, strace, timeout, and all four verified binaries are available locally. No raw capture has been committed and bead remains open.

Completion evidence (2026-07-16): Implemented a TLS responding recorder and noninteractive driver for pinned Vault 2.0.3, OpenBao 2.6.0, and fnox 1.30.0/1.29.0 through both CLI backends. Captured 11 path cases and six fault passes per rung, exact fnox subprocess argv, mount/KV/AppRole/seal/leader/health/lookup traffic, header/body shapes, TLS-root mechanism, retries, and exit status. Runtime proved both fnox versions work with OpenBao exposed as PATH-local vault. Raw/config/key/stdout/stderr data stayed in mode-0700 temporary storage and was deleted; normalized observations pass sentinel scan. Acceptance: full archive-hash-verified capture exited 0; ./scripts/check-compat-capture.sh passed 4 recorder/TLS lifecycle tests plus semantic replay for all 6 rungs; ./scripts/verify.sh all passed all 13 local checks; git diff --check passed; br dep cycles reported zero. CI remains workflow_dispatch-only.

title: U0.3: Normalize + commit client-trace fixtures (canary-clean)
description:
**Build:** Fixture normalization pipeline + committed fixtures in tests/fixtures/client-traces/ as first-class artifacts (KTD14).

**Normalization removes, before ANY artifact is committed:** tokens, credential-command output, TLS private material, host paths, usernames, and nondeterministic identifiers. The secret-output canary scan (U11) covers these fixtures — the synthetic canary credentials used during capture must be absent from committed artifacts.

**Why:** these fixtures are what U4 asserts auth envelopes against, what U5 builds the router against, and what U11 replays forever. They are the frozen half of Gate G0. Committing raw captures would leak capture-host details and make the suite nondeterministic.

**Acceptance:** fixtures committed and replayable; normalization is a repeatable script (rerunning capture + normalize yields stable fixtures); canary scan over the fixture tree is clean.
**Plan-review normalizer tests:** adversarial fixtures cover secrets nested in bodies, header case/duplicates, URLs/host paths/usernames, TLS material, credential-command output, JSON/shell/base64 encodings, and unknown schema fields. Unknown/suspicious fields fail closed. Two independent normalizations of identical input are byte-identical; fixture manifest records schema/tool version and digest. Both sensitive-field and registered-canary scans must pass before commit; logs name case/path/digest only, never capture content.

**Artifact safety:** unlike U0.2's private raw-capture bootstrap, every normalized/committed fixture, compatibility artifact, and generated doc from this bead directly uses U11.9a typed provenance and passes the full binary-safe U11.6 same-job scan before render/retention. Hostile filenames/encodings and seeded capture credentials are negative tests.
notes: Completion evidence (2026-07-16): Added strict client-trace normalizer, six retained fixtures, SHA-256 manifest, fixture replay, and a Rust retention gate using U11.9a typed Harness provenance plus U11.6 FullScanner before promotion. Normalization removes durations and constructs only allowlisted schema; unknown/nested fields fail closed; headers canonicalize case/duplicates. Adversarial tests cover nested secrets, URLs, host paths/usernames, TLS private markers, credential output, base64 encoded canaries, and hostile encoded filenames. Two independent normalizations were byte-identical; manifest digests verified; all six client/backend fixtures replayed; registered-canary/sensitive scans were clean. Acceptance: ./scripts/check-compat-capture.sh passed 8 Python tests, capture replay, normalization check, typed/full scan, and fixture replay; cargo gate negative tests passed; ./scripts/verify.sh all passed all 13 local checks including fmt/clippy/tests/docs/MSRV/harness/canary; git diff --check and br dep cycles passed. CI remains workflow_dispatch-only.

title: U0.4: docs/compatibility.md draft + BUSL exit-ladder verdict
description:
**Build:** Initial docs/compatibility.md — a human-readable matrix naming, per client version: every endpoint required and every prerequisite binary. Plus the recorded BUSL exit-ladder verdict.

**BUSL exit ladder (Scope Boundaries, wayfinder T03) — cheapest rung first:**
1. Point fnox at the `bao` CLI (zero code; U0.2's capture proves or kills this rung).
2. Contribute a direct-HTTP provider upstream to fnox — CONFIRMED welcome by jdx (fnox discussion #615): replace the existing vault provider's CLI-backed internals in place, preserving configuration/auth/TLS behavior, with compat tests against both Vault and OpenBao.
3. Document `vault` CLI installation with an honest licensing note — never claiming a fully-OSI toolchain while it is the only path.
A shim is NOT a v0.1 gate; it is post-v0.1 and evidence-gated (built only if all three rungs fail AND someone is actually blocked).

**Also:** update R1's endpoint matrix, AE8's documented onboarding sequence, and U4/U5/U11 fixture expectations wherever captured behavior contradicts plan assumptions — evidence wins over prose memory. Record contradictions explicitly in this bead when closing it.

**Acceptance:** matrix reviewed; per-client endpoint + prerequisite table complete; ladder verdict recorded; any plan-contradiction noted and propagated to the affected beads.
**Plan-review draft scope/parallelism:** this draft may begin from the automated capture after U0.2 while normalization proceeds in parallel; G0 still requires both. Include per-version token/TLS supply, path-character/list-root behavior, retry/error divergences, prerequisite binary/licensing facts, and tested configuration examples. It must never quote raw capture bodies/headers. Generated documentation is canary-scanned; final facts link only to normalized committed fixtures.

**Artifact safety:** unlike U0.2's private raw-capture bootstrap, every normalized/committed fixture, compatibility artifact, and generated doc from this bead directly uses U11.9a typed provenance and passes the full binary-safe U11.6 same-job scan before render/retention. Hostile filenames/encodings and seeded capture credentials are negative tests.
notes:
Conflict review (2026-07-16): This bead requires documented list-root behavior and facts linked only to normalized fixtures, but the U0.2/U0.3 fixtures contain KV get, status/health/lookup and AppRole calls—no LIST operation. Chosen resolution: state list-root/trailing behavior as unobserved for pinned clients, make no compatibility claim, and document the conservative provisional server rule from U3.1 (explicit LIST/list=true only; root/trailing empty accepted only there) pending a future pinned LIST capture. Rationale: evidence wins over the plan assumption and silence would falsely imply fixture support. All other facts will link to normalized manifest/fixtures.

Implementation and verification (2026-07-16): generated docs/compatibility.md from the six normalized pinned-client fixtures; validated endpoint, credential, subprocess, path, and fault facts; recorded BUSL exit ladder (Bao alias passes, direct HTTP welcome but unnecessary, BUSL Vault fallback, no v0.1 shim); updated R1, AE8, and U5 plan language; propagated needs-user-review notes to G0, U5.1, U5.3, and U11.1. Retention gate performs direct U11.6 full-artifact-v1 scanning plus typed U11.9a provenance capture before compare/promote. Hostile base64-body and encoded-filename tests pass. ./scripts/check-compat-capture.sh passes. Full local ./scripts/verify.sh all passes all 13 checks, run_id 0d979d73681a3dffc01abb9312222a90. No automatic CI was triggered.

title: Gate G0: client contract freeze
description:
**Review-and-freeze decision gate (G0).** Freezes the versioned client contract and client prerequisites: which endpoints each pinned client needs (R1's declared surface), the exact request/response shapes (fixtures), token/TLS supply mechanisms, and the supported segment-character contract that KTD9's parser will publish — the latter frozen FROM U0.2's recorded path-encoding table (per-client wire bytes for spaces, UTF-8, percent-encoding incl. %2F/%25, dot segments, slash variants), never from assumption.

**What G0 blocks:** the FINAL data-plane router (U5) and public compatibility claims. What it does NOT block: U1 scaffolding, configuration, local init design, control socket, transport work.

**Done-when (from the unit's own definition):** U0's named questions are answered with committed evidence — which endpoints each pinned client hits, how tokens and TLS roots are supplied, whether a fresh host runs fnox without the BUSL `vault` binary, and what path-segment encodings each client emits — not when the harness is beautiful.

**Exit:** R1's endpoint matrix, AE8's documented sequence, the segment-character/encoding table, and U5/U11's fixtures updated from captured behavior. Feeds milestone M0.
notes:
U0.4 review conflict (2026-07-16): G0 asks to freeze LIST/list=true behavior from captured fixtures, but U0.2/U0.3 captured no LIST operation. Resolution: freeze the evidence gap rather than invent a client claim; use the narrow provisional router rule documented in docs/compatibility.md until a pinned LIST capture replaces it. Also add /v1/sys/leader to the status endpoint set because both direct pinned CLIs called it. Rationale: fail closed on ambiguous paths and follow observed requests. Evidence: generated compatibility document, normalized six-rung fixtures, compatibility document gate, and local capture replay.

Gate verification (2026-07-16): froze the six exact pinned Linux-amd64 client rungs, prerequisite executable behavior, endpoint/request shapes, VAULT_TOKEN/VAULT_CACERT supply, path encoding decisions, BUSL exit-ladder result, and explicit LIST evidence gap in docs/compatibility.md plus normalized fixtures. R1/AE8/U5/U11 affected contracts carry the evidence corrections. ./scripts/check-compat-capture.sh passes, including byte-stable doc regeneration and full-artifact-v1/typed-provenance gates. cargo test --locked --test raw_target passes 8/8, including normalized-fixture governance and explicit LIST-root scoping. Full local 13-check verification run 0d979d73681a3dffc01abb9312222a90 passed on the same source tree. No automatic CI was triggered.

title: U1.1: Cargo scaffold, clap CLI, config loading, typed secret-source descriptors
description:
**Build:** Cargo workspace scaffold (single binary crate), `clap` CLI skeleton (`init`, `serve`; later subcommands slot in per unit), configuration loading, and typed secret-source descriptors.

**Config rules (R18/R37):**
- Sources: environment + a config file. UNKNOWN KEYS ARE FATAL — refuse to start naming the key (typo'd security settings must never be silently ignored).
- Secret values (age identity, TLS key passphrases, any credential) are accepted ONLY through typed secret-source descriptors: stdin, inherited file descriptor, systemd-style credentials directory (`LoadCredential`/`LoadCredentialEncrypted` layout, read at boot), or hidden interactive TTY prompt. Environment-variable delivery is development-only behind an explicit unsafe flag. Secret material via argv is refused (argv is world-readable via /proc and lands in shell history).
- Every refusal exits non-zero naming the offending setting.

**Also:** pin MSRV, commit Cargo.lock (CI enforces --locked from day one, R38), rustfmt/clippy config, module layout per plan file map (src/config.rs, src/startup.rs, src/control/, src/cli.rs). Dependency baseline from the fnox-proven stack: tokio, axum, clap, rustls; add redb/age/chacha20poly1305/blake3/ed25519-dalek/zeroize/secrecy as their units land.

**Acceptance:** binary builds; `--help` documents settings; unknown-config-key and argv-secret refusal tests pass (seed tests/startup.rs); secret-source descriptor parsing round-trips each channel.
**Plan-review config/secret-source contract:** freeze deterministic file/environment/CLI precedence, duplicate declaration behavior, mutually exclusive source descriptors, unknown-key refusal, and the rule that at most one setting may consume stdin. Unit tests cover every precedence/conflict pair and EOF/cancel/FD/TTY/credentials-dir parsing. Diagnostics identify setting and source KIND only; no value, raw env, FD path content, or prompt input is rendered.

**Single-mount defaults (trusted config owner):** freeze `mounts.secret.cas_required` (boolean, default false) and `mounts.secret.max_versions` (integer 0..1024; 0 or unset means built-in default 10). Only the fixed `secret/` mount exists. Unknown mount/default keys, wrong types, negatives, overflow, and >1024 are fatal before bind. Config/file/env/CLI precedence follows this bead's general rules. Existing paths without an explicit override inherit the effective mount value dynamically after restart; config changes never eagerly prune and take effect for CAS admission/current metadata immediately, while max-version pruning occurs only on the next successful write. Logs report key/source/effective non-secret value only.
notes:
Goal review note (2026-07-16): Contract ambiguity: the primary config rules authorize setting values from file and environment only, while the plan-review text later says file/environment/CLI precedence. Resolution: CLI participates only through --config (which overrides OLSS_CONFIG) and --unsafe-dev-secret-env; setting values remain file/environment-only, and all secret values remain forbidden on argv. This is safest and simplest because it preserves R37 and avoids a third configuration-value surface. Separate underspecification: no numeric MSRV was given. Resolution: pin Rust 1.85.0, the minimum required by the selected current clap 4.6.2 and toml 1.1.3 dependencies and edition 2024. Implementation: Cargo scaffold, strict typed config, descriptor channels, sanitized refusals, and naming propagation. Verification evidence: cargo +1.85.0 build --locked; cargo +1.85.0 clippy --locked --all-targets -- -D warnings; cargo +1.85.0 test --locked --all-targets (17 tests); cargo fmt --check; scripts/check-naming.sh.

Follow-up review note (2026-07-16): U11.0 requires cargo test --locked --doc, but Cargo exits 101 for a package with only a binary target. Resolution applied downstream: preserve one package and one deployable binary while adding an internal library target for shared modules/doctests. This is the smallest way to satisfy both contracts; runtime and public artifact tuple stay unchanged.

title: U1.2: init — exclusive one-time local initialization, disclosure-before-commit (R17/R30)
description:
**Build:** `init` — local, exclusive, one-time initialization (R30) with R17's failure-safe credential disclosure ordering.

**Behavior:**
1. Take the explicit OS-level exclusive lock (fd-lock on a lockfile in the data directory — KTD2 caveat: redb's internal lock silently no-ops on no-lock filesystems, so we never trust library-internal locking). Refuse a non-empty or already-initialized directory; refuse a filesystem that cannot hold the lock. **"Non-empty" means: contains any entry other than strictly validated incomplete-init artifacts — the lockfile, a store file containing no committed initialization transaction, and U6.5-owned reserve provisioning temp/orphan files whose names, owner, mode, allocation, and recorded metadata match the init protocol.** A rerun over such artifacts is a clean re-init (it truncates/replaces the uncommitted store file); anything else (foreign files, `lost+found`, an initialized store) refuses with the allowlisted entry kind, opaque artifact id/run-keyed name digest, and bounded count, instructing the operator to inspect the local directory—never echoing a raw basename. Without this definition the lockfile makes the directory non-empty and a literal implementation refuses its own happy path — and every abort-before-commit rerun.
2. Require an active age identity through U1.1's typed secret-source descriptor, derive its public active recipient, and optionally validate one distinct recovery PUBLIC recipient. Create the keyring envelope to that canonical set and decrypt-self-test it with the supplied active identity before any credential disclosure or commit; a wrong identity, duplicate/equal recovery recipient, malformed public recipient, or self-test failure leaves no initialized state. Never generate a throwaway recipient inside init. Then create store id, schema version, the tested encrypted keyring, audit genesis, and the first management identity in ONE transaction. (Internals wired in as units land: keyring creation = U2.3, audit genesis = U6.3, first identity + credential verifier + bootstrap grant = U3/U4 — see U3.2's init wiring: identity, admin-bundle grant, and credential must commit atomically or the bootstrap credential authenticates but is denied everything, recovery.reserve preallocation = U6.5 (R27). This bead owns the command frame, locking, refusals, transaction shape, and disclosure ordering.)
3. **R17 ordering — disclosure BEFORE durability:** open and validate the credential output sink (TTY or caller-supplied file descriptor) FIRST → generate the credential → write and flush it to the sink → only then commit the initialization transaction containing its verifier. A crash or broken pipe before commit leaves NO initialized store and a harmless disclosed string (init reruns cleanly); a crash after commit leaves an operator already holding the credential. A failed sink write ABORTS initialization.
4. The first management credential carries audience `control` and a short bootstrap TTL — **default 24h, overridable via an init-time `--bootstrap-ttl` flag**; it is stored only as a keyed verifier — never recoverable afterward (R24). No bootstrap route exists on any network listener, ever. **The disclosure output prints the credential's expiry and the exact finite rollover next step:** use the bootstrap over the control socket to issue a labeled, bounded, non-renewable normal control token through U4.5, test that new token on an authenticated control command, then revoke the bootstrap accessor. Normal admin/timer credentials are never described as durable or immortal; operators repeat issue→verify→revoke-predecessor before expiry. **Expiry/loss recovery:** the offline R41 emergency control-credential command (U8.3 — the same primitive clock repair uses); docs/operating.md documents it as the bootstrap-expired path. An initialized store with an expired bootstrap credential is recoverable, never a designed lockout.
5. Repeated or concurrent `init` cannot create a second first-administrator; re-init of an initialized store is refused. **Audit split (R30):** attempts against an initialized, UNLOCKED store are refused AND recorded in that store's audit chain; attempts while another process holds the lock, or before any store exists, are refused with a non-zero exit and stderr report only — no chain write is possible in those states, and the acceptance tests assert exactly this split.

**Acceptance:** broken/unwritable sink → abort before commit, no store, rerun succeeds; kill between sink flush and commit → retryable uninitialized dir; kill after commit → store exists, disclosed credential authenticates; two concurrent inits → exactly one first identity; rerun over leftover lockfile + uncommitted store file succeeds; init into a directory containing any foreign file refuses with kind+opaque id+keyed digest but never its raw name; re-init attempt against an initialized unlocked store is refused AND appears in the audit chain; disclosure output includes bootstrap expiry + finite issue→verify→revoke next-step text; active identity derivation/envelope self-test and optional distinct recovery-public-recipient validation occur before commit.
**Plan-review init test split/sink hardening:** U1.2-owned tests use a spy transaction to prove sink open+validation+write+flush precede commit, plus exclusive lock, concurrent init, incomplete-artifact reconciliation and foreign-file refusal. Downstream keyring/identity/credential/audit/reserve integration tails carry explicit owner ids in the ignore ledger. Freeze hard min/default/max bootstrap TTL and overflow/zero behavior. Raw-credential sinks are fstat-validated and persistent regular/block-device sinks are REFUSED. Approved sinks are a hidden TTY, pipe/socket, or anonymous memory-backed FD; non-secret expiry/next-step/JSON metadata uses ordinary stdout separately. The shipped CLI supports piping directly into encryption/password-manager tooling for durable custody. Logs show stage/reason/expiry only—never credential bytes; every failed path proves no store/identity/audit success became authoritative.

**Recovery-reserve init protocol (binding):** U6.5 provisions real allocated blocks through a no-follow same-directory temp file, verifies allocation/owner/mode, fsyncs, atomically renames to `recovery.reserve`, and fsyncs the parent BEFORE the initialization DB transaction commits. That transaction records the expected reserve identity/size/allocation metadata. Startup/retry reconciles every temp/reserve/record combination before admission: a valid orphan may be reused, malformed/foreign artifacts fail closed, and a committed initialized store without its matching reserve cannot serve. There is no fictional atomic filesystem+DB action; named kill points cover every boundary and U6.5 owns the deferred integration tail.

**Online/offline disclosure protocol:** offline init writes+flushes the approved sink before commit. Any later online caller of the shared R17 primitive uses a request-nonce/digest handshake over the owner socket: the CLI writes+flushes the approved sink, ACKs the nonce/digest, and only then may the daemon commit verifier+audit state. Disconnect before commit leaves an unusable orphan; a lost final reply leaves a usable value already delivered.

**Key-custody/init acceptance:** `key age-identity generate` (U2.3) → encrypted/password-manager custody of the private identity → `init` typed-source input → envelope self-test → clean restart with runtime/tmpfs credential is the canonical F1 path. Tests cover active-only and active+recovery setup, duplicate/equal recipients, wrong active identity, malformed/unsupported recipient, source EOF/cancel, self-test fault, no private identity in argv/env/persistent plaintext/log/artifact, and restart using only the provisioned active identity. Recovery remains optional for a healthy store; U8.3 accepts any identity that unwraps the current keyring for offline emergency access.

**Hostile-name diagnostics:** seeded credential/canary/control-character/invalid-UTF8 basenames, many entries, symlink/hardlink, and rename races never render raw names/context in stderr, JSON, harness logs, or sanitized manifests; diagnostics remain locally actionable through opaque id/digest and directory-inspection guidance.
notes:
Review conflict (2026-07-16): the phrase “hidden TTY” is ambiguous for a credential OUTPUT sink because terminal output is visible even though echo settings govern input. Resolution: accept a pre-opened character-device/TTY FD as the named sink class, never mirror it to stdout/logs, and document that terminal visibility/custody is the operator responsibility; prefer a pipe, Unix socket, or anonymous memory FD for durable encrypted/password-manager custody. Persistent regular and block-device sinks are refused. Rationale: preserves the listed sink class without falsely promising invisible terminal output.

Implementation and verification (2026-07-16): added the real U1.2 initialization frame with owner-only directory validation, explicit flock, strict lock/uncommitted-store/reserve artifact reconciliation, bounded keyed diagnostics without raw names, symlink/hardlink/rename-race refusal, coupled Write+AsFd sink validation, 5m/24h/7d TTL bounds, prepare/self-test-before-disclosure, credential write+flush-before-commit, retry semantics, initialized-store audit callback, finite issue→verify→revoke receipt, CLI flags and safe integration-pending refusal, operating guidance, and explicit downstream owner ledger entries. Focused init suite passes 10/10; startup/startup_skeleton and clippy pass. Full local ./scripts/verify.sh all passes all 13 checks, run_id a2c2cbaef7c683f0afd096f35c56798d. No automatic CI was triggered.

title: U1.3: serve fail-closed startup validation matrix + shutdown skeleton (R18/R35/R36)
notes:
Conflict review (2026-07-16): Acceptance names real store/keyring/schema/lifecycle/clock/audit/reserve states whose owning beads (U2.2/U2.3/U6.3/U6.5/U8/U10) are still open and are not dependencies, while the same bead explicitly requires U1 skeleton tests separated from downstream integration tails in an ignore ledger. Chosen resolution: implement the complete closed typed startup/shutdown contract, pure fail-closed validator, real data-directory and pre-bind listener seams, and live health skeleton now; record each unavailable storage/audit/reserve adapter with exact owner bead and acceptance tail, without inventing a temporary store format. Freeze skeleton policy at 30s drain; queued-not-started work cancels at admission stop, begun transactions may finish only inside deadline; deadline exit 75 with unclean marker, second signal exit 130 with immediate unclean zeroization, clean exit 0 only after flush and unsigned shutdown commit. Rationale: preserves fail-closed invariants and avoids a fake persistence layer that downstream work would silently depend on.

Resolution/implementation evidence (2026-07-16): Implemented closed StartupSnapshot validator and stable StartupCode refusals for key material, initialization, transport, directory safety, store-id mismatch, schema migration/unsupported states, complete lifecycle enum and marker agreement, pending anchors/conflicting operations, clock, lock and reserve recovery. Added owner/mode/no-symlink directory inspection; no-follow owner-only flock with second-instance refusal; validate-before-bind listener seam and live health server. CLI serve now refuses missing key material and never treats a directory as initialized. Implemented 30s shutdown hooks/order with queued cancellation, bounded begun drain, flush, unsigned shutdown commit, unclean failure/deadline handling, second-signal 130, and unconditional zeroization. Added docs/integration-tail-ledger.json with 12 concrete downstream owner/verification tails instead of a fake store format. Verification: tests/startup.rs 6/6 and startup_skeleton.rs 9/9 passed, including refusal matrix, no-write schemas, marker/pending-anchor cases, real lock/directory checks, valid live health, shutdown success/failure/deadline/signal, and ledger validation. ./scripts/verify.sh all passed all 13 local checks including fmt/clippy/tests/docs/MSRV/harness/canary; git diff --check and br dep cycles passed.

title: U1.4: owner-only control socket + SO_PEERCRED + control router skeleton (R34)
description:
**Build:** The owner-restricted local control socket + control-plane router skeleton (R34, KTD1).

**Rules:**
- Management operations — identity, grant, credential, rotation, consumer, audit-query, backup, key ops — are reachable ONLY here (or via offline CLI commands). They are ABSENT from the remote router: a remote client with a valid management-capable token gets explicit route-not-found, not an authorization failure (AE12 seam).
- Unix socket with owner-only permissions. On Linux, each accepted connection must ALSO present a kernel-reported peer uid (SO_PEERCRED) equal to the configured service owner. The v0.1 owner-mode socket has no incoherent second UID/group/ACL allowlist; administrators run the CLI as the service account (for example via tightly scoped sudo). Socket mode bits, the peer-uid check, and the control credential are ALL required — none substitutes for another (defense in depth: mode bits can be raced/misconfigured, uid can be shared, credentials can leak; requiring all three means one failure is not a compromise).
- Control actions remain authenticated (control-audience credential) and audited — attributable even though local.
- Served with `axum::serve` on a tokio UnixListener (axum's own Unix-listener support — KTD1); same tower middleware family as the data plane where applicable.

**Why local-only:** the highest-privilege operations are simply not remotely reachable, and no incompatible pseudo-Vault management endpoints get invented. Remote administration, if it ever exists, is a later phase with its own authentication design.

**Acceptance:** management routes 404/absent on the remote router and succeed over the socket; socket created with owner-only mode bits (asserted via stat in tests/startup.rs scenario 12); disallowed peer uid → rejected and audited; wrong-audience credential on the socket → rejected (with U4).
**Plan-review Unix-socket lifecycle:** verify secure owner-only parent directory; `lstat` and refuse symlink/non-socket/wrong-owner paths; probe a socket before removing a stale one; refuse active listeners; bind under restrictive umask; verify owner/mode after bind; clean up only the inode this process created. SO_PEERCRED mapping has explicit unknown/wrong-UID refusal and cannot rely on mode bits alone. Startup/transport tests cover stale/active/race/symlink/mode/peer cases with safe inode/uid/reason logs.

**Plan-audit ownership correction:** socket mode, parent-directory policy, and SO_PEERCRED now describe one implementable rule: only the service-owner UID connects in v0.1. Root's ability to bypass mode bits is not treated as an application authorization mechanism; the control credential remains mandatory. Tests prove configured foreign UIDs cannot connect and the operating guide demonstrates the service-account invocation.
notes: Implementation review note (2026-07-16): Acceptance requires disallowed peer UID rejection to be audited, but the durable encrypted audit chain is owned by open bead olss-charter-qul.4.3 (U6.3) and is not a dependency of this bead; wrong-audience authentication is explicitly deferred to U4. Safest resolution: U1.4 requires a PeerAudit sink at bind time and emits a typed refusal for unavailable/wrong SO_PEERCRED before dropping the connection; U6.3 must provide the durable sink and U4 must wrap control routes with control-audience authentication. Until then only a non-mutating local status route exists, so no unauthenticated management mutation is exposed. Implementation adds distinct remote/control routers, axum Unix serving, current-service-UID SO_PEERCRED enforcement, 0600 socket and 0700-parent checks, active/stale/symlink/type/owner/mode/race refusal, inode-safe cleanup, and operator guidance. Verification: cargo test --locked --test control_socket (5/5), strict workspace clippy, and all 11 local verification-manifest checks including MSRV and canary gate passed; automatic CI remained disabled.

title: U1.5: clock model — high-water mark, tolerance, readiness, offline clock repair
description:
**Build:** The clock model (Assumptions — clock model) + offline `clock repair` command.

**Model:** wall clock is trusted for token expiry (R9), rotation age (R12), and audit display timestamps; hash-chain order is COMMIT SEQUENCE, not wall time. At startup record a (wall_clock, monotonic_clock) anchor; effective time advances from the monotonic clock while periodically checking wall-clock agreement; small drift reconciles within a documented tolerance. The store `meta` table persists a high-water mark of the highest accepted wall-clock time (updated on every durable coordinator transaction and by a bounded coalesced idle checkpoint loop through KTD8). EFFECTIVE TIME NEVER MOVES BACKWARD while the server runs. The persisted mark is itself a KTD15 MAC'd clear record (U2.5) — offline mark-lowering is detected at boot, closing the clock-rollback TTL-extension hole.

**Failure behavior:**
- Startup: clock behind the persisted mark beyond tolerance → refuse (exit non-zero); persisted mark implausibly ahead of current clock → refuse. An explicit audited local override may start the server once — behind-mark case ONLY; an implausibly-ahead mark is a wrong mark, recoverable exclusively via `clock repair` (an override boot would clamp effective time to the poisoned future mark and permanently poison U6.3's non-regressing envelope timeline). **Override semantics:** the override is a one-invocation CLI flag consumed at boot, its use written to the audit chain at open; the boot clamps effective time to max(wall clock, persisted mark) — the mark is PRESERVED, never lowered — so TTL conclusions never extend (a rollback-attack boot with the override still cannot resurrect or stretch outstanding bearer credentials; effective time stands at the mark until wall clock catches up). Repairing a genuinely wrong mark is exclusively `clock repair`'s job, with its epoch bump — the override never substitutes for it.
- Runtime: forward or backward discontinuity beyond tolerance → readiness false BEFORE the anomalous value is persisted (a rolled-back clock cannot silently extend bearer credentials; an NTP/virtualization fault cannot poison the store into a durable startup lockout). **Readiness auto-recovers** once wall and monotonic clocks re-agree within tolerance for a documented settle period — no restart required.

**`clock repair` (offline):** for a mark known to be wrong. Requires the exact old mark, a replacement time, and an audited reason; increments the credential epoch before the store returns to ready — prior TTL conclusions can no longer be trusted, so every outstanding bearer credential dies (R41 primitive). Because the epoch bump kills the operator's own control credential too — and no bootstrap route exists on any listener — clock repair reuses R41's FULL primitive (owned by U8.3, bead olss-charter-qul.10.3): it mints and discloses a replacement emergency control credential with R17's sink-validated disclosure-before-commit ordering. An epoch-bumping command must never lock the operator out. (The epoch-bump + replacement-credential portion completes when U8.3's R41 primitive lands — same land-as-ignored pattern as U1.6's note; until then `clock repair` must NOT bump the epoch, because a bump without replacement-credential minting is exactly the lockout this design forbids.) Per U1.3's lifecycle rule, repair executes as a single atomic store transaction — the store stays `ready`; crash mid-repair leaves the prior mark intact.

**Explicitly rejected:** monotonic-only TTLs (do not survive restart usefully); auto-fixing operator clocks. Operators without NTP accept that TTL enforcement tracks their clock.

**Acceptance:** boot-behind-mark refusal + one-shot audited override; override boot clamps effective time to the mark (token issued pre-rollback still expires on schedule — no TTL extension) and the mark is unchanged after the override boot; runtime future-jump → readiness false, mark NOT persisted; runtime backward step → readiness false, effective time never regresses, no TTL extension, mark unchanged; readiness auto-recovers after clocks re-agree for the settle period; restart after injected poisoned future mark → refused; clock repair resets mark, bumps epoch, old credentials invalid, replacement emergency control credential disclosed exactly once and authenticates.

**Note:** persistence of the mark lands with U2.2's meta table — this bead depends on it.
**Plan-review security correction (supersedes “only during writes anyway” and any absolute cross-crash no-extension claim):** accepted effective time is persisted on every durable coordinator transaction, clean shutdown, AND at a fixed documented idle checkpoint interval chosen from U2.1's durability/capacity measurements (not operator-tunable in v0.1). The existing clock-agreement monitor submits a coalesced safety command—at most one pending—through bounded reserved/maintenance admission. Each idle checkpoint updates the MAC'd watermark and commits one bounded audit event; if it cannot commit before the interval/deadline, readiness fails closed rather than letting the durable bound silently grow. Anomalous forward/backward wall observations are never checkpointed.

The honest maximum restart rollback/TTL-extension window is `idle_checkpoint_interval + clock_tolerance + worst_case_checkpoint_commit_latency`; publish the actual values and include their event/storage cost in U6.5. Effective time still never regresses in-process, and startup behind the durable mark still refuses/clamps as specified. Zero extension across arbitrary crash/reboot would require an external trusted time source and is NOT claimed.

**Additional automated acceptance:** deterministic fake wall/monotonic clocks cover idle advance past a token's expiry → crash immediately before/after checkpoint → wall rollback → restart; extension never exceeds the frozen bound and is zero after a completed checkpoint. Cover checkpoint coalescing, writer saturation/missed-deadline readiness failure, clean-shutdown checkpoint, small drift boundary N−1/N/N+1, and no watermark advance on anomalous time. U6.3 owns the canonical clock-watermark event and U11.8's ignore ledger prevents the integration tail remaining ignored after that owner closes.
notes: Conflict and resolution (2026-07-17): U1.5 acceptance names a production clock repair that bumps the credential epoch and discloses a replacement emergency credential, while the same contract explicitly forbids any real epoch bump until U8.3 owns and lands the full R41 replacement-credential primitive. U2.5 MACs and U6.2/U6.3 coordinator/audit machinery are also downstream dependencies, so pretending those production adapters exist would violate fail-closed and atomicity invariants. Safest resolution: implement the complete deterministic clock policy and generic disclosure-before-commit repair state machine now; keep the production clock-repair CLI fail-closed with stable integration_pending; register exact integration tails to U2.5, U6.2, U6.3, and U8.3. No mark-only repair exists. Implementation: fixed 2s tolerance, 10s recovery settle, 30s coalesced idle checkpoint, 100ms commit deadline, 24h poisoned-future cutoff, non-regressing effective time, audited one-shot override, CAS redb watermark writes, exact-old repair validation, replacement credential disclosure before atomic epoch transaction, startup classification, operator docs, and real-store crash-bound tests. Verification: clock unit 8/8, integration 2/2, startup 9/9, strict all-feature clippy passed; complete local manifest run b21adab328fd6096a05a91f49307008c passed compat pins/capture, lock/MSRV, fmt, clippy, all-target default tests, docs, Rust 1.85 build/tests, harness clippy/default/MSRV tests, and canary gate. Published honest restart TTL-extension bound is 32.1s.

title: U1.6: startup/init/shutdown test suite (tests/startup.rs, AE4 + refusal matrix)
description:
**Build:** tests/startup.rs — the startup/init/shutdown suite, covering AE4 and the full R18 refusal matrix. Test-first discipline: these exist before/alongside the checks. All scenarios log through the shared test-observability harness (U11.9a) — scenario begin/end, per-step, failure-context dump.

**Scenarios (each one a named test):**
1. AE4: boot with no encryption key material → exits non-zero naming the missing setting; does NOT generate a throwaway key.
2. `serve` on an uninitialized store → non-zero; `init` on an initialized store → refused AND recorded in that store's audit chain (U1.2 audit split); two concurrent `init` runs → exactly one first management identity (R30).
3. Non-loopback listener without TLS → non-zero (R18/R20 seam).
4. Second server instance on the same data directory → non-zero on the explicit OS lock (KTD2 caveat).
5. Any non-ready lifecycle (reencrypting/restoring/migrating/compacting) or schema newer than binary → non-zero (R35).
6. Secret via argv or unknown config key → non-zero (R37/R18).
7. `init` with broken/unwritable credential sink → aborts before commit, no store, rerun succeeds; kill points between sink flush and commit → retryable uninitialized dir; kill after commit → disclosed credential authenticates (R17); the disclosure output contains the bootstrap expiry and next-step text (U1.2).
8. Key material via systemd-style credentials directory (LoadCredential layout) loads; same content via argv refused (R21/R37) — this test also backs U12's shipped example units.
9. Boot with clock behind persisted mark beyond tolerance → non-zero; audited local override starts once (R18); override boot leaves the persisted mark unchanged and clamps effective time (no TTL extension — U1.5).
10. Runtime far-future wall-clock jump with normal monotonic → readiness false; future observation NOT persisted as new mark; readiness auto-recovers after clocks re-agree for the settle period (U1.5).
10b. Runtime wall-clock ROLLBACK beyond tolerance with normal monotonic → readiness false BEFORE persistence; effective time does not move backward; a token issued pre-rollback still expires on its original schedule (no TTL extension); persisted mark unchanged; readiness auto-recovers after re-agreement for the settle period (U1.5).
11. Restart after injected poisoned future mark → refused; `clock repair` resets, bumps credential epoch, old credentials invalid (R18/R41); the replacement emergency control credential is disclosed via R17 sink ordering and authenticates — clock repair never locks the operator out.
12. Management routes absent from remote router, served on control socket (feeds AE12).
13. Valid config → listener binds, health responds.
14. World-writable or foreign-owned data directory, and a symlinked store path → non-zero naming the offending path (R18 rows with no prior scenario).
15. Keyring embedded store id ≠ clear store id → non-zero (KTD3).
16. Lock-acquisition failure path → non-zero (unit-level fault injection on the fd-lock call — a no-lock filesystem is impractical to mount in CI).
17. `init` while a running `serve` holds the exclusive lock → refused (non-zero + stderr only; no chain write — U1.2 audit split).
18. SIGTERM with in-flight requests → admission stops, drain completes, final UNSIGNED shutdown event committed and the chain verifies to it, zeroize hook runs (R36).
19. Secret via environment variable without the explicit unsafe development flag → non-zero naming the setting; with the flag → accepted and a warning logged (R37/U1.1).
20. Key material delivered via stdin and via inherited FD → loads and serve proceeds (U1.1's remaining delivery channels, exercised end-to-end).
21. TTY-prompt delivery channel exercised by a deterministic CI PTY harness covering hidden/no-echo success, EOF, cancel, broken TTY, non-TTY refusal, terminal restoration, and canary-clean output.
22. `init` into a directory containing a foreign file → non-zero reports only the entry kind, an opaque entry id, and a run-keyed digest—never the raw basename/path or terminal-control bytes; hostile UTF-8/control-sequence names are tested. Rerun over recognized incomplete-init artifacts (lockfile + uncommitted store file) succeeds (U1.2 non-empty definition).

**Un-ignore ownership (nothing stays ignored forever):** U2.3 (olss-charter-qul.3.3) completes scenarios 7–8 and 15; U4.1 (olss-charter-qul.6.1) completes the credential-authenticates tails; U8.3 (olss-charter-qul.10.3) completes scenario 11 (it owns the R41 primitive). Each of those beads is done ONLY when its scenarios run un-ignored. U6.3 (olss-charter-qul.4.3) completes scenario 2's audit-chain-write tail and scenario 18's chain-verification tail.

**Verification:** `cargo test --test startup` green.
**Plan-review test-contract refinements (binding):** scenario 21 has NO manual escape hatch. CI must drive a deterministic PTY and prove hidden/no-echo input, success, EOF, cancel, broken TTY, non-TTY refusal, terminal restoration, and canary-clean stdout/stderr. Add named cases for config/file/env precedence and mutually exclusive secret sources; control-socket parent/mode/symlink/stale/active-listener/peer-UID safety; shutdown deadline/forced exit; and U1.5's idle-advance → crash → rollback → restart bound.

This bead is the startup test CONTRACT plus the U1-owned cases, not proof that every deferred integration tail already works. Every `#[ignore]` must carry a machine-readable scenario id, owner bead id, and reason. The command reports the exact active ignored ids; “green” may not imply full coverage. U11.8 fails an unowned ignore and fails once an owner closes while its case remains ignored; M3 permits zero ignored R/AE security cases. Logs include scenario/step, stable refusal code, safe setting/path identifier, seed, duration, child exit/signal, and redacted reproducer—never secret source contents or disclosed credentials.

**Final added cases/ownership:** reject a raw bootstrap-credential FD backed by a persistent regular or block device before generation/commit; prove TTY and pipe/socket disclosure, separated non-secret metadata, and no credential in argv/environment/data-dir/run artifacts. Exercise reserve allocation short-write/ENOSPC, temp rename/fsync, valid-orphan retry, foreign/malformed reserve refusal, and committed-store/missing-reserve startup refusal; U6.5 owns those deferred integration tails. For scenario 18, U9.4 owns queued cancellation, begun-transaction drain, executor barrier, deadline/second-signal and clean-marker transport tails; U6.3 continues to own the final audit-event/chain assertions.

**Config/restart additions:** startup tests freeze `mounts.secret.cas_required` and `mounts.secret.max_versions` defaults, precedence, 0/unset semantics, bounds/type/unknown-key refusal, and false→true/10→N restart behavior. They assert no listener bind on invalid values and no secret-bearing config/environment dump; U5.6 owns the API effect.

**Reserve command/startup cases:** add named scenarios for online stop-admitting → status → release → external capacity recovery → recreate → readiness restoration; shutdown after release followed by offline recreate; healthy-state release refusal; wrong generation/reason/confirmation/capability; final-barrier revocation; lost reply/idempotent retry; and every released/recreate-requested record/file startup combination. Each case proves the data listener remains unbound or non-ready until the matching allocated reserve is authenticated. Logs contain state/generation/byte counts/capacity band/point only and pass the same-job canary gate.
notes: Conflict and resolution (2026-07-17): U1.6 requires stdin/FD/systemd-credential/TTY delivery to proceed through real serve, but U2.3 owns the first real keyring-open consumer and was explicitly deferred by the user; U1.6 deferred-owner prose names U2.3 for scenarios 7-8/15 but accidentally omits scenario 20/21 consumption. Implementing a fake serve consumer would weaken the boot contract. Safest resolution: fully test actual typed runtime credential-directory and inherited socket-FD reads, a deterministic real PTY matrix (hidden input, empty cancel, EOF, broken hangup, non-TTY refusal, restoration, canary-clean captures), and register startup.secret_source_consumption to U2.3 for the real decrypt/readiness tail. Other audit/auth/shutdown/reserve/clock tails remain mapped to their authoritative beads. Implementation adds a 28-entry machine-readable scenario manifest for all 22 cases, 10b, and binding refinements; exact evidence functions; concrete tail contracts; zero ignored scenarios; harness begin/step/end events for every ID; and a direct FD duplication fix so socket descriptors work. Verification: focused startup contract 2/2, clock 2/2, control 5/5, init 10/10, startup CLI 9/9, startup skeleton 10/10, PTY 3/3; complete local manifest run befa0c21bc6d58bf24bdf63619eb16da passed compatibility, lock/MSRV, fmt, clippy, default all-target tests, docs, Rust 1.85 build/tests, harness default/MSRV checks, and canary gate.

title: U2.1: KTD2 redb proof spike — store-facts + thresholds (Gate G1)
notes:
Preregistration review note (2026-07-16, before any spike execution): Two externally sourced requirements cannot be observed on this host. Hetzner local metadata exposes provider=Hetzner, region=us-east, zone=ash-dc1, but not commercial instance type; safe resolution is the immutable observable surrogate Hetzner vServer/KVM/2 AMD EPYC-Milan vCPU/7.6 GiB/non-rotational QEMU disk/ext4, with any fingerprint change forcing rerun. Certified aarch64 performance requires a native external host; emulation is rejected as evidence. Protocol preregisters native aarch64 as required/pending rather than inventing a number. redb 2.6.3 is selected because it is the newest crates.io version compatible with project MSRV 1.85; current redb 4.1.0 is not selected. No benchmark or spike measurement had run when research/ktd2/protocol.json was written. Thresholds and workload are now frozen.

Execution evidence (2026-07-16): Implemented the locked Rust spike under research/ktd2/spike and reran the frozen protocol after correcting the snapshot allowance formula. Reference-host Gate G1 verdict is redb_pass: atomic transactions 1000/1000; durable p99 at concurrency 32 and 100 req/s = 4.633 ms (limit 25 ms); crash recovery 150/150 across six points; concurrent snapshot consistent with writer maximum 5.244 ms versus 8.812 ms allowance; post-compaction file/live ratio 2.616 (limit 3.0); x86_64 XChaCha encrypt 715.251 MiB/s, decrypt 710.311 MiB/s, p99 13/13 us. Exact raw values remain authoritative in research/ktd2/results.json. redb source inspection confirms Unix flock and Windows LockFile, but WASI/fallback silently omit locks; resolution is certified Linux filesystems with working flock only plus downstream startup probe fail-closed. Full local verification manifest passed all 11 checks, including MSRV 1.85 and canary gate; automatic CI remains disabled per user instruction. External blocker remains: required native aarch64 XChaCha performance cannot be measured on this x86_64-only host; no native target, emulator, configured remote host, or provisioned machine exists, and emulation would not be performance evidence. Closing is withheld until an authorized native aarch64 host measurement is appended.

External-host handoff hardening (2026-07-16): Added measure-aarch64-xchacha, a native-only command that refuses non-aarch64 runtimes before producing output and records the frozen protocol digest, native host fingerprint, aggregate XChaCha rates, percentiles, and raw samples in a separate JSON artifact. README records the exact command and forbids overwriting reference-host results.json. Local unit tests (3/3), explicit x86 refusal/no-artifact check, and full local verification run 2791ccd604e4544f09bc857858094b76 passed. This exhausts safe local work; the native aarch64 execution itself remains externally blocked.

External-artifact verification hardening (2026-07-16): The first native handoff checked only Rust compile target, which could be satisfied by user-mode emulation. Chosen correction: require both compiled aarch64 and kernel uname -m=aarch64, reject x86 CPU fingerprints, capture portable ARM CPU identity, and add verify-aarch64-xchacha. The verifier checks exact fields, frozen protocol digest, native host/kernel identity, the registered 4 KiB x 20,000 workload, all sorted raw samples, and recomputes throughput and p99 summaries. Tampering, wrong-host, digest, summary, and extra-field cases are tested. Focused default/MSRV tests and clippy passed; full local verification run 530faea41219b2d7b5c35c9a45336fcc passed all scenarios. Native execution remains required before closure.

User waiver and closure resolution (2026-07-16): After the native-aarch64 blocker was explained, the user explicitly directed “let's skip this one.” Resolution: waive only the native aarch64 XChaCha performance observation; do not waive or alter the measured storage correctness, recovery, snapshot, compaction, durable latency, or x86_64 cipher results. No ARM performance claim is made. The residual risk is unknown native ARM cipher capacity until the retained native measurement command is run. Documentation records this boundary. Focused spike tests passed 4/4 and full local verification run 9515dbdd5ef0ee03bc365768ae47470e passed every scenario.

title: U2.2: store schema — meta/system_keyring/secret_meta/secrets tables, lifecycle stamps (R35/KTD11)
description:
**Build:** The store schema module — one redb file, multiple tables, because KTD8's atomicity demands ONE durability domain.

**Tables (initial set; later units add identities/grants/credentials/rotation/audit through this module):**
- `meta`: store id, format_version, lifecycle state (`ready | reencrypting | restoring | migrating | compacting` — R35), and the wall-clock high-water mark (clock model; updated on every durable coordinator transaction and by U1.5’s bounded coalesced idle checkpoint loop).
- `system_keyring`: the age-encrypted keyring envelope as ONE opaque value — deliberately NOT a sidecar file, so keyring changes commit atomically with their audit events in the same transaction (KTD3/KTD8).
- `secret_meta`: first-class, schema-versioned per-secret metadata record (KTD11): KV custom_metadata, max_versions, cas_required, server fields (last_completed_rotation_at, rotation state, R12 rotation interval — control-plane-writable only, metadata schema version). Separate from version ciphertext so listing/age/status queries NEVER decrypt value blobs. secret_meta also carries a schema-versioned generic `rotation_protection` field — the `open_snapshot` invariant OWNED HERE and G2-frozen with the clear-record format: a non-empty value marks prior versions protected by an open coordination window; values are opaque version-tagged tokens, never U7 enum strings; U7 alone maps its lifecycle transitions onto it, and U5.8 purge fails closed on any known-active or unknown protection version/value. **Also carries a MAC'd version-set summary (KTD15): current version number, max version number, and per-version soft-delete/destroy markers, generation-bumped on EVERY version append/delete/undelete/destroy.** This is load-bearing twice over: it gives U5.2's deletion-state markers (R29) a MAC'd home, and it makes offline deletion of the newest version row digest-visible — without it, an offline editor who deletes the latest `secrets` row silently serves the prior version as "latest" past every integrity check (the rotation-staleness attack the charter's threat model claims to stop).
- `secrets`: keyed by logical path; versioned records (version number, created_time, key id, nonce, ciphertext). **Integrity note:** per-version `created_time` is authenticated via the record AEAD's AAD (U2.4) — it feeds R12 rotation-age math and must not be offline-editable.

**Rules:** `serve` requires lifecycle=ready and refuses schema versions newer than the binary (enforced in U1.3; the stamp lives here). Unknown format_version in meta → open refused. Migrations are forward-only, take a verified backup first, record the transition in the audit log; CI retains a restore-and-migrate fixture for every released schema (R35 — enforcement lands with U10/U12; the version stamp is what makes deferred binary-rollback safe).

**Acceptance:** open/create round-trips; unknown format_version refused; lifecycle stamp readable pre-keyring (it gates startup before decryption — see U2.5's provisional-read rule); high-water-mark field persists; version-set summary MAC verifies and its generation bumps on each append/delete/undelete/destroy.
**Plan-review codec contract:** every table key/value has an explicit versioned canonical codec—never Rust ABI/layout, serde defaults, debug text, or derive order. Freeze integer widths/endianness/time units/length maxima and strict no-trailing-bytes decode. Unit/golden tests cover deterministic round-trip, zero/max/overflow lengths, truncation at every field, unknown version, duplicate/noncanonical keys, and lifecycle/format migration dispatch with safe field/check logs.

**Operational state codecs (binding):** an initialized store's lifecycle enum is exactly `ready | reencrypting | restoring | migrating | compacting`; an uninitialized directory has no committed `meta` record rather than a magic lifecycle value. Freeze one optional KTD15-authenticated `meta.pending_anchor.v1` value with schema version, kind (`record-key | metadata-key | migration | compaction | normal-restore | rollback-fork`), opaque operation/marker id, plan-or-activation digest, installed schema/keyring/payload generation or incarnation as applicable, post-operation state digest, and status. Only the matching externally registered state checkpoint may clear it through U6.2; unknown kind/version, duplicate marker, generation/CAS mismatch, transplant, or trailing bytes fail closed. Freeze `maintenance.marker.v1` as the fixed-name no-follow sibling marker used by U10.7/U10.8: store id, migration-or-compaction kind, opaque job id, final-plan digest, source/target format and head/state commitments, phase, temp-file identity, owner, and KTD15 MAC—never arbitrary raw paths. U1 owns startup gating, U8/U10 own workflows, and U6.5 owns diagnostics/clear semantics. Golden/unit/fault tests edit, truncate, transplant, duplicate, replay, swap marker↔DB ids, and inject every marker/lifecycle/pending-anchor commit/fsync boundary with typed canary-clean logs.

**Rewrite-job status codec:** freeze KTD15-authenticated `key_rewrite_job.v1` with kind, operation id, installed generation/state digest, checkpoint digest, postflight backup artifact/signature/receipt digests and generations, and closed enum `installed_pending_anchor | anchored_rewrite_complete_recovery_pending | complete_recovery_current`. Only owner transitions may advance it; backward/skip/mismatched evidence fails. It is distinct from `meta.pending_anchor`, which clears at the first transition.
notes:
Conflict/resolution (2026-07-16): The bead binds canonical maintenance-marker, pending-anchor, and rewrite-job codecs while also demanding commit/fsync-boundary workflow fault injection, but explicitly assigns those workflows to U8/U10 and their coordination/diagnostic behavior to U6.2/U6.5. It also names rotation state before U7 owns transitions. Chosen resolution: implement and authenticate the complete storage codecs, minimal closed storage states, owner-checked forward-only rewrite transitions, fixed marker identity, and structural/edit/transplant/replay defenses now; do not invent filesystem rewrite workflows or audit/checkpoint coordination ahead of their dependency owners. Rotation protection remains an opaque bounded version-tagged value. Later owner beads must use these codecs and add their named boundary faults without changing v1 bytes except through forward migration.

Implementation evidence: Added redb 2.6.3 to the main server and one-file meta/system_keyring/secret_meta/secrets tables. Added explicit v1 big-endian canonical codecs with bounded lengths and strict unknown/truncation/trailing/noncanonical rejection for store IDs, lifecycle, high-water time, keyring envelope, logical/secret keys, secret records, secret metadata, version-set summary, pending anchors, maintenance markers, and rewrite jobs. Clear-record MAC framing binds domain, store id, primary key, schema/value bytes, and generation; database operations derive store id from committed meta. Version summaries bump generation for append/delete/undelete/destroy. Store ID is immutable through meta CAS; secret value version must match its key.

Verification evidence: cargo test --locked --test store passed 11/11 focused tests covering stable golden bytes, deterministic round trips, every-prefix truncation, unknown/trailing versions, duplicate/noncanonical maps and paths, zero/max/overflow lengths, lifecycle/high-water persistence before keyring, unknown format refusal, real redb reopen, MAC edit/store/path transplant, fixed marker identity, owner/backward/skip rewrite refusal, and version/key mismatch. ./scripts/verify.sh all run aabb5e897497dc4725ad1d9540cdda4b passed every local scenario including fmt, clippy -D warnings, all-target tests, docs, Rust 1.85 build/tests, harness suites, compatibility gates, and canary scan.

title: U2.3: age-wrapped fixed-purpose keyring — init generation, boot decrypt-once, store-id binding (KTD3)
description:
**Build:** Keyring lifecycle (KTD3): generation at init, the age envelope, boot-time decrypt-once, store-id binding.

**Contents (compact fixed-purpose keyring — independent random 256-bit symmetric keys, each with a key id):**
- record-encryption key (decrypt-only predecessors retained after rotation),
- credential-verifier MAC key,
- metadata-integrity MAC key (R39/KTD15),
- audit-payload key,
- audit-index key — minted at init like the others (32 random bytes; keeps the keyring format stable) but DORMANT in v0.1: KTD16's index is deferred, the key exists unused so the index later needs no keyring migration (wayfinder T10).
Keyring plaintext also carries: store id, keyring format version, keyring generation, recipient metadata.

**Canonical recipient vocabulary and envelope:** the **active recipient** is the one current daemon-keyring recipient derived from the active age identity; the **recovery recipient** is an optional, distinct off-host keyring recipient. The keyring envelope is age v0.12-encrypted to exactly one active recipient and at most one recovery recipient; canonical fingerprint ordering, duplicate/equality rejection, and hard cardinality/encoded-size bounds apply. Stored as one opaque value in `system_keyring` (not a sidecar). The age crate is pre-1.0: pin the exact version; decrypt fixtures must survive upgrades.

**Clear keyring-metadata record (created in the same init transaction as the envelope):** keyring generation, keyring format version, recipient fingerprints (non-secret), and last-rewrap audit sequence — KTD15-MAC'd like other clear metadata, and cross-checked against the decrypted envelope's own fields at boot; mismatch → readiness false. Without this record there is nothing MAC'd/digest-visible for "keyring metadata" (named in U2.5's record class list and G2 item 1) to mean: the envelope is opaque, so rewrap rollback detection (U8.1), doctor's keyring-generation report (U12.1), and the state digest all key off THIS record.

**Boot:** open DB → read envelope → decrypt EXACTLY ONCE into `secrecy` types → compare embedded store id with clear store id BEFORE any other record is accepted — a transplanted envelope is rejected before readiness. **Provisional-read closure (mirrors U2.5):** meta fields read before the keyring opens (lifecycle, format_version, store id) are provisional gate inputs; immediately after keyring open, their KTD15 MACs are verified BEFORE readiness — failure → readiness false. The server age identity itself arrives only via approved channels (R21/R37): never an application-managed or plaintext persistent-disk source, shell history, or argv; runtime/tmpfs systemd credentials and encrypted-at-rest provisioning follow the custody rule below.

**Why a keyring instead of per-blob age (planning-revised, KTD3):** recipient rotation becomes an atomic envelope rewrap instead of a full-store re-encrypt; an offline recovery recipient costs nothing per record; per-record overhead drops; purpose separation keeps token verification, metadata integrity, and audit confidentiality off the secret-encryption key. Recipients stay OUT of the authorization path; age remains the only asymmetric mechanism and the seal boundary — no seal state machine, no KEK/DEK policy hierarchy.

**Age-identity bootstrap command:** `key age-identity generate --purpose active|recovery|audit-export` is stateless and may run before a store exists. Each invocation generates exactly one separately labeled age identity, writes the private identity only through R17's approved precommit TTY/pipe/socket/anonymous-memory sink, and emits the corresponding public recipient/fingerprint plus custody metadata on normal human/JSON output. It accepts no private-output argv/environment/persistent-plaintext sink. A short write may disclose an unusable partial orphan and a lost final reply may leave a complete operator-held orphan, but neither reports success or creates store state; retry generates a fresh value and custody tooling discards/replaces the orphan. Flush/ACK/cancel/RNG failures have stable outcomes. Invoke the command separately for distinct active and recovery purposes; audit-export configuration uses another separately custodied `audit-export` invocation rather than silently reusing DR identity. Init/recipient-set commands reject equal or duplicate fingerprints where their owner policy requires separation. Metadata JSON includes the closed purpose value, algorithm, public recipient/fingerprint, sink outcome id, and no private bytes; unknown purpose values refuse before RNG or sink creation.

**Wire into init (U1.2):** keyring creation joins init's single transaction. Init consumes the active identity through a typed source, derives its recipient, and optionally accepts only the recovery PUBLIC recipient.

**Un-ignore ownership:** this bead completes tests/startup.rs scenarios 7–8 and 15 (they run un-ignored before this bead closes).

**Acceptance:** wrong/absent identity → fails closed, no partial plaintext; transplanted envelope rejected on store-id mismatch; keyring opens once and only once per process (assert via instrumentation/test hook); keyring-metadata record MAC verifies and matches envelope fields; post-open provisional-meta re-verification failure → readiness false.
**Plan-review ownership correction:** this bead owns envelope decrypt, embedded/clear field agreement, boot-once secrecy and store-id binding. U2.5 owns MAC computation plus provisional-meta re-verification; those named startup/store tails remain ignored with owner `.3.5` until it lands. U2.3 no longer waits for the all-of-U1 startup suite; it depends directly on U11.9a and lands/unignores its own keyring scenarios. Add recipient-set canonicalization, age envelope unknown-version/malformed cases, key-id uniqueness, boot-once instrumentation and canary-clean failure logs.

**Identity-source custody precision:** the daemon consumes a boot identity through the typed source and zeroizes that input after opening the keyring. It never writes the identity to its data directory or logs. A systemd credentials directory is accepted only as the runtime/tmpfs delivery endpoint; shipped persistent provisioning uses encrypted-at-rest credentials. Tests distinguish runtime credential loading from a forbidden application-managed plaintext identity file.

**Bounded retained generations:** the v0.1 keyring codec freezes `MAX_AUDIT_PAYLOAD_GENERATIONS=32` with warning threshold 24; credential, record, and metadata families retain only the generations their owner semantics require. Envelope decode/encode and capacity preflight refuse over-count/oversize before allocation or RNG. Since every v0.1 audit event is retained, audit generations cannot be retired; doctor/docs give the post-v0.1 archive/prune+migration remediation. Unit vectors cover 23/24/31/32/33, duplicate ids, encoded-size overflow, concurrent final-slot rotation, and canary-safe diagnostics.
notes:
Deferred at user request on 2026-07-16 after a buildable partial checkpoint. Partial implementation adds the pinned age v0.12 keyring core, bounded canonical codec, store integration, decrypt-once/store-id/metadata checks, and focused unit tests (5 passing). Bead remains open and in immutable scope S; no acceptance criterion is waived and work must resume before goal completion.

Conflict and resolution (2026-07-17): U2.3 says to wire keyring creation into init, while the complete init backend still lacks first identity/grant (U3.2), bootstrap verifier (U4.1), audit genesis (U6.3), and recovery reserve (U6.5); activating the CLI with placeholder records would violate the atomic-init charter. Safest resolution: implement the real typed-source KeyringInitTransaction as a composable segment of U1.2, self-test the age envelope before returning it, and commit meta+MAC-sealed keyring metadata+opaque envelope atomically in redb, while leaving the outer CLI fail-closed until its authoritative owners land. The bootstrap command also names ACK/cancel behavior without defining a wire protocol; resolution: the flushed private sink plus subsequent public metadata is the local success ACK, all write/flush/cancel/final-reply failures return stable non-success and create no store state, and retry generates a fresh orphan-replacement identity. Implementation adds fixed-purpose bounded key generation, canonical recipient ordering, age v0.12 wrap/decrypt-once, store-id binding, typed boot source consumption and zeroization, stateless key age-identity generate human/JSON output, safe FD duplication, exact fixture, capacity/concurrency vectors, custody docs, and completed startup tail evidence. Verification: focused format+clippy+39 integration tests+7 keyring unit vectors passed; full local CI-equivalent run 2bbd467c92df56ce52065576b5ca010d passed all 13 manifest checks including default and Rust 1.85 all-target tests, docs, harness checks, and canary gate on Linux x86_64. ARM64 was unavailable by user statement and remains externally unverified; no ARM64 result is claimed. U2.5 retains ownership of provisional clear-meta MAC re-verification after keyring open.

title: U2.4: record AEAD — XChaCha20-Poly1305, canonical binary header/AAD, fresh-nonce discipline (KTD3/R4)
description:
**Build:** Record-level AEAD (KTD3): XChaCha20-Poly1305 (RustCrypto `chacha20poly1305` — NCC-audited; `aes-gcm-siv` noted unaudited in review) with the canonical binary header/AAD encoding.

**Canonical cryptographic encoding — this is half of Gate G2, get it exactly right:** record headers and AEAD associated data use a FIXED binary format: magic bytes, format version, cipher_suite_id, store id, record-domain id, key id, length-prefixed mount and canonical path segments, logical record id, secret version where applicable, and the record's `created_time`.
