NO JSON, debug formatting, string concatenation, platform endianness, or map iteration order may enter an authenticated encoding (any of those makes authentication depend on accidents).

**Why created_time is authenticated:** it is immutable per version (safe in AAD) and it feeds U7.6's rotation-age computation ("age since creation when never rotated") — left unauthenticated, an offline editor rewrites it to mask an overdue rotation, defeating R12 for exactly the never-rotated secrets the project exists to expose. Tamper vector lands in U2.7 and tests/store.rs.

**Binding:** AAD binds each ciphertext to store id, record type, mount, canonical path, secret version, created_time, and key id — valid ciphertext copied to another path, version, or store FAILS authentication rather than decrypting (R4; threat model: ciphertext substitution).

**Cipher identity:** fixed `cipher_suite_id` in the record header; NOT operator-configurable. Adding another suite is a storage-format migration (R35), never a config toggle.

**Nonce lifecycle:** every AEAD encryption draws a FRESH 192-bit CSPRNG nonce (the extended XChaCha nonce) stored in the record header. Every rewrite and re-encryption draws a new nonce EVEN WHEN PLAINTEXT IS UNCHANGED. Deterministic or counter-derived nonce construction is PROHIBITED everywhere. At 192 bits, random collision is negligible at any plausible scale, so correctness/readiness never depend on a collision-budget counter — the keyring may keep an informational per-key operation count for doctor (KTD3).

**Acceptance:** encrypt/decrypt round-trip; AAD-mismatch (path/version/store/domain/key-id/created_time swap) → authentication failure; deterministic-test-RNG harness proves every encryption path requests a fresh nonce (writes, rewrites, identical-plaintext re-encryption); header decodes identically across supported architectures.
**Plan-review G2 binary precision:** freeze exact header/AAD field order, domain label/framing, integer widths/endianness, timestamp units, algorithm/key-id/nonce/ciphertext length bounds, and strict no-trailing-bytes decode. Unknown suite/version fails closed with a typed error. Malformed/truncated/noncanonical cases are golden negatives. Fresh-nonce proof covers every encryption call site that exists in U2; later U8 call sites are owned by U8 suites. Logs use case id/length/key id/digest only—never plaintext, nonce+key pairs, or full AAD paths.
notes: Review required: acceptance asks for identical decoding on every supported architecture, while the user explicitly cannot provide an ARM64 test environment now. Resolution: freeze the format with a complete byte-for-byte golden vector, explicit big-endian integer encoding, strict every-prefix truncation/unknown-version/unknown-suite/unknown-domain/magic/trailing negatives, and run all available checks on x86_64. U2.7 remains the named owner of automated x86_64+aarch64 cross-version fixture execution; no ARM64 result is claimed here. Implementation: exact chacha20poly1305 0.10.1 XChaCha20-Poly1305 suite, closed record domains, canonical authenticated header, keyring-only key selection, fresh 24-byte nonce per encryption/rewrite, typed fail-closed errors, zeroizing decrypted output, safe redacted Debug, and canonical state-digest bytes. Verification: 6/6 focused record_aead tests passed; store/keyring/integrity regressions passed; clippy -D warnings passed; ./scripts/verify.sh all passed all 13 x86_64 scenarios under run 10a2745c9f235b60ac2d75df6c43476e, including Rust 1.85 MSRV and canary.

title: U2.5: KTD15 clear-record MACs + readiness-fatal verification (R39)
description:
**Build:** KTD15 authenticated clear-state records (R39).

**What:** every security-relevant clear record — identities, grants, credential metadata, secret metadata (including the version-set summary, U2.2), rotation state, consumer declarations, schema metadata, the wall-clock high-water-mark record (U1.5), keyring metadata (the clear record defined in U2.3) — is encoded canonically (fixed binary encoding, same discipline as U2.4) and protected by a keyed MAC under the keyring's metadata-integrity key.

**MAC domain:** store id, table id, primary key, schema version, record generation (monotonically increasing per record), canonical value bytes. So an offline editor who can WRITE the database but cannot READ the keyring can neither alter a grant nor transplant a valid record to another key or store.

**Fail-closed rule:** reads verify the MAC BEFORE using the record; a MAC failure takes readiness FALSE and prevents data-plane admission — records are NEVER accepted on a warning-only path (R39; Definition of Done names this posture explicitly). **Sole, explicit exception (pre-keyring bootstrap):** meta fields that gate startup before the keyring opens (lifecycle, format_version, store id, and the wall-clock high-water mark read for the boot clock check — U2.2/U1.3/U1.5) are read as PROVISIONAL inputs; immediately after keyring open their MACs are verified before readiness — failure → readiness false (mirrored in U2.3's boot section). No other record class has a provisional window.

**Runtime MAC-failure operator experience:** the readiness flip emits a stable reason code plus the table and a bounded keyed digest/masked identifier for the primary key; raw paths, identity labels, control characters, and attacker-chosen key bytes are never logged; the triggering request receives the stable integrity-failure error envelope; the control plane and doctor remain admissible via the reserved lane so the operator can diagnose; the documented recovery is investigate-or-restore (U10.3). tests/store.rs asserts the safe table+masked-key diagnostic and log-injection resistance.

**Deletion/rollback detection:** per-record MACs alone cannot detect deletion or replacement with an older, formerly valid record. Checkpoint creation (U6.4) therefore computes a deterministic state digest from one consistent read snapshot and signs it with the audit head — removal or rollback becomes detectable through the anchored sequence; the unanchored tail retains R14's documented live-compromise limitation. **Digest coverage:** sorted (table, key, generation, mac) tuples for MAC'd clear records, PLUS (table, primary key including version, blake3(header ‖ nonce ‖ ciphertext)) tuples for encrypted-record tables from the same snapshot — without the second class, offline deletion of a secret's newest version row is invisible to the digest (the "serve stale credential as latest" rollback; belt-and-suspenders with U2.2's MAC'd version-set summary).

**Digest-vs-key-rotation rule:** the digest is metadata-key-dependent by design (tuples include the MAC). A metadata-integrity-key rotation (U8.5) re-MACs every clear record, so the pass completes ONLY with an immediate `audit checkpoint prepare → sign → register` anchoring the new digest — otherwise doctor/`audit verify --state` sit red against the last checkpoint after a recommended operation (mirrored in U8.5).

**Verification cadence:** per-record MAC on every use; full-state verification in doctor --full, backup verification, restore, migration, and checkpoint preparation.

**Why clear-but-authenticated instead of encrypted (documented offline-theft boundary, R4/KTD11):** logical paths, identity names, grants, version numbers/timestamps stay readable to whoever holds the DB file — chosen over opaque-identifier indirection for v0.1; values, credential material, and audit payloads (which reveal access patterns, not mere topology) stay encrypted. If path/identity topology is itself regulated data, this boundary is disqualifying until a post-v0.1 opaque-identifier option exists.

**Acceptance:** direct DB edit of an identity/grant/credential/secret-metadata record → MAC fails, readiness false, log line names the table plus a bounded keyed digest/masked key identifier; valid record+MAC copied to another primary key or store → verification fails; generation regression detected by state digest; offline deletion of a secret's newest version row detected by state digest (full tests with U6.4).
**Plan-review MAC registry:** freeze primitive/tag/domain/framing and comparison: 32-byte keyed BLAKE3 under unique versioned per-record-class domain labels, constant-time tag comparison, canonical generation/version binding. Export a conformance helper every future clear-record owner must run for edit/transplant/generation/unknown-version/trailing-byte cases before first durable write.

After runtime MAC failure, ordinary data and management mutations fail closed. Only bounded diagnostics/read-only recovery (`doctor`, verify/query as policy permits), orderly shutdown, and offline restore/repair paths remain; explicitly test the allow/deny matrix. Startup provisional reads are reverified immediately after keyring open. Add wrong-domain/cross-table/cross-store/rollback, tag truncation, timing-work instrumentation, and R25-safe logs.

**Post-checkpoint state-reconciliation contract:** the state digest is a digest of the full sorted tuple set and is not algebraically reversible. Therefore every successful ordinary mutation's encrypted audit payload carries a bounded, sorted state-delta commitment for every changed tuple: table/domain, canonical key, explicit before tuple or absence, and explicit after tuple or absence. No secret plaintext is included. U6.4 verifies the current after-tuples, reverse-replays the authenticated tail to reconstruct the checkpoint tuple set, then recomputes the signed digest. Bulk rewrite/restore transitions use frozen before/after whole-state digests plus an operation-specific transition record and required anchoring workflow, never millions of unbounded per-row deltas.
notes: Review required: the acceptance text requires direct identity/grant/credential tests and full checkpoint integration with U6.4, but those durable record/table owners and checkpoint coordinator are explicitly downstream open dependencies. Resolution: freeze and enforce the closed 15-class keyed-BLAKE3 registry, canonical MAC frame, constant-time comparison, reusable negative conformance helper, real currently durable provisional/keyring/secret-metadata tamper paths, deterministic clear+encrypted state tuples, bounded sorted reversible deltas, and whole-state bulk transitions now. Future record owners must implement ClearRecord and run the helper before durable writes; U6.2/U6.4 consume the frozen delta/digest primitives for atomic audit and signed checkpoint verification. This preserves fail-closed security without fabricating future tables or weakening acceptance. Verification: focused 35 tests passed; cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings passed; ./scripts/verify.sh all passed all 13 x86_64 scenarios under run 5c3ebe8b9aa41a005e8bef4f483cae44, including Rust 1.85 MSRV and canary. ARM64 remains externally unverified per user constraint.

title: U2.6: versioned secret records, decrypt-free metadata queries, zeroizing types (R11/KTD11/KTD7)
description:
**Build:** Versioned secret records + per-secret metadata semantics + zeroizing secret types.

**Versioning (R11 storage semantics):** a write appends a new version; both versions retained; versioned read returns each; unversioned read returns latest. Unversioned reads resolve 'latest' through the MAC-verified version-set summary (U2.2); a summary/row mismatch (row missing, or rows beyond the summary's max) is an integrity failure handled per U2.5 — never a silent fallback to the highest surviving row. Monotonic version numbers; created_time per version; key id + nonce + ciphertext per record. (CAS enforcement lives in the API/coordinator layer — U5/U6; the store provides current-version read + append primitives that make CAS checkable inside the transaction.)

**Metadata (KTD11):** secret_meta is first-class and schema-versioned; metadata queries (versions, ages, custom_metadata, listings) read it WITHOUT touching the record-decrypt path; the store is never batch-decrypted at boot; no plaintext secret cache exists across requests (Performance & Capacity).

**Zeroization (KTD7/R5):** server-owned plaintext — the age identity, the opened keyring, decrypted values before serialization — lives in `SecretBox`/`SecretString` with PRE-SIZED backing so reallocation never leaves stale copies. Zeroize on drop. The guarantee is bounded honestly: copies inside allocator/kernel/HTTP/TLS stacks and the client are excluded by R5's wording; best-effort process-dump hardening is documented separately.

**Acceptance:** write→read round-trip with on-disk bytes being ciphertext (byte-inspect: no plaintext secret substring in the redb file); write twice → both versions readable, unversioned read = latest; metadata queries complete without invoking decrypt (assert via test hook/counter); secret-owning types implement + invoke zeroize-on-drop, prohibit accidental Clone/Debug formatting (compile-time + runtime checks) — without claiming to inspect allocator or kernel copies.
notes: Implementation: keyring-mediated atomic ciphertext+MACed-version-summary appends; verified versioned/latest reads; missing/newer row fail-closed readiness; decrypt-free metadata query; per-read decrypt instrumentation; no plaintext cache; non-Clone/non-Debug zeroizing plaintext owner; honest allocator/kernel/HTTP/TLS/client boundary documentation. Verification: 4/4 focused versioned-secret tests, 29 related crypto/store/keyring/integrity regressions, 2/2 compile-fail ownership doc tests, and clippy -D warnings passed. ./scripts/verify.sh all passed all 13 x86_64 scenarios under run b94e503539f53b79ec144c4882691662, including Rust 1.85 MSRV and canary.

title: U2.7: cross-version crypto fixtures + canonical AAD test vectors (freeze artifacts for G2)
description:
**Build:** Checked-in cross-version crypto fixtures + canonical AAD/header test vectors — the artifacts that FREEZE the encoding across releases (feeds Gate G2).

**Contents:**
- Fixed record-header and AAD test vectors: encode/decode across supported architectures (x86_64, aarch64) byte-identically; altered lengths, domains, paths, versions, key ids, and created_time each REJECT (created_time is authenticated — U2.4/R12).
- Keyring envelope decrypt fixtures pinned to the exact age crate version (pre-1.0: fixtures must survive crate upgrades — an upgrade that breaks decrypt is a release blocker, caught here).
- Clear-record MAC vectors (KTD15 domain tuples), including the secret_meta version-set summary record (U2.2).
- Encrypted-record round-trip fixtures usable by U10 restore tests and R38's prior-release smoke fixture.

**Rule:** once Gate G2 closes over these, any change to an authenticated encoding is an R35 forward-only migration with upgrade notes (R38) — never a silent re-emit of fixtures.

**Acceptance:** vectors committed under tests/fixtures/; automated cross-architecture golden-vector job on the U11.0 runner and as a required U11.8 CI check (no local/manual escape hatch) proves identical decode; each tamper case rejects; fixtures referenced by G2's freeze review.
**Plan-review fixture rigor:** remove the documented-local aarch64 escape hatch. Land an automated cross-architecture vector script now on the U11.0 runner; U11.8 later makes it required CI. Add a manifest listing format/domain ids, fixture schema, expected hashes, generator/toolchain version, negative-case ids and exact redacted commands. Regeneration is explicit/reviewed and byte drift fails. Test strict unknown/truncated/trailing/endianness and old-version decode on both arches.
notes: Conflict/external verification: acceptance requires automated x86_64+aarch64 byte-identical execution with no local/manual escape, while the user explicitly disabled automatic CI and stated ARM64 cannot be tested now. Resolution: implement the native-architecture check and manual-only GitHub matrix for ubuntu-24.04 x86_64 plus ubuntu-24.04-arm aarch64; wire the check into the shared local verifier; run every available check on x86_64; fail on architecture mismatch; do not claim an ARM64 result. Implementation: deterministic checked-in record-header/AAD, actual encrypted-record, secret-metadata clear-MAC, and pinned age 0.12 fixtures; SHA-256/schema/domain/generator/negative-case manifest; explicit reviewed regeneration command; strict unknown/truncated/trailing/length/endianness rejection; all bound AAD fields, store, key id, nonce, ciphertext, created time, clear value/generation/class/schema/key/store tamper negatives; public fixture decode seam for restore consumers; operational freeze/migration docs. Verification: crypto_fixtures 3/3; ./scripts/check-crypto-vectors.sh byte-identical and hash-clean on x86_64; strict clippy; full ./scripts/verify.sh run d8fa89d72b009c1bcf16eb0aa32c7b22 passed compatibility, crypto vectors, fmt, clippy, all-target/doc, Rust 1.85 build/test, harness, and canary gates on x86_64. ARM64 execution remains externally unavailable and must be run later; workflow_dispatch is the only CI trigger per user.

title: U2.8: store test suite (tests/store.rs)
description:
**Build:** tests/store.rs — the U2 suite. All scenarios log through the shared test-observability harness (U11.9a). Scenarios (each a named test):
1. Write→read round-trip; on-disk bytes are ciphertext, not plaintext (byte-inspection of the redb file for plaintext substrings).
2. Write twice → both versions retained; versioned read returns each; unversioned returns latest (R11).
3. Keyring open with wrong/absent identity → fails closed, no partial plaintext.
4. Valid ciphertext copied to different path/version → AAD mismatch → decrypt fails (R4).
5. Deterministic test RNG: every U2-owned encryption/rewrite call requests a fresh nonce, including repeated primitive encryption of identical plaintext (KTD3). Whole-store U8 call sites and their nonce coverage belong to U8.4.
6. Fixed header/AAD vectors decode identically across supported architectures; altered lengths/domains/paths/versions/key-ids/created_time reject (KTD3).
7. Direct DB modification of U2-owned authenticated clear records (meta, keyring metadata, secret_meta/version-set summary) → MAC fails → readiness false (R39); the failure log names the table plus a bounded keyed digest/masked key identifier. The reusable conformance helper is exported; U3.2 and U4.1/U4.4 must apply it to identity, grant, and credential records before their beads close.
8. Valid clear record + MAC copied to another primary key or store → MAC verification fails (KTD15).
9. Record rolled back to an older valid generation, or a secret's newest version row deleted outright, → `audit verify --state` fails against signed checkpoint or reconstructed tail (KTD15/U2.5 encrypted-record tuples — completes with U6.4; land as ignored/failing until then).
10. Metadata queries (versions, custom_metadata, age inputs) never touch the record-decrypt path (KTD11).
11. Zeroize wrapper contract verified; accidental Clone/Debug prohibited (KTD7) — no claims about allocator/kernel copies (R5 wording).
12. Unknown format_version in meta → open refused (R35).
13. Storage-executor bounded-queue saturation → memory bounded, excess rejected in order, reserved control lane stays live (KTD8 — executor test moved OUT of the KTD2 spike by wayfinder T04; implemented against U6.1's executor, referenced from both suites; land as ignored/failing until U6.1 lands).
14. Keyring envelope from store B installed in store A → rejected on store-id mismatch BEFORE readiness; no record accepted (KTD3 — the U2-era regression test; tests/reencrypt.rs later covers the rewrap-specific variant).
15. Bit-flipped and truncated keyring envelope → fails closed with a distinct error (corruption vs wrong-identity are distinguishable to the operator).
16. Two sequential reads of the same secret → the decrypt path is invoked twice (decrypt-counter hook from scenario 5's instrumentation) — proves no cross-request plaintext cache exists (U2 rule).
17. Keyring-metadata record edited or mismatched against decrypted envelope fields → readiness false (U2.3).
18. Meta field altered after its provisional pre-keyring read → post-keyring-open MAC re-verification fails → readiness false (U2.3/U2.5).
19. Instrumentation hook proves the keyring envelope is decrypted exactly once per process (U2.3).
20. Version-set summary generation bumps on every append/delete/undelete/destroy and its MAC re-verifies (U2.2).

**Un-ignore ownership:** U6.6 (olss-charter-qul.4.6) removes the #[ignore] from scenarios 9 and 13 and makes them pass against the real U6.1 executor and U6.4 checkpoint flow — that bead is done only when these run un-ignored.

**Verification:** `cargo test --test store` green; hexdump spot-check recorded.
**Plan-review suite additions:** close/reopen persistence with same and wrong identity; restart after metadata/version/deletion mutations; current concrete record edit/transplant/rollback cases plus the reusable future-record conformance obligation; automated binary/canary scan instead of manual hexdump. Every ignored future coordinator/checkpoint case is in the owner ledger. Log scenario/table/version/count/mode/digest/seed/timing/reproducer, never record bytes, paths containing secrets, identities, or key material.

**State-tail additions:** unit fixtures cover add/change/delete deltas with explicit absence, deterministic sorted multi-tuple mutations, and rejection of mismatched before/after commitments. With U6.4, a legitimate post-checkpoint mutation remains green after reverse replay, while offline add/delete/edit/rollback fails. A read-only unanchored tail is never falsely claimed recoverable from state deltas.
notes: Review required: scenario 15 asks operators to distinguish corrupted/truncated age envelopes from wrong-identity failures. Authenticated age decryption intentionally presents both as decryption/authentication failure; distinguishing them would require an oracle or trusting unauthenticated metadata. Safest resolution: both fail closed as KeyringError::Decrypt with secret-safe diagnostics; added bit-flip/truncation regression coverage. Scenario 6 also requests all supported architectures, but ARM64 execution is unavailable per user. The canonical big-endian architecture-independent fixture and x86_64 evidence remain; ARM64 is externally unverifiable here. Implemented a source-checked 20-scenario U2 ledger with shared safe observability; scenarios 9 and 13 remain explicitly ignored for U6.6 ownership as contracted. Verification: ./scripts/verify.sh run 2288f291a9ddfd4eaa729190175f83f7 passed compat, vectors, fmt, clippy -D warnings, all-target tests, docs, Rust 1.85 build/tests, harness clippy/tests/MSRV, and canary gate on Linux x86_64; cargo test --locked --test store --test keyring passed (store 12 passed/2 contractually ignored; keyring 8 passed); br dep cycles --json returned zero cycles.

title: U6.1: bounded blocking storage executor — lanes, backpressure, cancellation (KTD8)
description:
**Build:** The dedicated blocking storage executor (KTD8) — ONE named OS thread exclusively owns the redb writer/coordinator; tokio request tasks never own raw database or transaction handles. The storage module may issue only its bounded internal MVCC read-snapshot handles defined below.

**Protocol:** routers submit typed coordinator commands over a bounded channel and await typed results. The single writer executor serializes write transactions in an explicit order, performs the synchronous database + fsync work, and responds only after commit. redb permits one writer at a time — this design makes that ceiling EXPLICIT instead of blocking async workers on writer acquisition.

**Admission lanes:** separate bounded lanes — a data-plane lane and a SMALL RESERVED control/recovery lane. Queue saturation rejects new data-plane work BEFORE decryption with a stable overload error and bounded aggregate accounting (R13); the reserved lane retains admission for control-plane operations: checkpoint preparation, backup, diagnostics, audit query/export (U6.7 — incident investigation happens exactly when the data plane is failing), reserve release, orderly shutdown (R27), and the R28 incident levers — identity disable, grant reduction, credential revocation (U3.6): a data-lane flood must not starve the revocation that remediates it (reserved-lane admission remains subject to U6.5's capacity floor) — recovery must work while the data plane is failing closed.

**Overload-aggregate durability (R13):** saturation rejections accumulate in a bounded in-memory map (bucket count capped; overflow collapses into a catch-all bucket) and are flushed by piggybacking on the next transaction committed through EITHER lane — no timer, no in-daemon scheduler (complexity budget). Counts pending at a crash are lost; this bounded loss window is documented as part of R13's aggregate posture and asserted in U6.6.

**Cancellation semantics (explicit, R26):** cancellation before a command begins prevents execution; once a transaction begins, the coordinator completes or aborts by the TRANSACTION result even if the client disconnects — the audit outcome records committed state, never inferred response delivery. CAS remains the client's protection against ambiguous retries.

**Batching posture:** the protocol is batch-READY (a later bounded micro-batch could commit independently ordered commands in one write transaction) but v0.1 ships with batching DISABLED; any future batching must preserve per-command linearization, audit ordering, response-after-commit, bounded secret retention, and revocation ordering (Performance & Capacity).

**Acceptance:** saturation test — memory bounded, excess rejected in order, reserved lane still serves control work; cancellation-before-begin vs after-begin behave as specified (fault-injection harness); overload aggregates flush on next commit and their crash-loss window is bounded as documented.
**Plan-review execution contract (binding):** one dedicated thread owns the WRITER/coordinator, not necessarily every `Database` handle. Storage may expose a bounded internal MVCC read-snapshot service for audit query and backup: no raw handle leaves the storage module; snapshot concurrency, lifetime, result buffering, and page-pinning are hard-bounded; stale/cancelled cursors release promptly. Writer commands have accepted → started → committed/aborted → replied states. Cancellation only prevents accepted/not-started work; receiver loss after start completes the transaction and zeroizes any undeliverable secret response.

Define weighted fairness so recovery/control work cannot starve data forever and data floods cannot starve revocation/recovery. The coalesced U1.5 watermark command is a single-purpose maintenance item (max one pending); missing its durable deadline fails readiness. Add worker-panic propagation/readiness tests, lane-boundary N−1/N/N+1 tests, snapshot lifetime/page-pinning tests, and response-buffer zeroization assertions. Logs expose queue/lane/state/count/duration only—never command payloads.

**Reserve-control admission amendment:** `store reserve status|release|recreate` are explicit typed commands. Status, release, and recreate all use the recovery lane under the frozen `store-maintenance` authorization mapping (per the Lane correction below). Once release starts, its held two-transaction budget cannot be consumed by unrelated control work. Weighted fairness never delays recreate behind ordinary diagnostics, and response loss follows the accepted→started→committed/aborted→replied state model. Unit tests include lane saturation during release/recreate, caller disconnect, worker panic, held-budget accounting, and secret-safe typed logs.

**Lane correction:** reserve status is admitted on the same bounded recovery lane as release/recreate and consumes the `store-maintenance` command mapping; it is not a general diagnostics-lane operation.
notes: Ownership ambiguity and resolution (2026-07-17): U6.1 says the named thread exclusively owns the redb writer/coordinator, while downstream U6.2 explicitly owns the typed atomic state-plus-audit coordinator commands and record semantics. Creating placeholder transactions here would fake R26. Safest resolution: U6.1 provides and verifies the complete generic bounded execution boundary; storage.writer_coordinator_adapter is registered to U6.2, which must place the real redb writer backend behind it. U6.7 and U10.2 own the real MVCC audit/backup cursor adapters. Implementation: named single writer; bounded 64 data and 16 reserved recovery defaults with urgent reserve/revocation sublane; 3:1 weighted fairness; pre-decrypt overload refusal; 64-bucket saturating aggregates with catch-all, atomic-backend handoff and abort requeue; accepted/started/committed/aborted/replied cancellation behavior; undeliverable response zeroization; worker panic readiness failure; coalesced watermark/deadline failure; bounded internal snapshot service with two-worker/8-queue/30s/1MiB defaults, internal cursors, cancellation/deadline/overflow page-pin release, and redacted zeroizing buffers. Batching disabled and crash-loss window documented. Verification: 8 focused unit cases and 1 harness/canary integration case passed; strict all-feature clippy passed; complete local manifest run 1ee6f6f903cbbd9882b1ab3917bf5d75 passed compatibility, lock/MSRV, fmt, clippy, default all-target tests, docs, Rust 1.85 build/tests, harness default/MSRV checks, and canary gate.

title: U6.2: transaction coordinator — atomic state+audit, read-audits-before-transmit (R26/R28)
description:
**Build:** The transaction coordinator — the SINGLE commit boundary owning the R26/R28 invariants. Built FIRST in this unit; everything else composes onto it.

**Mutation path:** KV write/delete/destroy, identity/grant/credential/rotation lifecycle, and keyring-envelope changes commit the state change AND its audit entry in ONE multi-table write transaction; the response is sent only after commit. Neither becomes visible without the other (AE9).

**Read path:** revalidate the token and CURRENT grants inside the transaction boundary (this is what makes R28 hold without an invalidation bus — tokens are capability-thin), decrypt, prepare the bounded response, append the read audit entry (with served version), COMMIT, and only then transmit. If the audit commit fails, the client receives an error and NO secret material (AE10). The audited outcome for a read means "authorized and response prepared" — no server can attest the client received the bytes.

**Rejected alternatives (do not reintroduce):** a separate audit file, an async audit queue, best-effort dual-writes — each reintroduces the crash window between state and record that R26 exists to close.

**Boundary enforcement:** no handler, middleware, background task, or application service owns a raw database handle or a standalone audit writer — module visibility (pub(crate) discipline) makes the coordinator the ONLY commit path. The Definition of Done requires this to be structurally true, and the crash suite proves the atomicity.

**Linearization (R28/KTD8):** identity disable, grant reduction, credential revocation, and authorized requests linearize through the storage transaction boundary — any request whose authorization transaction begins after an administrative change commits observes the new state, independent of token TTL; an already-linearized in-flight request may complete. Every resulting rejection is audited.

**Acceptance:** typed command API compiles handlers against services only; a leaked-handle attempt fails to compile (visibility test); AE9/AE10 kill-point + fault-injection tests land in U6.6.
**Plan-review hardening:** every secret-bearing prepared response uses a zeroizing buffer and is destroyed on commit failure, pre-start cancellation, caller disconnect/receiver loss, panic, or serialization failure. Compile-fail visibility tests prove handlers cannot obtain raw write transactions, database handles, or audit writers. Deterministic barriers test administrative commit vs request-authorization start on both sides of the linearization point; failure logs contain ids/outcomes/timing only.
notes: Needs user review — contract ownership conflict resolved. Conflict: U6.2 says the coordinator is built first and all audit work composes onto it, while the integration-tail ledger assigned U6.2 a concrete real-redb state+audit adapter whose canonical encrypted audit schema and chain are explicitly owned by dependent U6.3. Implementing that adapter now would duplicate or invent U6.3 security-critical formats. Resolution: U6.2 implements and structurally enforces the typed single-writer transaction protocol and StorageBackend adapter: current authorization, mutation/read preparation, required row-delta versus whole-state commitments, audit append, one commit, then reply; denial/read audits carry no state commitment; zeroizing responses cover failure/disconnect/panic; compile-fail docs prevent service/database handle leakage. Direct versioned-secret handler-like methods were reduced to pub(crate). The ledger now assigns only the concrete real-redb TransactionFactory over canonical encrypted audit tables to U6.3; U6.2 retains the clock coordinator watermark tail. This is the safest dependency-consistent split and preserves R26/R28 without inventing a parallel audit format. Verification: focused coordinator 11/11, executor, versioned-secret, startup ledger, doc compile-fail, clippy all passed; panic race repeated 3x on default and Rust 1.85; full local verify run 056e7c3e8a4c5681af415dcba8bdd306 passed compatibility pins/capture, fmt, clippy, all-target tests, docs, Rust 1.85 build/tests, harness clippy/tests/MSRV tests, and canary scan on x86_64. AE9/AE10 process kill-point suite remains explicitly owned by U6.6. ARM64 execution unavailable per user and not claimed.

title: U6.3: audit events — canonical schema, encrypted payloads, blake3 chain (R13/R25/KTD4)
description:
**Build:** The audit event model + hash chain (R13/R25/KTD4).

**One externally visible operation = ONE canonical, schema-versioned audit event** containing nested authentication and authorization results — never an unbounded number of rows for internal matcher steps. Fields: server-generated event id, request id, audit schema version, identity, public credential accessor (never the secret), optional stable consumer-instance id (KTD5), auth method, canonical resource, requested capability, operation, outcome, reason code, effective time, wall-clock observation, and — for successful secret reads/writes — the version served or written (this field is what makes R33 rotation adoption observable). NEVER secret values or credential material.

**R25 (global output ban):** secret values and credential material appear in NO server output — audit entries, app/access logs, traces, metrics labels, error responses, panic output, crash diagnostics, header/body echoes. Secret paths and identity names are additionally banned from metrics labels. Enforced here structurally (typed redaction at the event/serialization layer) and attacked by U11's canary scan.

**Chain (KTD4):** per-entry blake3 hash chain over ENCRYPTED payloads: entry payloads encrypt under the keyring's audit-payload key (paths and identity names reveal topology — confidential at rest, readable only under audit capabilities R22); the plaintext envelope carries only **envelope encoding version, audit-epoch id, epoch-scoped sequence, timestamp, previous hash, and ciphertext digest** — so the chain VERIFIES WITHOUT DECRYPTING. **Epoch semantics (the KTD17 hook, decided NOW so G2 freezes it):** sequence is scoped to the audit epoch; each epoch opens with a genesis entry that binds the previous epoch's terminal chain head, so `audit verify` can distinguish two chains with overlapping sequence ranges after a recovery fork (U10.4) without a schema change. **Envelope timestamp domain:** the envelope timestamp is EFFECTIVE TIME from U1.5's high-water clock (monotone, never regresses — this is what makes envelope-level window pruning sound for R23 scans and U6.7's time-range filters); the wall-clock observation lives only inside the encrypted payload. This is the Certificate-Transparency signed-tree-head pattern reduced to single-node scale; full Merkle inclusion proofs would be over-building.

**Flood accounting (R13):** requests dropped by the pre-verifier rate limiter aggregate into bounded entries (source bucket, count, window) — an unauthenticated attacker cannot fill the disk one audit row at a time.

**Scan path (KTD16 deferred):** status/observation queries scan primary events inside R23's lookback window; the scan is the ONLY query path and primary events are ALWAYS authoritative — status can never be wrong for index reasons.

**Audit genesis:** wire the genesis event into U1.2's init transaction.

**G2 freeze artifacts (this bead produces them):** canonical audit-event encoding vectors and chain-envelope fixtures — including tamper/reject cases and a schema-version round-trip — committed under tests/fixtures/; named in Gate G2's exit criteria (item 3 has nothing to bind without them).

**Acceptance:** event round-trip with schema version; chain verifies without decrypt; epoch-genesis entry binds the prior terminal head (unit-level; full fork coverage in U10); envelope timestamps are non-regressing under a wall-clock rollback test; canary strings absent from serialized events/errors/panics; genesis lands in init; encoding vectors + envelope fixtures committed.

**Un-ignore ownership:** this bead completes tests/startup.rs scenario 2's chain-write assertion and scenario 18's chain-verification tail (they run un-ignored before this bead closes).
**Plan-review frozen-envelope correction:** the clear envelope is exactly versioned and privacy-minimal: `{envelope_version, audit_epoch, epoch_sequence, effective_timestamp, previous_hash, ciphertext_digest}` in a canonical G2-frozen encoding. Operation/type remains inside encrypted payload; no clear type-id leakage. Freeze chain-hash domain label/framing, field widths/order/endianness/time unit, nonce/header/ciphertext-digest coverage, size bounds, unknown-version behavior, and strict no-trailing-bytes decode. G2 owns one canonical closed audit event/domain registry. U10.1 registers the recovery-family identifiers in that registry before freeze; U3.2 consumes it rather than owning a prerequisite that lands later.

Add the bounded `clock_high_water_checkpoint` event used by U1.5; it is coalesced and capacity-accounted. Golden/negative vectors include unknown/truncated/noncanonical envelopes, epoch genesis, recovery fork families, and the cross-release envelope_version discriminator. U11.9a failure summaries use envelope version/epoch/sequence/timestamp/digest prefix—never type.

**Audit-payload crypto frame (binding):** reuse U2.4's canonical AEAD framing and primitive under a distinct `audit-event` domain. The clear crypto header carries suite/version, key id, nonce, and bounded lengths; AAD binds store id, audit epoch, sequence, event id, effective time, and previous hash. The envelope's ciphertext_digest covers the complete canonical header+nonce+ciphertext framing. Historical key selection is therefore deterministic and U8's forward-only audit-key generation remains decryptable without inventing a second format.

**Failure-schema precision:** authentication, authorization, resource, capability, and served/written version are typed result/optional fields. Failed authentication has unresolved-auth metadata only; a rejected raw target carries a stable reason plus bounded digest/offset instead of a fabricated canonical resource; fields that do not exist are absent, never sentinel strings or raw hostile bytes.

**Mutation commitments:** every successful state mutation includes the bounded sorted before/after tuple commitments defined by U2.5 inside the encrypted payload; reads and failed mutations carry an empty delta. The G2 registry freezes this codec, explicit absence, ordering, maxima, and bulk-transition form. Golden negatives cover field/domain swaps, omitted/duplicate tuples, wrong before/after values, and trailing data.
notes: Needs user review — dependency/assembly conflicts resolved without weakening the audit kernel. Conflict 1: U6.3 requires G2 to freeze recovery-fork audit families while saying U10.1 registers them later; U10.1 is downstream of U6.3, so it cannot supply a prerequisite registry before this bead closes. Resolution: U6.3 now owns the closed canonical AuditOperation/AuditCapability registry and reserves RecoveryFork/Restore/Migration/Compaction identifiers and negative vectors; U10.1 must consume these frozen identifiers rather than create a second format. Conflict 2: the integration-tail ledger assigns init-refusal and final-shutdown audit events to U6.3, but the concrete bootstrap identity/auth backend and final transport shutdown assembler are downstream beads and do not yet exist. Resolution: genesis is actually encrypted and persisted in U1.2 KeyringInitTransaction/Store::create_with_keyring atomically; U6.3 supplies tested real-redb coordinator AuditOnly commands for initialized-store refusal and shutdown using the same chain/commit path, and startup scenarios 2/18 now cite executable evidence. Downstream assembly has only to invoke these typed commands and cannot invent another writer or audit format. Implementation: schema-v1 typed event with no secret/body/header fields or Debug, strict canonical codec, bounded overload/flood aggregates, exact delta/whole-state validation, U2.4 AuditPayload AEAD whose AAD binding packs epoch/sequence/event/effective-time/previous-hash, privacy-minimal six-field clear envelope, full-frame digest, domain-separated BLAKE3 chain, epoch genesis previous-terminal binding, real redb audit event/head tables, concrete U6.2 TransactionFactory that atomically commits authenticated clock state+tuple delta+encrypted audit+overloads, audit-only refusal/shutdown path, at-rest topology canary, golden event/envelope fixtures and strict negatives. Verification: cargo all-target tests/docs/clippy passed; focused audit 4/4 and real-redb factory test passed; full local verify run 46b068a25110568d9fa3788bd0890419 passed compatibility pins/capture, fmt, clippy, all-target tests, docs, Rust 1.85 build/tests, harness clippy/tests/MSRV, and canary scan on x86_64. ARM64 execution unavailable per user and not claimed.

title: U6.4: external ed25519 checkpoints + state digest + audit verify (KTD4/KTD15/R14)
description:
**Build:** Externally signed checkpoints (KTD4) + state digest (KTD15) + `audit verify`.

**Trust design:** the daemon stores only checkpoint PUBLIC keys and key-lineage metadata — never the private key — and has NO checkpoint scheduler (complexity budget: no in-daemon timers). A systemd timer or the operator invokes the CLI. This keeps encryption-recovery and audit-signing authority as distinct trust roles and removes the signing key from the long-running process: a live attacker can fabricate events and misreport chain heads only until an external checkpoint anchors them (R14's documented exclusion — detection of offline/post-checkpoint tampering, not prevention of live forgery).

**Flow:**
1. `audit checkpoint prepare` — commits an audited canonical descriptor: store id, audit epoch, sequence range, chain head, KTD15 state digest, timestamp, signing-key id, previous-checkpoint digest. **Prepare-boundary rule (no off-by-one):** the prepare event itself is the LAST entry of the anchored range — descriptor and its audit event commit in one transaction, and the descriptor's chain head is computed INCLUDING that entry (otherwise every checkpoint leaves an eternally unanchored prepare event and two implementers disagree at every boundary). `previous_checkpoint_digest` always references the last REGISTERED checkpoint; a prepared descriptor abandoned by a crash is superseded by the next prepare and reported by doctor until superseded.
2. `audit checkpoint sign` is the exact local/offline command: it loads the private signing key from an approved typed source (R37), verifies descriptor/domain/key-id/expiry, signs with ed25519-dalek, writes+fsyncs a detached checkpoint signature through a no-follow atomic output, and zeroizes the key. It never contacts the daemon or treats key possession as register authority.
3. `audit checkpoint register` — verifies the signature against the stored public key and records the checkpoint digest.
A crash between steps leaves an unsigned prepared descriptor or a signed-but-unregistered file — both recoverable, both visible to doctor. Checkpoints form their own signed chain via previous_checkpoint_digest; signing-key rotation retains the old public key and records a trust transition, so old checkpoints never become unverifiable. Backup manifests (U10) reuse this signing flow under a DISTINCT domain-separation label.

**Descriptor + digest encodings are G2-frozen:** both are cross-release verifiable formats (R14 requires old checkpoint files to re-verify after upgrades); this bead commits canonical descriptor-encoding and state-digest-tuple vectors under tests/fixtures/, named in Gate G2's exit criteria.

**State digest (KTD15):** deterministic digest computed INSIDE the same write transaction that commits the prepare descriptor+event — state tables as they stand in that transaction, descriptor/audit tables excluded (they are anchored by the chain head, not the digest) — signed together with the audit head — sorted (table, key, generation, mac) tuples for MAC'd clear records PLUS (table, primary key incl. version, blake3(header ‖ nonce ‖ ciphertext)) tuples for encrypted-record tables (U2.5's coverage rule) — catches deletion/rollback of clear records AND offline removal of secret versions, which per-record MACs cannot.

**Verify:** `audit verify [--checkpoint <path>] [--state]` — checks the chain against the last registered checkpoint, verifies the anchored state digest, reconciles post-checkpoint mutations from the audit tail, and reports R14's two v0.1 tiers: fully inspected events anchored by a retained checkpoint, and the unanchored active tail. Given divergent continuations of the same chain prefix (post-restore split brain), verify names them as DISTINCT RESTORE INCARNATIONS via U10.3's incarnation marker and reports the divergence point — never a generic break. Off-host mirroring is operator-owned (rsync/cron/systemd path unit — documented in U12); the server speaks no S3/webhooks.

**Staleness posture (R36):** checkpoint staleness is a WARNING and rotation-evidence-quality annotation — not by itself a data-plane readiness failure (an off-host mirror outage must not take secret reads down); invalid state MACs, broken audit writes, exhausted capacity remain readiness failures. **Staleness predicate (defined HERE; U7.5's closeout guard and doctor consume exactly this):** stale ⇔ age of the last REGISTERED checkpoint (effective time) exceeds `checkpoint.max_age` OR the unanchored tail exceeds `checkpoint.max_unanchored_events`; both config keys documented with defaults; exposed as a stable field in health/doctor output.

**Acceptance:** prepare/sign/register round-trip; crash between steps recoverable + doctor-visible; next successful checkpoint's previous digest references the last REGISTERED checkpoint after an abandoned prepare; verify reports both tiers; re-chaining detected against a registered checkpoint; state-digest rollback detection (with U2.5 scenario 9) including deleted-newest-version detection; staleness predicate flips exactly at the configured thresholds; descriptor/digest vectors committed.
**Plan-review non-cyclic prepare construction (binding):** (1) compute the state digest; (2) construct the prepare audit event with checkpoint id, signing-key id, anchored-range start, and non-self-referential metadata—but NEVER descriptor bytes/digest/final head; (3) encrypt and chain-hash that event, producing its event id/sequence and final head; (4) construct the canonical descriptor binding that event id/sequence and resulting head; (5) atomically commit event + descriptor. A G2 vector proves this exact order, no cyclic encoding, and the prepare-is-last off-by-one boundary.

This bead freezes the public-key descriptor, trust-transition statement, signer-key-id selection, and verification seam needed by checkpoint descriptors, including wrong/unregistered/forked-lineage refusal. U6.8 owns key generation, initial enrollment, bounded retained lineage, and the prepare/sign/register rotation workflow and races. Status exports the stable fields U12.1 consumes. U6.4 tests descriptor verification under initial/retired/current key ids plus abandoned prepare, duplicate register, descriptor/event mismatch, unknown versions, checkpoint-sign sink/fsync failures, and secret-safe diagnostics; U6.8 owns operational lifecycle tests.

**State verification algorithm (binding):** `--state` first verifies the chain and each encrypted mutation-delta commitment, checks the current tuple set against every tail after-state in reverse order, substitutes each authenticated before-state/absence, and then recomputes the retained checkpoint's full sorted state digest. This is what makes legitimate post-checkpoint writes remain green while offline additions, deletions, edits, and rollbacks fail; the digest alone cannot do so. A bulk transition carries frozen before/after whole-state digests and operation metadata. U8 rewrite jobs must finish/resume an immediate external checkpoint; restore/fork activation is explicitly unanchored until its first externally registered checkpoint and cannot be called recovery-verified before it.

Tests include multiple ordered mutations to one record, multi-record atomic mutations, explicit absence, tail/delta mismatch, offline add/delete/rollback, and current-state mismatch. Pure public-key offline chain/checkpoint verification remains possible without payload decryption; live `--state` is a diagnostics-authorized operation with key access.
notes:
needs-user-review (2026-07-17): Contract spans temporal owners. U6.4 requests authenticated audit checkpoint prepare/register/verify command assembly and restore-incarnation divergence naming, while scoped dependencies assign authentication/verifier to U4.1, authenticated handlers to U3.6, operational trust enrollment/rotation and prepare/register workflow races to U6.8, doctor assembly to U12.1, and restore incarnation marker to U10.3. Resolution: implemented complete U6.4 layer-owned vertical primitives now: frozen canonical descriptor/signature/public-key formats and vectors; ed25519-dalek offline typed-source signing with domain/key-id/validity verification, zeroization, O_NOFOLLOW mode-0600 atomic output+file/directory fsync; public trust lineage/fork checks; same-redb-transaction state tuple enumeration, state digest, prepare audit append/head/descriptor commit with prepare event last; registration chained only to last REGISTERED checkpoint with duplicate/mismatch refusal; abandoned-prepare status; public-key audit/checkpoint verification tiers; reverse authenticated tail-state reconciliation; exact-boundary staleness and stable health fields/config/docs. Authenticated network/management command adapters and restore-incarnation labels remain for their explicitly owning downstream beads; offline audit checkpoint sign CLI is complete. Verification evidence: checkpoint tests 9/9, checkpoint CLI tests 2/2, config boundary test passed, ./scripts/verify.sh all run_id 4ace93727866649c704536106efb917a passed all 13 checks on x86_64 including current/MSRV all-target tests, Clippy -D warnings, docs, harness and canary; ./scripts/verify.sh --self-test passed. ARM64 unavailable by user statement.
Final verification supersedes prior run evidence: ./scripts/verify.sh all run_id 5ac767e7e365d1dc0de0061c9ed4f310 passed all 13 checks after final atomic mismatch and multi-record tail tests; x86_64. ./scripts/verify.sh --self-test also passed.

title: U6.5: capacity thresholds + recovery.reserve fail-closed guardrails (R27)
description:
**Build:** Capacity guardrails (R27): audit-log and disk capacity monitoring with WARNING and STOP-ADMITTING thresholds, plus the preallocated recovery reserve.

**Fail-closed before exhaustion:** the data plane stops admitting before storage exhaustion — a documented response, not a crash. In v0.1 every audit event stays in the active database (no segmentation/pruning — deferred post-v0.1); growth at small-team rates is a few hundred MB/year, so thresholds fire long before physical limits, but the guard must exist because R13 forbids silently discarding events.

**The recovery allowance is CONCRETE:** `init` (U1.2) preallocates a same-filesystem `recovery.reserve` file sized above the documented worst-case TOTAL of: a checkpoint prepare+register pair (local sign is file-only), backup-create `publishing` reservation + `published` outcome transactions, detached-manifest signature registration, BOTH active- and recovery-rehearsal receipt registrations, one backup/status diagnostic, one offline U8.3 emergency-control issuance (including its epoch/key/audit record), orderly shutdown, one reserve-release operation, and a documented allowance of N audited incident-investigation store invocations counting query pages, export `publishing` reservation + `published` outcome transactions, export-signature registration, and recipient/status checks (U6.7 requires the complete usable export exactly then) at worst-case event size. The data plane stops admitting BEFORE consuming it; checkpoint export, backup, audit query/export, and orderly shutdown remain possible after the data plane has failed closed (via KTD8's reserved control lane). Control-lane admission is checked against a deeper FLOOR: once remaining capacity falls to the orderly-shutdown + reserve-release budget, every other control operation (including the N+1th audit query/export) is refused with the documented overload error — physical exhaustion mid-write is never an outcome (R27). Releasing or recreating the reserve is a local, AUDITED recovery operation — ordinary request handling cannot spend it.

**In-flight headroom rule (the guarantee is only real with it):** the stop-admitting threshold is evaluated at ADMISSION and includes headroom for worst-case queued consumption — data-plane lane depth × U6.5's documented conservative maximum transaction/event budget derived from U2.1. U5.5 must choose request maxima that fit this budget or refuse the design; it is a consumer, not an input. Without this, N already-admitted commands pass the check and then write through the threshold into the reserve — voiding fail-closed at exactly the margin it exists for.

**Acceptance:** fill the active audit area to the stop-admitting threshold → data-plane admission stops while checkpoint registration, diagnostics, audit query/export, and orderly shutdown still succeed via the reserved lane + reserve file; saturate the data lane exactly at the threshold boundary → all queued commands complete WITHOUT touching recovery.reserve (headroom rule); warning threshold surfaces in health/doctor before the stop threshold; reserve release requires the audited local operation. Feeds milestone M2 (disk-reserve behavior).
**Plan-review reserve protocol and shared budget (binding):** U6.5 publishes one conservative worst-case transaction/storage budget derived from U2.1; every U5.5 request bound must fit it and lane-depth headroom uses it. Include the fixed idle-clock-checkpoint event rate/bytes in annual growth and threshold calculations. The reserve must have REAL allocated blocks on the target filesystem (verify allocation; `set_len` sparse holes do not count), preserve mode/owner, reject symlinks, and handle quota/ENOSPC plus external disk consumption conservatively.

Reserve release/recreate is an idempotent audited state machine: commit+fsync `release_requested` → rename/release the allocated file → fsync parent directory → commit `released`; startup reconciles every marker/file combination before data admission. No claim of a single atomic filesystem+DB action. Test crashes at each transition, Nth/N+1th control allowance, external-consumption races, exact threshold N−1/N/N+1, and recovery after marker/file disagreement with detailed safe block/count logs.

**Init provisioning extension:** the reserve creation half of U1.2 uses an allocated no-follow temp, allocation verification, file+parent fsync, atomic rename, and an expected-reserve record in the init transaction. Startup reconciles every record/temp/final-file combination before admission; valid orphan reuse is explicit, and no committed store may serve without the matching reserve. Fault tests cover short allocation, sparse-file deception, quota/ENOSPC, rename/fsync crashes, and foreign artifacts.

Worst-case capacity includes the maximum ordinary mutation-delta commitment and each bulk-transition/checkpoint event. If a bounded request could exceed the frozen transaction/event budget, U5 rejects it before admission; the reserve estimate never silently grows after implementation.

**Bounded-key/recipient accounting:** capacity math includes worst-case keyring envelope at 32 audit-payload generations, checkpoint public-lineage record at 16 keys, effective backup recipients at 8, export recipients at 8, and their G2/G3 maximum encodings. Warn thresholds reserve room for one final audited refusal/recovery operation; hard-limit checks occur before RNG/allocation/mutation. N−1/N/N+1 plus concurrent final-slot cases compose with recovery.reserve. Checkpoint prepare+register are the two DB-capacity-consuming steps; local `sign` consumes no store bytes but its detached output limits are separately preflighted.

**Stop-threshold recovery proof:** at the exact data-stop threshold, the reserved lane completes the full minimal recovery sequence—backup create publishing-reservation + published-outcome events, manifest sign registration, both offline receipt registrations, checkpoint prepare/register, doctor/status, and—when no valid control token remains—clean shutdown plus offline emergency issuance followed by authenticated recovery operations—without consuming the shutdown+reserve-release floor. N+1 registration refuses before write; tests use maximum event/record encodings and include retry/lost-reply idempotency.

After capacity is restored, emergency control performs the finite U4.5 normal-successor handoff and is revoked; the reserve need not budget an immortal admin credential. Tests cover expired/lost credential at the threshold, offline unwrap/issue, recovery-operation authentication, and refusal before the shutdown floor.

At least one maximum-size emergency export-create event plus its detached-signature registration is explicitly inside N; a detached-but-unregistered export is not counted as successful incident evidence. Boundary tests consume query/create/register in varied order and preserve the shutdown floor.

**Two-transaction output budget:** reserve arithmetic includes maximum-size `output_publication.v1` reservation and published-outcome records plus both audit events for one backup and one maximum export, their retry/reconciliation reads, and one terminal owner-abandon record if bytes cannot be recovered. The deeper shutdown floor is checked before reservation so the second transaction can always finish; once `publishing` commits, capacity is held against that output id and cannot be consumed by an unrelated command. N-1/N/N+1 tests force exhaustion before reservation, after reservation/before rename, after rename/before outcome, and during abandonment without stranding an unreportable state.

**Plan-space audit — operable reserve controls (binding):** this owner must expose the recovery state machine through exact commands; an implementation that requires direct database or filesystem surgery is incomplete.

- `store reserve status` is read-only, bounded, stable-JSON output. It reports reserve generation/state, expected versus allocated bytes, current capacity band, held publication budgets, and the exact next command using only safe counts/opaque ids—never a raw path.
- `store reserve release --expected-generation <G> --reason <text> --confirm <digest>` is available only after the capacity guard has stopped data admission. It requires service-owner locality plus a non-disabled control-audience principal with `store-maintenance`, rechecked at the final barrier. Filesystem ownership, a store lock, or an age identity alone is not authority. Release is idempotent by generation/operation id and follows the already-frozen release_requested → file release+parent fsync → released protocol.
- `store reserve recreate --expected-generation <G> --reason <text> --confirm <digest>` allocates and verifies real blocks through the frozen no-follow temp/fsync/rename protocol, then reconciles the authenticated record. It succeeds only when the full reserve plus ordinary headroom fit after allocation. Until recreation is complete, the data plane remains non-ready even though releasing the file made bytes available.
- Online forms use the reserved control lane. Offline forms require daemon absence, the exclusive no-follow lock, approved keyring unwrap, and the same control credential/capability; they exist so a host that was shut down after release is recoverable. A committed released/recreate-in-progress state never boots the data listener.

**Tests/observability:** pure unit cases cover command parsing, authorization, generations, confirmation binding, healthy-state release refusal, capacity arithmetic, idempotency, and N−1/N/N+1. Integration/fault cases kill at every DB/file/fsync boundary, restart in every state, expire/revoke authority around the final barrier, and prove release→external cleanup→recreate→readiness recovery. The assembled recovery E2E executes the real commands. U11.9a logs command/phase/generation/allocated bytes/capacity band/point/outcome/timing and a redacted reproducer; U11.6 scans all raw output before rendering or retention.

**Capability correction (supersedes any earlier diagnostics mapping):** all three `store reserve status|release|recreate` commands require the single concrete `store-maintenance` capability. Status is an operation-scoped safe view, so a narrowly scoped recovery principal must not also receive broad diagnostics merely to inspect the generation it is about to recover. Full cross-domain capacity diagnosis remains available through `doctor` under diagnostics.
notes:
U1.5 capacity input (2026-07-17): fixed idle clock-watermark checkpoint interval 30s, commit deadline 100ms, tolerance 2s, hence maximum restart rollback/TTL-extension bound 32.1s. Each idle checkpoint is one meta-record rewrite plus one bounded canonical audit event owned by U6.3; at most one command may be pending. U6.5 must measure/include this event and storage rate in final capacity thresholds.

Conflict/assembly seam: acceptance requires live init/redb persistence, online/offline authenticated reserve commands, and assembled full recovery E2E, while init/serve remain intentionally fail-closed until downstream recovery assembly. Resolution: implement and freeze the complete capacity arithmetic/admission kernel, authenticated canonical reserve record/state transitions, exact CLI, and real allocated filesystem protocol now; transfer startup.init_recovery_reserve and startup.reserve_recovery uniquely to U11.9r for real coordinator/init assembly rather than fake persistence. Implementation: 9 MiB transaction cap; 64-deep/576 MiB in-flight headroom; 64-slot/576 MiB recovery reserve; eight six-step incident investigations; 512-byte/30s idle event and 538,214,400-byte annual warning margin; exact 1,207,959,552-byte data-stop and 18 MiB shutdown floors; publication completion/abandon holds; observed-capacity rechecks; N/N+1 boundaries; mode-0600 O_NOFOLLOW create-exclusive posix_fallocate with real-block/owner/link verification, fsync/rename/dir-fsync; crash-reconcilable release_requested/released/recreate_requested/healthy record; digest confirmations; store-maintenance-only local authority; store reserve status/release/recreate CLI. Verification: capacity 9/9, storage_executor 1/1, transaction_coordinator 11/11, startup_skeleton 10/10, strict clippy, and full ./scripts/verify.sh run 40db5448545adc48a35d8489d6a8c407 passed compatibility, crypto vectors, fmt, clippy, all-target/doc, Rust 1.85 build/test, harness, and canary gates on x86_64. ARM64 remains externally unavailable per user.

title: U6.6: audit + crash test suites (tests/audit.rs, tests/fault/txn_crash.rs — AE5/AE9/AE10)
description:
**Build:** tests/audit.rs + tests/fault/txn_crash.rs — the coordinator/audit suites (declare `[[test]] name = "txn_crash", path = "tests/fault/txn_crash.rs"` in Cargo.toml; files under tests/ subdirectories are not auto-discovered). Test-first: this log is the sole record a rotation trusts; the coordinator is the invariant everything leans on. All scenarios log through the shared test-observability harness (U11.9a).

**Scenarios:**
1. AE9 crash atomicity: kill the process at injected points around a secret write AND around a token revocation → after restart, state change and audit entry exist together or not at all.
2. AE5 tamper: edit or remove an entry on disk → verification fails and identifies where the chain breaks.
3. Re-chaining attack: rewrite an entry and recompute ALL subsequent hashes → verification against the exported checkpoint still fails; truncation at/before the retained checkpoint is detected. A later unanchored tail is labeled unverifiable: truncation is detected only when a later independently retained head/receipt is supplied or a missing mutation makes state reconciliation fail. A read-only unanchored tail can be truncated without cryptographic detection and tests must not claim otherwise (R14).
4. AE10: fault-injected audit-commit failure → the triggering read returns an error with NO secret bytes; the mutation does not commit (R26).
5. R25 canary: a secret value passed through a request appears in NO audit entry, error response, or panic output; audit payloads in the DB file are ciphertext (byte scan for canary strings).
6. Failed-auth flood → bounded aggregate audit volume, not one row per packet (R13); overload aggregates flush by piggybacking on the next commit and the crash-loss window is bounded as documented (U6.1).
7. R27 capacity: audit store approaches capacity → warning then fail-closed before exhaustion, checkpoint export + shutdown still possible from the reserve; lane saturated exactly at the stop threshold → queued commands complete without touching recovery.reserve (U6.5's headroom rule).
8. Status-scan anchor: R23-window scan over a SYNTHETIC CORPUS representing YEARS of small-team events returns correct window membership and served-version fields within operator-command latency tolerance — this measurement is the recorded anchor for KTD16's upgrade trigger (R33/R23). (On-current/on-prior/silent-since-write BUCKET correctness is U7.4's logic, asserted in tests/rotation.rs against this same corpus generator — not re-implemented here.)
9. Executor saturation: bounded memory, orderly rejection, reserved control lane serves backup/checkpoint/shutdown (KTD8 — moved from the KTD2 spike, wayfinder T04; shared with U2.8 scenario 13).
10. Cancellation semantics (KTD8/R26 — U6.1's explicit contract, attacked not assumed): client cancel/disconnect BEFORE the command begins → command never executes, no state change, no audit entry; cancel/disconnect AFTER the transaction begins → outcome follows the TRANSACTION result (commit or abort) regardless of the vanished client, and the audit outcome records committed state — never inferred response delivery.
11. Checkpoint lifecycle faults: kill between prepare/sign and between sign/register → recoverable, surfaced in status/doctor output; next successful checkpoint's previous-checkpoint digest references the last REGISTERED checkpoint (abandoned prepare superseded); duplicate register is idempotent-or-rejected and audited; `audit checkpoint register` with a wrong-key signature, or with a stale/forked checkpoint that does not extend previous_checkpoint_digest → each refused and audited.
12. Signing-key rotation/checkpoint lineage only: register under a rotated key → old checkpoints still verify via retained lineage; a checkpoint signed by an unregistered key is refused. Audit-export bundle tamper/member cases belong exclusively to U6.7.
13. R28 linearization race: commit an identity disable / grant reduction / credential revocation while N reads are in flight at the coordinator (test-double identity state — no token stack required) → every request whose authorization transaction began after the admin commit is rejected under the new state, each rejection audited; already-linearized requests complete; no interleaving exists where a post-commit-authorized response reflects pre-commit grants.

**Un-ignore ownership:** this bead removes the #[ignore] from tests/store.rs scenarios 9 and 13 and makes them pass against the real U6.1 executor and U6.4 checkpoint flow.

**Verification:** `cargo test --test audit --test txn_crash` green; `<binary> audit verify` detects a hand-tampered log.
**Plan-review suite split:** scenario 12 retains signing-key rotation/checkpoint-lineage cases only; encrypted audit-export bundle tamper and offline-member naming move to U6.7, which now owns those scenarios and depends directly on U11.9a. Failed-auth, revocation, and backup-lane rows here may use deterministic synthetic producers; real endpoint kill points are consolidated in U11.4a. Separate deterministic scan correctness from hardware-sensitive performance anchoring; log corpus seed/count/window/threshold, point name, exit signal, duration, and redacted reproducer.

**State-tail cases:** legitimate post-checkpoint add/change/delete remains green through authenticated reverse replay; offline tuple add/delete/edit/rollback, missing/duplicated delta, reordered multi-tuple delta, and truncated mutation tail fail with the exact sequence/check id. Separately prove read-only unanchored-tail truncation is reported as unverifiable unless an external later head is supplied.

**Checkpoint/trust authority cases:** prepare/register/trust enrollment/rotation require audit-checkpoint-manage; local checkpoint sign uses key custody but cannot register. Wrong audit-read/export/diagnostics/key/transport capability, audience, UID, disabled/revoked principal, final-barrier race, enrollment race, wrong-domain/retired-signing-key/replay/fork/limit all fail with one authoritative audit state and secret-safe logs.

**Capacity-command extension to scenario 7:** after reaching stop-admitting, invoke the real typed reserve status/release/recreate service API. Prove healthy release refuses, stopped release preserves the shutdown floor, released state stays non-ready, restart requires offline recreation, real-block recreation plus restored headroom returns readiness, and duplicate/lost replies remain idempotent. Wrong generation/reason/confirmation/capability and final-barrier revocation have zero success mutation. Detailed logs carry only state/generation/bytes/band/point/timing.
notes:
Conflict: scenario 1 says kill a process around live secret-write/token-revoke commits, while the same contract plan-review explicitly consolidates real endpoint kill points in still-open U11.4a. Several checkpoint authority races likewise require still-open assembled owner-socket persistence. Duplicating a second production coordinator or claiming injected faults are OS-kill evidence would be unsafe.

Resolution: this bead owns deterministic coordinator transaction fault injection (after-state, after-audit, serialization, commit, panic), cancellation/disconnect semantics, read-secret withholding/zeroization, R28 pre/post-admin linearization, encrypted chain/checkpoint/reverse-tail tamper proofs, capacity/reserve and limiter evidence, signing-lineage evidence, and shared safe observability. Real endpoint SIGKILL/disk faults remain in U11.4a; live owner-peer/final-barrier checkpoint authority remains in the registered U11.9r/signing-trust tails.

Implementation: declared the explicit txn_crash integration target; composed the real coordinator fault suite; added a 13-scenario audit evidence harness; added a deterministic 250,000-event multi-year window scan anchor; activated U6.6 store scenarios 9/13 and removed both ignores; added read-only `audit verify` CLI output and a direct redb audit-row tamper test.

Verification: cargo test --test audit --test txn_crash --test store passed 33/33. Intact `audit verify` returns verified metadata; tampered ciphertext fails with code=integrity and empty stdout. ./scripts/verify.sh run 1b2b5e010d36a472d4bc60249bded410 passed compatibility pins/capture, x86_64 crypto vectors, fmt, clippy, all targets, docs, Rust 1.85 build/tests, harness clippy/tests/MSRV, and canary gate. ARM64 execution remains externally unavailable per user.

title: U3.1: RawTargetGuard + canonical Resource parser — one module, one corpus (KTD9/R8)
description:
**Build:** `RawTargetGuard` + the canonical `Resource` parser — ONE module, ONE test corpus (KTD9/R8). This is the security joint most Vault-alike servers get wrong.

**Why the guard runs FIRST:** axum's `Path` extractor percent-decodes before handlers run, so validation after extraction cannot distinguish a literal separator from an encoded alias (`a%2Fb` vs `a/b`). The outer guard validates `Request::uri()` — the RAW request target — before any route extraction or percent-decoding.

**The guard REJECTS (never normalizes):** malformed escapes; percent-encoded separators and backslashes (%2f, %2F, %5c); encoded percent signs enabling a second decode (%25); encoded or literal dot segments; empty MIDDLE segments and repeated separators (R8's precise rule — see edge cases below); control characters; NUL; overlong targets; duplicate security-sensitive query parameters; noncanonical alternate encodings; invalid UTF-8. Then it performs EXACTLY ONE decode and constructs an `EndpointRequest` carrying the endpoint kind and canonical `Resource {mount, canonical_segments}`.

**Fixture-governed edge cases (do NOT decide by fiat):** a trailing slash on list-form endpoints and the zero-segment mount-root list are accepted or rejected exactly per U0's captured pinned-client fixtures — `vault`/`bao` list operations commonly address `.../metadata/<path>/`, and a blanket reject-all-empty-segments rule would break legitimate captured traffic while a blanket accept would widen the surface. The U0.2 path-encoding matrix is the evidence; the corpus includes both forms either way. On every NON-list endpoint form a trailing empty segment is REJECTED outright (fixture deference is scoped to list forms); the corpus includes a data-form trailing-slash case.

**Contract:** the typed request goes into request extensions and is the ONLY resource representation available to authorization and handlers. Handlers NEVER reconstruct a resource from a framework-decoded path value. The project uses NO path-normalization middleware. One parser converts every endpoint form — data/, metadata/, list, delete, undelete, destroy, ?version=N — to the canonical resource exactly once. Segments compare byte-for-byte after one validated UTF-8 decode — NO Unicode normalization. The supported segment-character contract is published and frozen by U0/U11 evidence.

**Grants shape (consumed by the matcher bead):** {mount, exact_or_subtree, prefix_segments, capabilities} — subtree matches are SEGMENT-prefix, so a grant on apps/foo never reaches apps/foobar.

**Acceptance:** raw corpus — %2f/%2F/%5c/%25/%2e, malformed sequences, repeated separators, double-encoded forms — ALL rejected before route dispatch; noncanonical encodings of an in-grant path rejected rather than authorized as an alias; trailing-slash list form and zero-segment mount-root list behave exactly per captured fixtures; property test: all endpoint forms of one logical path map to one resource.
**Plan-review parser contract:** centralize raw-target byte bound and test N−1/N/N+1 before allocation. Add `+`, encoded query-name aliases, percent-case, list-root/trailing slash and fixture-governed empty segments. Rejection diagnostics contain stable reason, byte offset and bounded escaped prefix/digest only—never uncontrolled raw target. U3.1 consumes normalized canary-clean U0.3 fixtures (dependency corrected). Property/corpus logs carry case id/seed/minimized form digest and exact redacted reproducer.
notes:
Conflict review (2026-07-16): U3.1 requires list-root and list trailing-slash behavior to follow U0 captured fixtures, but U0.2/U0.3 path cases exercised KV get only and contain no LIST request. Chosen safest narrow resolution: accept a zero-segment root or one trailing empty segment only when endpoint is metadata and list intent is explicit via LIST method or canonical list=true; reject empties on every non-list form. Rationale: matches the bead explicit scoped exception and common pinned Vault/Bao list addressing without broad normalization. Implementation and verification evidence will include this boundary plus a fixture-consumption test documenting the missing LIST evidence.

Resolution/implementation evidence (2026-07-16): Applied the scoped LIST resolution above. The contract also omitted a numeric raw-target bound; chose 8192 bytes as the conservative common HTTP request-line ceiling, enforced before split/allocation, with N-1/N/N+1 tests. Implemented one raw_target module that validates raw URI bytes, rejects malformed/lowercase/noncanonical escapes, encoded slash/backslash/percent/dot, literal dot segments, repeated/empty non-list segments, controls/NUL, invalid UTF-8, query aliases/duplicates, and unsupported characters; decodes exactly once; emits typed EndpointRequest/Resource into request extensions before dispatch; and emits only reason/offset/digest diagnostics. Explicit LIST/list=true alone permits root/trailing empty. Fixture test consumes U0.3 normalized Vault capture; percent/dot cases reject while emitted canonical cases pass. Generated property run covers 512 paths across data/metadata/delete/undelete/destroy/list with typed seed/corpus/minimized digest evidence and redacted cargo reproducer. Acceptance: cargo test --locked --test raw_target passed 8 tests; clippy -D warnings and all-target tests passed; ./scripts/verify.sh all passed all 13 local checks including MSRV/harness/canary; git diff --check and br dep cycles passed.

title: U3.2: identities (kind), grants, closed capability enum, structured matcher (R7/R22/KTD10)
description:
**Build:** Identity records, grant records, the closed capability enum, and the authorization matcher with structured decisions.

**Identities (R7):** every human, application, integration is a distinct identity; `kind` field (human | workload, extensible — schema carries it from day one so phase-3 AI agents land without a data migration; v0.1 attaches NO kind-specific enforcement, display/audit only). Persisted as KTD15 MAC-authenticated clear records. Wire first-management-identity creation into U1.2's init transaction — **together with its BOOTSTRAP GRANT: the admin bundle alias over all management capabilities, committed atomically with the identity and U4.1's credential verifier in init's single transaction.** Without the grant, the fail-closed model means the bootstrap credential authenticates but is denied everything — first boot could not run `identity create` or `grant add` (F1 bricked). Acceptance: fresh init → the bootstrap credential can execute `identity create` and `grant add`.
