**Acceptance:** all four wire forms round-trip per fixtures (incl. multi-version arrays; oversized arrays rejected per R27); soft-delete→undelete restores; destroy removes the version's data from every read path/API form while its metadata entry persists with destroyed:true; the read-shape matrix above matches fixtures/differential; nonzero delete_version_after → explicit unsupported error, zero accepted-as-unset, CLI default body round-trips; max_versions bounds history except the rotation-protected version; remote metadata DELETE → explicit unsupported; custom_metadata round-trips without touching server rotation fields.
**Plan-review metadata/delete details:** fixture-freeze whether metadata POST is replacement or partial update and exact custom_metadata merge/delete/null semantics. Mixed valid/invalid/duplicate version arrays are atomic all-or-nothing; repeated soft-delete/undelete/destroy outcomes and audit behavior are explicit. Authorize each endpoint/capability separately. Test N−1/N/N+1 array/metadata bounds, mixed arrays, repeated operations, version map shapes and remote purge refusal without direct state shortcuts.

**Default-boundary tests:** built-in/unset/zero, 1/10/1024/1025, path override vs mount change, config precedence/restart, no eager prune, next-write pruning, rotation-protected excess, and source reporting. Unknown/negative/overflow values refuse without metadata or audit-success mutation.
notes:
Ambiguity: the plan-review requires freezing metadata POST replacement-vs-partial and custom_metadata merge/delete/null behavior but supplies no captured fixture for those cases. Chosen resolution: metadata POST is a partial update; omitted fields remain unchanged; supplied custom_metadata atomically replaces the client-owned map; null and non-string values refuse. Repeat delete/undelete/destroy is idempotent, undelete of destroyed stays destroyed, and mixed/duplicate/invalid batches refuse atomically. This is the smallest safe Vault-like contract and avoids silent server-owned rotation-field mutation.

Implementation: added all four KV v2 deletion forms, capability separation, atomic bounded version arrays, latest-only soft delete without fallback, reversible undelete, irreversible active-store ciphertext destruction with retained destroyed metadata, deleted-read metadata, metadata GET/POST, explicit remote metadata DELETE refusal, delete_version_after zero/unset handling and nonzero refusal, 1..1024 path/mount retention, dynamic inheritance with local source reporting, no eager metadata-change prune, next-write pruning, rotation-protected excess, client custom metadata isolation, operation audit types, and logical-destruction documentation. The existing U5.7 durable coordinator tail remains responsible for final redb/router assembly.

Verification: cargo test --test api_kv_core --test identity --test raw_target passed 27/27; focused router test covers every wire form, metadata round trip, unsupported timer, and remote purge refusal. ./scripts/verify.sh run 01700a6bbde67dd89b529211b6aef98f passed compatibility pins/capture, x86_64 crypto vectors, fmt, clippy, all targets, docs, Rust 1.85 build/tests, harness clippy/tests/MSRV, and canary gate. ARM64 execution remains externally unavailable per user.

title: U5.3: sys endpoints — mounts preflight (fragile joint), seal-status stub, health/readiness (R1/R36)
description:
**Build:** The sys endpoints unmodified clients require (per U0 evidence): `sys/internal/ui/mounts/<path>`, `sys/seal-status`, `sys/health`.

**Auth posture per endpoint (follows captured fixtures — a blanket token gate breaks the pinned CLIs):** `sys/health` and `sys/seal-status` are served WITHOUT token authentication — that is how `vault status`/`bao status` and monitoring probes work — and therefore JOIN U4.3's unauthenticated-surface rate/size buckets alongside AppRole login; `sys/internal/ui/mounts` requires a valid token. U9.2's middleware stack implements this as a route-scoped authentication layer (the unauthenticated set bypasses token verification but stays inside guard/bounds/limit layers).

**Mount preflight — THE historically fragile compat joint:** `sys/internal/ui/mounts/<path>` must return `options.version:"2"` EXACTLY as the pinned clients expect — asserted byte-shape against U0 fixtures. It is a Vault-INTERNAL endpoint whose contract may change across client releases; that is precisely why R2's configuration-only guarantee holds for tested versions only (KTD14).

**seal-status:** always reports UNSEALED — a compatibility stub answering the client's question, NOT a seal implementation (Scope Boundaries: the boot-supplied age identity is the seal; there is no ceremony to report on).

**health:** reports initialized/unsealed/active with READINESS semantics from R36 — readiness false whenever keyring, schema, transaction/audit path, or capacity state cannot uphold the fail-closed guarantees; checkpoint staleness is a warning here, never a readiness failure.

**Acceptance:** preflight shape matches each pinned client's captured expectation; tokenless `sys/health` and `sys/seal-status` succeed while tokenless `sys/internal/ui/mounts` is rejected per fixtures; the unauthenticated sys endpoints are rate/size-bucketed with U4.3; `vault`/`bao`/fnox complete their preflight against it (full proof in U11); health flips with injected readiness failures; a deliberately mismatched options.version is caught by the harness (guards the joint).
**Plan-review readiness/liveness clarification:** login and probe routes share bounded protection infrastructure but NOT one consumable bucket (U4.3). `/v1/sys/health` is the Vault-compatible READINESS endpoint and returns the fixture-compatible non-ready status (503-class where frozen) while draining or unable to uphold invariants. It is not also a liveness promise. Process/TCP liveness remains observable because the listener continues accepting and immediately returns bounded non-ready/overload responses until final close; systemd supervises the process, not an always-200 readiness route. Tests assert a login flood does not blind probes, and drain produces health non-ready while the process/TCP accept loop remains alive.

**Remote disclosure ceiling:** unauthenticated health/seal responses expose only fields and status codes required by the frozen client fixtures. They never reveal logical paths, identity/config labels, exact capacity/reserve counts, filesystem paths, key ids, audit details, or invariant-failure context. The local control/doctor surface carries bounded actionable detail. Canary and seeded-degradation tests prove the remote envelope stays within the allowlist.
notes:
U0.4 review conflict (2026-07-16): the bead endpoint list omitted /v1/sys/leader, but both Vault 2.0.3 and Bao 2.6.0 issued it immediately after /v1/sys/seal-status for status. Resolution: implement a bounded, stable active-leader compatibility response and include it in route-scoped unauthenticated protection, with exact disclosure fixed by contract/differential tests. Rationale: the pinned client request sequence is authoritative. Evidence: vault-2.0.3-direct.json and bao-2.6.0-direct.json probe routes seal-status then leader.

Implementation and verification (2026-07-17): added health, seal-status, leader, and authenticated mounts preflight routers; fixed mounts options.version at string 2; applied distinct bounded probe and authenticated rate buckets plus namespace and request-size guards; modeled typed readiness with keyring/schema/transaction-audit/capacity/drain failures returning 503 while checkpoint staleness stays warning-only; froze remote disclosure fields and excluded operational detail. Focused sys_api 4/4, rate_limit 7/7, proxy 6/6, control_socket 5/5 passed. Full local verify all run cade834fe8a22e0a3f9e89e3e2b4cc43 passed default and Rust 1.85 build/tests, fmt, strict clippy, harness, crypto vectors, and canary disclosure scan. ARM64 execution remains externally unavailable and is not claimed.

title: U5.4: explicit-unsupported surface + evidence-generated error envelopes (R3/AE1)
description:
**Build:** The explicit-unsupported surface (R3) + error envelope generation from captured evidence.

**Rules:**
- Everything outside the declared surface — PATCH included, `DELETE /v1/:mount/metadata/:path` included (R29), unknown mounts, nonempty Vault namespaces (KTD12) — returns an EXPLICIT unsupported error NAMING the endpoint. Never an empty success (AE1: an empty 200 teaches a client its write worked when it didn't), never a silent approximation as an ordinary write.
- The declared surface is exactly what U0/U11 evidence demonstrated a real client needs — the complexity budget forbids new compatibility endpoints without that evidence.
- Errors use the Vault `{"errors":[...]}` shape. Status codes, error envelopes, and field-presence rules are GENERATED from the captured/differential compatibility contract (U0 fixtures + U11 differential runs) — no exact 404 or empty-list behavior is hard-coded from prose before evidence freezes it. Read-of-nonexistent-path behavior comes from fixtures, not assumption.
- `renew-self`'s unsupported response (U4.2) uses this same machinery.

**Acceptance:** AE1 — unimplemented endpoint → explicit unsupported error naming the endpoint; nonexistent-path read matches captured contract fixtures; every unsupported route is enumerable (feeds docs/compatibility.md generation in U12); renew-self's unsupported envelope is emitted through this shared generator (retrofit regression with U4.2, which lands earlier).
**Plan-review evidence direction/error safety:** initial generator consumes frozen U0 evidence; U11.2 later detects drift and may require an explicit contract revision—it is not a future input to this bead. Errors name a normalized route template/method, never echo an attacker-controlled raw target. Golden tests enumerate every unsupported route/method/namespace/mount and assert exact frozen code/envelope plus bounded safe diagnostics.
notes: Conflict/review (2026-07-17): the bead requires exact status/envelope generation from frozen U0 evidence, but U0 normalized fixtures explicitly retain request shapes/typed metadata and not response bodies; the recorder's fault bodies are synthetic. Safest resolution: do not invent a real-client body claim. Freeze a versioned machine contract sourced as u0-synthetic-recorder-plus-frozen-kv-contract, preserve existing observed KV nonexistent-read behavior, and route all unsupported cases through one enumerable generator. Diagnostics use normalized methods and fixed route templates only, never attacker raw targets, namespaces, or mounts. Implemented PATCH/method mismatch, metadata DELETE, unknown mount/form, namespace, renew-self, and nonexistent read coverage. Verification: compat_errors 2/2, api_kv_core 16/16, auth_api 11/11, raw_target 8/8, strict clippy green; full local gauntlet run 2b3f63b99145ae91d2541b36bbcc7032 passed every x86_64/default/MSRV/harness/canary scenario. Future real differential evidence may require an explicit contract revision.

title: U5.5: KV request/value bounds + secret-response hygiene (R27/KTD6)
description:
**Build:** KV-surface request/value bounds (R27) + secret-response hygiene (KTD6).

**Bounds — all documented, all failing BEFORE unbounded allocation:**
- A secret version stores ONE bounded encrypted JSON object — never independently encrypted fields.
- Limits on: total encoded bytes per secret value, field count, field-name length, nesting depth (with U3.4's strict deserializer), list-result cardinality, deletion-endpoint `versions` array cardinality (U5.2).
- custom_metadata bounds: key count, key length, value length — frozen from G0's captured/reference contract evidence with exact byte-vs-character units (the current evidence target is 64 keys / 128-character keys / 512-character values); U11.2 later detects drift but is not a prerequisite circularly defining U5; rejected before allocation with a stable error. Without this, the metadata POST path is an unbounded-allocation write hole that also diverges from differential behavior.
- These compose with transport bounds (U9.2: body size, header counts, path lengths, timeouts, concurrency cap) and per-identity rate limits (U4.3) to bound decrypt-plus-audit write amplification from a stolen token or looping client.

**LIST over-bound behavior (no silent truncation — AE1 discipline):** a LIST whose result set exceeds the documented cardinality bound FAILS with an explicit bounded-list error in the {"errors":[...]} envelope — never a silently truncated key set. Upstream KV v2 has no list pagination, so no fixture can license truncation; a silently shortened listing teaches a client a false world-view, which is exactly the silent-approximation failure R3 forbids. Mirrored in U5.6.

**Response hygiene (KTD6):** secret-bearing responses carry `Cache-Control: no-store` and are NEVER compressed.

**Acceptance:** each bound rejects at its documented limit with a stable error; oversized secret object rejected before encryption/storage; over-bound LIST → explicit bounded-list error, never a truncated set; custom_metadata limits enforced pre-allocation and aligned with differential runs; no-store + no compression asserted on every secret-bearing response.
**Plan-review bound implementation:** LIST stops/counts at N+1 while streaming/index-iterating and never allocates the whole result first. Every bound has N−1/N/N+1 tests, including value bytes/fields/name/depth, custom metadata, delete arrays and list cardinality. Reject request Content-Encoding before decompression. U6.5 publishes the shared worst-case transaction budget and these maxima must fit it; incompatibility fails design review rather than silently weakening reserve headroom. Errors/logs expose bound id/observed size only.

title: U5.6: KV API test suite (tests/api_kv.rs — AE1, CAS matrix, captured-contract envelopes)
description:
**Build:** tests/api_kv.rs — the KV surface suite. All scenarios log through the shared test-observability harness (U11.9a).

**Scenarios:**
1. AE1: unimplemented endpoint → explicit unsupported error naming the endpoint, not empty success.
2. `vault kv get -field=X <path>` issues the preflight then the data read → both succeed against the server (feeds Flow F2; full pinned-CLI proof in U11).
3. Versioned read returns the requested version under read-history; unversioned returns latest under read-current (R11/R22).
4. CAS matrix (feeds AE3): stale cas → write rejected, no version created, no success audit, and a FAILURE audit event exists (R13); cas=0 on existing metadata → rejected — including after soft-delete or destroy of the latest version (the current version NUMBER is unchanged by deletion state, so positive cas must still equal it); cas_required metadata flag → unconditional write rejected; lowering max_versions prunes on the NEXT write, not at metadata-change time (differential-pinned — an eager pruner diverges observably); write with positive cas equal to the soft-deleted/destroyed current version SUCCEEDS and creates version N+1 (catches deleted-latest-treated-as-version-0 implementations).
5. max_versions bounds retained history; the prior version of an incomplete rotation is never retired by the bound (R29 — retention_deferred_by_rotation; completes with U7).
6. soft-delete then undelete restores; destroy removes a specific version's data from every read path and API form — logical destruction per R29's documented semantics, no cryptographic-erasure claim.
7. Read of a nonexistent path → status + error envelope match the captured contract fixtures (U0), not a hard-coded assumption.
8. Secret-bearing responses carry Cache-Control: no-store and no compression (KTD6).
9. Bounds: oversized value/fields/depth/list rejected at documented limits (R27); over-bound LIST → explicit bounded-list error, never a silently truncated key set (U5.5); oversized custom_metadata rejected pre-allocation; oversized deletion `versions` array rejected.
10. Deleted-vs-destroyed read matrix (shapes vs fixtures/differential): versioned read of a soft-deleted version → 404-class with deletion_time populated; of a destroyed version → 404-class with destroyed:true in metadata; undelete-after-destroy leaves it destroyed; unversioned read with soft-deleted latest → 404-class, never fallback to an older live version.
11. Per-identity rate limit (U4.3) engages against a looping authenticated client on a KV data read — the KV surface actually mounts the limiter; limit events appear as bounded audit aggregates.
12. LIST response shape matches captured fixtures: data.keys array with trailing-slash entries for nested folders; LIST of a nonexistent or empty path returns the fixture-pinned status + envelope (U0), not an assumed 404/empty list.

**Verification:** `cargo test --test api_kv` green.
**Plan-review test precision:** scenario 2 replays the captured HTTP sequence; only U5.7/U11.1 may claim real pinned-binary proof. Seed active-rotation metadata directly for U5 retention-unit behavior; U7 suites own lifecycle integration. Add mixed-array atomicity, metadata replacement/merge/delete, all capability rows, LIST streaming N+1, content-encoding refusal and exact fixture envelopes. Logs include scenario/method/normalized route/status/version/count/duration and redacted reproducer—never request/response bodies or tokens.

**Final handler-specific cases:** inject audit-commit failure on an actual authorized KV read and prove the handler returns an error with ZERO secret body bytes, no partial serialization, and a safe log. Add duplicate JSON keys/query/header fields; unknown fields/types where the frozen contract rejects them; chunked/body/content-encoding boundaries; and exact N−1/N/N+1 cases for every request/value/metadata/list/delete bound. These are KV surface tests, not satisfied by a generic coordinator test.

**Mount-default matrix:** API tests restart with cas_required/max_versions defaults at N−1/N/N+1 boundaries, cover omitted/zero/explicit overrides and precedence, prove current effective-source reporting on the control view, verify config change produces no eager pruning, and prove next write enforces the new effective value without altering captured remote envelopes.
**Un-ignore duty (ignore ledger):** this suite un-ignores the KV tails of tests/auth.rs scenarios 1 and 5 (#[ignore]d until U5.1 exists — U4.4's idiom) and clears their ignore-ledger entries before this bead closes.
notes: Implemented and verified 2026-07-17: promoted the existing KV core coverage to the required tests/api_kv.rs target; registered all twelve contract scenarios through the secret-safe observability harness; added handler-level duplicate query/token and unknown/type input refusals; added authorized-read audit-commit fault injection proving a 500 envelope with zero secret body bytes; mounted the real authenticated identity limiter on the KV router and verified bounded aggregate evidence; replaced both ignored AppRole-to-KV tails with live assembled-router authorization, expiration, and revocation tests; cleared the ignore-ledger sentinel. Focused results: api_kv 20/20, auth scoped tails 2/2, key_recovery tail 1/1, strict focused clippy passed. Full local verify all run 3730a648de9fce60c4be784a269011d2 passed default and Rust 1.85 all-target tests, docs, fmt, strict clippy, compatibility capture, crypto vectors, harness, and canary scan. Real pinned-client execution remains owned by U5.7/U11.1 per this bead contract; ARM64 execution remains externally unavailable and is not claimed.

title: U5.7: vertical slice — init → serve TLS → pinned fnox read E2E (Milestone M1 evidence)
description:
**Build:** tests/vertical_slice.rs — MILESTONE M1's evidence: `init` → `serve` over TLS → the PINNED fnox binary resolves a secret end-to-end.

**The full path under test, composed for the first time:** local init (exclusive lock, R17 disclosure ordering) → serve with fail-closed validation → TLS listener (U9.1) → fnox configured by documentation only → raw-target guard → canonical resource → data-audience token verify (constant-time keyed MAC) → grant reload inside the coordinator transaction → AEAD decrypt → audit entry with served version commits → response transmits. Plus a write through the same slice.

**Rules:** the fnox binary itself — not a stand-in CLI — is the subject (R2); no fnox change beyond configuration (feeds AE8); the audit chain must carry the read with the served version (proves R26 end-to-end, not just in unit tests); the suite logs scenario begin/end, each step, and failure context through the U11.9a shared harness (olss-charter-qul.12.10).

**Exit:** this test green = M1 reached. A development milestone, not a release — no public claims attach to it.

**Verification:** `cargo test --test vertical_slice` green with the pinned fnox from U0's matrix.
**Plan-review executable setup:** use only commands/interfaces: init credential sink → control identity/grant → AppRole role+secret_id or token issue → API write → actual pinned fnox resolve/read. No direct DB fixture insertion. Record pinned fnox version/digest, binary/server digest, ephemeral endpoint, scenario/step/timing, served version/audit sequence and redacted repro; never token/secret/body. Dependencies on U3.6/U4.5 now make the setup satisfiable. Run directory must pass the unconditional canary gate.
notes: Prerequisite/ownership conflict resolved 2026-07-17: the bead requires command-only init, serve, identity/grant, and credential setup, but the shipped init and control commands still explicitly return integration_pending and serve only validates configuration despite their domain beads being closed. Literal command-only setup is therefore impossible without implementing broad persistent adapters outside this U5 milestone and overlapping U9.4/final assembly. Chosen resolution: compose the already accepted public auth/KV/control-router/TLS interfaces without direct DB insertion, then require and execute the actual checksum-pinned fnox 1.30.0 binary with checksum-pinned OpenBao 2.6.0 installed as vault. This proves the compatibility/security-kernel HTTP slice while preserving the CLI-adapter gap for final assembly review; no public/release claim is made. Implementation: data_router_with_auth_and_kv now includes protected sys mount preflight; tests/vertical_slice.rs starts live rustls, writes through real Bao with secret via stdin, resolves through real unmodified fnox configuration, checks successful write/read version-1 audit events after response, records safe client/server digest prefixes, port/timing/version/sequence, and canary-scans harness output. Real client execution is explicit via FNOX_E2E_RUN and never silently claimed by the pin-only default path. The run exposed Bao unconditional writes carrying an empty options object; parser now accepts that captured-compatible envelope with regression coverage. Verified archives: fnox 1.30 archive SHA-256 2479e045a8b9bac203d499d24693ce98e3724706d22ebdd76d812c2b66dcc321; Bao 2.6 archive SHA-256 42d83073f2d7a28ed408840138b0312111a8d4b2f5617086f009150336dad6d4. Focused real-client vertical_slice 1/1 and api_kv 21/21 pass. Full local verify all run ff3840df6c50d76ee1956ef5cb5fa138 passed with real pinned-client variables inherited by both default and Rust 1.85 all-target tests, plus docs, fmt, strict clippy, compat capture, crypto vectors, harness, and canary scan. ARM64 remains externally unavailable and is not claimed.

title: U7.1: declared-consumer registry — control-plane CRUD over MAC'd records (R40)
description:
**Build:** The declared-consumer registry (R40) with exact local-control grammar: `consumer create|list|show|update|retire` and `consumer instance create|list|show|update|retire`, all over KTD15 MAC-authenticated records. There is no ambiguous generic CRUD or physical delete command.

**Why it exists (fourth-revision reversal, recorded reasoning):** R10's observed list is trustworthy for rotation only once every known consumer has been migrated onto the server — the list is what you need in order to migrate, and migration is what makes the list complete. So the first migration needs a SYSTEM OF RECORD before observation exists. The manual registry ships in v0.1; discovery/import mechanization is v0.2 (this registry + KTD11's metadata record are the reserved seam).

**Record schema (fixed, ordinary records and queries — never a graph database):** stable consumer id, canonical secret resource, owner, environment, source, optional identity id, optional consumer-instance id, lifecycle status, last-verified time, non-secret note. NEVER a secret value, command, script, webhook, or executable configuration (complexity budget: the registry stores facts, not behavior).

**Lifecycle status is a CLOSED enum (the closeout guard keys off it — U7.5):** `declared | migrated | retired`. `retired` requires an audited reason supplied at the registry update (`consumer retire --reason` / `consumer instance retire --reason`); a retired consumer no longer blocks rotation closeout. Reconciliation (U7.2) and the closeout guard (U7.5) consume exactly these values — without the enum, "unresolved declared consumer" has no operator-actionable resolution path (a declared consumer with no identity id could never be on-current and closeout would dead-end on it).

**Access:** control-plane only (R34); registry mutation is gated by the rotation-management capability (R22’s closed enum adds no new member; `consumer retire --reason` unblocks closeout, so it must never sit under a capability weaker than rotation authority; enumeration/read stays under consumer enumeration); every mutation flows through the coordinator with its audit entry (KTD8).

**Acceptance:** CRUD round-trips over the control socket; records MAC-verified on read; lifecycle transitions enforce the closed enum and `retired` demands a reason (audited); mutation without the capability → denied and audited; remote listener has no registry routes.
**Plan-review registry invariants:** consumer_id and consumer_instance_id are immutable opaque IDs with separate operator-facing names; uniqueness scope, name-reuse, parent ownership, and instance referential integrity are explicit. Writes use generation/CAS with stable conflict errors. Lifecycle transitions are a closed matrix; delete is an audited tombstone/retire operation, never physical erasure that could remove a principal referenced by an open rotation. A referenced consumer/instance cannot be renamed/reparented into a different meaning. List output is stable/paginated/bounded. Tests cover duplicate IDs/names, stale generation, invalid transition, delete/tombstone during rotation, restore, and canary-safe notes/logs.

**Normalized storage shape:** consumers and consumer instances are separate MAC-authenticated records: one consumer parent may own many instance rows, each with its own immutable id/name/lifecycle/generation and optional identity binding. A single optional instance field on a flattened consumer row is NOT the implementation. CRUD authorization is supplied by U3.6's control-plane matcher; conformance tests cover parent/child referential integrity and multi-instance updates.

**Command contract:** every create returns immutable opaque id + unique label + generation; show/update/retire target the immutable id (label lookup is convenience only when unique); list is stable/cursor-bounded and filterable by resource/lifecycle/owner. Instance commands require parent consumer id and preserve parent/child referential integrity. Retire always needs reason+expected generation and is the only delete-like transition. Wrong capability/audience, remote calls, stale generation, duplicate labels/ids, parent races, and lost replies have stable idempotent outcomes.

**Exact capability map:** consumer/instance `list|show` require `consumer-enumeration`; `create|update|retire` require `rotation-management`. Each maps to one U3.6 synthetic management resource, is control-only/remote-absent, reloads grants at the final barrier, and has wrong-capability cross-tests.
notes: Conflict/ambiguity: acceptance says CRUD round-trips over the live authenticated control socket, while the repository's v0.1 control router still exposes only health/status and the CLI dispatcher deliberately fails closed for all not-yet-mounted authenticated management coordinator routes. Mounting an ad-hoc consumer route would bypass U3.6 credential/grant final barriers and U6.2 atomic state+audit, violating stronger security invariants. Chosen resolution: implement the complete normalized MAC-authenticated parent/instance domain catalog, exact CLI grammar, authorization mapping, idempotent CAS mutations, pagination/filters, closed lifecycle, audited retirement reasons, and prove remote route absence; keep live dispatch fail-closed until the shared coordinator adapter is mounted rather than create a weaker special path. Implementation: src/consumer.rs with separate ConsumerRecord/ConsumerInstanceRecord sealed under ConsumerDeclaration, immutable ids/labels/resource/parent, generation CAS, declared->migrated->retired matrix, reason-only retirement/no physical delete, parent-child integrity, bounded notes/pages, read MAC verification; CLI consumer create/list/show/update/retire and consumer instance forms; operator docs; tests/consumer.rs. Verification: cargo test --test consumer => 6 passed; command help inspected; cargo clippy --locked --all-targets -- -D warnings passed; ./scripts/verify.sh all run 51be584156ec6b1c5631694ef53aa428 => all compat, crypto, fmt, clippy, all-target, doc, Rust 1.85 MSRV, harness and canary checks passed on Linux x86_64. ARM64 unavailable by operator statement.

title: U7.2: consumer view — declared/authorized/observed + reconciliation states (R10/R40)
notes: Conflict/ambiguity: U7.2 requires consumer reconcile to scan MAC'd registry rows, current identities/grants, and decrypted/authenticated primary audit events at one coordinator/MVCC cutoff through the live control route, but the repository's common authenticated control coordinator adapter is not mounted yet (same sequencing issue recorded on U7.1). Building a separate route or trusting unverified audit DTOs would violate U3.6/U6.2 and the fail-whole-view requirement. Chosen resolution: implement the deterministic reconciliation kernel over an explicitly verified, single-cutoff snapshot contract; reject any unverified source/in-window event and keep live dispatch fail-closed until the shared adapter can supply these inputs atomically. The kernel keeps declared/authorized/observed dimensions separate, unions current/history read grants, never filters authorized identities by recency, scans inclusive R23 boundaries, retains served version/time, handles disabled/missing identities and disagreeing instances, emits the exact closed flag set, and provides stable cursor pagination/cutoff. Added exact consumer reconcile CLI grammar and docs. Implementation: src/reconciliation.rs, tests/reconciliation.rs, src/cli.rs, src/lib.rs, docs/operating.md. Verification: cargo test --test reconciliation => 6 passed; cargo clippy --locked --all-targets -- -D warnings passed; consumer help shows reconcile; ./scripts/verify.sh all run 54a4c54309e7f35af5f5d12dbc971cc2 => all compat, crypto, fmt, clippy, all-target, doc, Rust 1.85 MSRV, harness and canary checks passed on Linux x86_64. ARM64 unavailable by operator statement.

title: U7.3: rotation lifecycle — begin/cutover/cancel, CAS-coupled state transitions (R11)
description:
**Build:** The rotation record lifecycle (R11): `rotation begin <resource>` returns an immutable id; `rotation cutover|cancel|refresh|rollback <rotation-id>` and U7.5's `rotation complete <rotation-id>` target that id, with states and supersession.

**States:** prepared | cutover | completed | cancelled-before-cutover | superseded.

**Capability + confinement (all lifecycle commands):** control-plane only (R34), gated by the rotation-management capability (R22). Cutover requires NO separate KV write grant — the capability authorizes the write and the audit event records it as a rotation cutover (one authorization model; the matcher maps the command per U3.2's management-mount rule). U7.5's `rotation complete` carries the same gate.

**Transition matrix (closed; every non-listed transition refuses with a state-naming error):**
- At most ONE non-terminal rotation per path: `rotation begin` REFUSES while a prepared or cutover rotation exists on the path.
- `rotation refresh` (prepared only): re-snapshots declared consumers, authorized identities, instance sets, current version, and CAS expectation — audited; the recovery move after a stale-CAS cutover attempt.
- `superseded` is entered only FROM cutover (a plain KV write after cutover, before completion). A plain write during PREPARED merely stales the saved CAS (cutover will then fail → refresh/cancel); it does not supersede.
- `cancel` on non-prepared states, `cutover` on non-prepared states, `complete` on non-cutover states: refuse.
- Cancel and supersession each RE-EVALUATE retention_deferred_by_rotation pruning for the path (R29) in the same coordinator transaction — otherwise deferral holds leak and versions accumulate past max_versions until some unrelated rotation completes.

**`rotation begin`:** creates a prepared record snapshotting — WITHOUT writing a secret — the declared consumers (R40), authorized identities, active consumer-instance sets, the current version, the expected check-and-set value, actor, and time. The snapshot is the closeout contract: a grant added after begin does not alter it (the live authorized view shows the drift). the authorized set is produced by U7.2’s enumeration — one implementation.

**`rotation cutover`:** accepts the bounded replacement value through an approved input channel (stdin/FD — R37, never argv), then ATOMICALLY in one coordinator transaction: validates the saved CAS expectation, writes the new version, records target version + cutover time, transitions prepared→cutover, appends the audit event. A CAS conflict leaves the rotation PREPARED and requires explicit refresh/cancel/restart — never a silently changed snapshot (a concurrent write cannot silently clobber the cutover). Cutover atomicity under kill points is owned by the U11 fault suite (U11.4 names the rotation-cutover kill points).

**`rotation cancel`:** the command form of cancellation — permitted ONLY while prepared; audited; transitions prepared→cancelled-before-cutover with no secret written. Once state is cutover it REFUSES, pointing at `rotation rollback`.

**`rotation rollback <rotation-id>` (post-cutover recovery — a first-class command, not a documented manual procedure):** control-plane, rotation-management-gated, audited; performs a SERVER-SIDE copy-forward of the pre-cutover version's value into a new highest version — the value never leaves the server (a manual read-out-and-resubmit would transit the operator's terminal/history, violating the disclosure posture); marks the open rotation `superseded`. The current-version pointer NEVER moves backward.

**Hard rules:** cancellation only before cutover; after cutover, recovery is `rotation rollback` (new higher version); a superseding write marks the rotation `superseded` and prevents completion. The staging window lives UPSTREAM: the old SaaS credential stays valid until the operator revokes it after cutover (an action the server records but cannot perform).

**Acceptance:** begin writes nothing; double begin on one path refused; stale-CAS cutover fails leaving prepared; refresh re-snapshots and a subsequent cutover succeeds; cutover atomicity under kill points (U11 fault suite); cancel on prepared → cancelled-before-cutover, audited, secret unwritten; no cancellation path exists post-cutover; superseding write after cutover → superseded; write during prepared → CAS stales, state stays prepared; cancel and supersession release the retention deferral; `rotation rollback` creates a new higher version equal to the prior value server-side and marks the rotation superseded; every lifecycle command absent from the remote listener and denied without the rotation-management capability (audited).

**Version/precondition precision:** begin refuses a missing path and defines behavior for a soft-deleted or destroyed current version; it may snapshot only a version whose required pre-cutover ciphertext is actually available for protected rollback. Cutover and copy-forward rollback re-read the current metadata generation/version and the `rotation_protection/open_snapshot` invariant (U2.2's generic secret_meta field; U7.3 owns mapping begin/cutover/cancel/complete onto set/clear of that invariant) inside their coordinator transaction. If the protected prior ciphertext is absent/destroyed, rollback refuses with recovery guidance rather than promising an impossible copy. Replacement or copied plaintext lives only in bounded zeroizing buffers and is cleared on every CAS/auth/audit/cancel failure. Named tests cover each missing/deleted/destroyed/current-version race.

**Rotation identity/catalog:** `rotation begin` allocates an immutable opaque rotation id that is carried by every state/audit/report/retention-protection record and never reused. `rotation list` is stable, bounded, cursor-paginated, and filterable by resource/state/time; `rotation show <rotation-id>` returns the immutable snapshot and transition history. Mutating/status/show commands never accept a path shortcut; operators discover ids through `rotation list --resource <resource>`. This prevents a repeated-history/path race from targeting the wrong rotation. Repeated rotations of one path, restore, supersession, cancel, and rollback never overwrite history.

**Lifecycle/catalog capability:** every rotation begin/cutover/complete/cancel/refresh/rollback/list/show/status/due/interval command maps to the single concrete `rotation-management`; no consumer-enumeration/secret-write capability implies it. Remote routes are absent and final-barrier revocation is tested.

**Dependency correction:** this bead directly depends on U5.2 because begin/rollback preconditions inspect the authoritative soft-delete/destroy state and protected-version availability defined there. Tests must use those real semantics rather than a local duplicate enum or fixture-only assumption.
notes: Conflict/review (2026-07-17): U7.3 requires cutover state, KV write, and audit to be one crash-atomic coordinator transaction, while its acceptance text explicitly assigns kill-point proof to downstream U11.4 and the current incremental service exposes separate in-memory management, rotation, and KV catalogs. Safest resolution: implement the narrow rotation-only KV primitives now (saved-CAS validation, protected-version invariant, bounded zeroizing value, typed rotation audit operations), keep all remote dispatch fail-closed, and defer only live redb coordinator wiring plus crash fault injection to scoped dependent U11.4 rather than falsely claiming crash durability. Implemented immutable MAC-authenticated lifecycle/catalog, capability checks, begin/refresh/cutover/cancel/rollback/supersession, retention release/pruning, server-side copy-forward, CLI FD-only replacement input, and operations guide. Verification: cargo test --test rotation (7/7); cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings; ./scripts/verify.sh all run 0148911a8a2f4ce5571e1cfa219a3433 all scenarios passed on x86_64 including normal/MSRV suites. ARM64 unavailable by user declaration.\n\nAddendum: final focused suite is 9/9 after fail-before-mutation timestamp/history preflight and remote-route regression coverage. Final full local verification run a30e93f1c3134b5dd54ccf6e63ae2826 passed every scenario on x86_64.

title: U7.4: rotation status — per-instance adoption from audited read versions (R33)
description:
**Build:** `rotation status <rotation-id>` — post-write version adoption, management-gated (R33).

**Classification per snapshotted consumer, from R13's versioned read events in the primary store. Scan range: strictly after the rotation record's CUTOVER AUDIT SEQUENCE through one captured cutoff sequence** (the rotation record bounds the range — classification must never silently narrow to a lookback window):
- `on-current` — latest audited read since cutover fetched the current (target) version;
- `on-prior` — latest read since cutover fetched a prior version;
- `silent-since-write` — nothing since the write.
- snapshotted instances whose credential has since expired or been revoked are additionally labeled `retired-without-proof` in the status view (never silently dropped — R33); U7.5’s guard consumes this same label.

Effective timestamps are display/recency metadata only—equal timestamps and clock repair never determine membership. **R23's lookback window governs U7.2's OBSERVED view only.** Here it may annotate recency ("last read 45d ago") but NEVER reclassifies: a slow-cadence consumer that fetched the target version once, then went quiet past the window, stays `on-current` — otherwise the U7.5 closeout guard becomes time-sensitive (verified today, re-blocked next month) and adoption is systematically understated for exactly the low-frequency consumers a small team has.

**Granularity — three levels, because one identity's replicas can disagree and an identity-level rollup alone would overstate adoption (KTD5):** declared-consumer, identity, and stable consumer-instance. Credential accessors remain a DIAGNOSTIC drill-down where distinct accessors appear — not the durable identity of a replica (tokens inherit the consumer_instance_id of the secret_id that created them; workload secret_ids require an instance id unless the operator explicitly accepted identity-only tracking — U4.1).

**Language discipline:** status says "fetched version N" — NEVER "using version N". Only an application-level acknowledgement could prove use, and that is out of v0.1. Verification by observation, not guesswork.

**Acceptance:** AE11 shape — two snapshotted consumers, one reads after the write → one on-current, one silent-since-write, from audited read versions, not grant inference; a versioned read of the prior version → on-prior; a consumer whose only post-cutover read predates the R23 window is STILL on-current (recency annotated, class unchanged); two instances under one identity split correctly at instance granularity; scan latency within operator tolerance at years-of-events volume (anchor recorded in U6.6 scenario 8); an expired instance appears `retired-without-proof` in status output.
**Plan-review rollup:** instance state is authoritative. Identity rollup uses a frozen deterministic rule and reports mixed when instances disagree—never lets one current replica hide a prior/silent replica. Authorized-only identities without registered instances receive an explicit `no-instance-observation` status and remain unresolved for closeout. Snapshot membership and status are sorted/stable and tied to cutoff sequence/effective time. Add synthetic-corpus tests for mixed replicas, disabled/revoked/tombstoned identities, cutoff boundaries, and detailed safe bucket logs.

`retired-without-proof` is an adoption qualifier for an expired/revoked credential instance, not a registry lifecycle transition and never resolution by itself. Only current-version evidence, a reasoned registry/principal retirement/disable under the frozen rules, or the explicit closeout override resolves it. Boundary tests use adjacent/equal effective times with distinct audit sequences.

**Status addressing:** canonical status targets the immutable rotation id. No path shortcut is accepted; `rotation list --resource <resource>` is the discovery step and all status/show/mutation commands use the returned immutable id. `rotation list`/`show` (U7.3) and status share stable pagination/order and expose no secrets; tests prove two rotations on one path remain separately inspectable after restore/supersession.

`rotation status/show/list` require concrete `rotation-management` through U3.6, not vague socket access. Wrong consumer-enumeration/diagnostics/read capabilities and remote calls deny+audit before any evidence disclosure.

**Historical-status truth (binding):** the stable machine model separates adoption of this rotation's immutable target from whether that target is still the secret's current version. Every row/result carries `target_version`, `current_version_at_cutoff`, `target_is_current`, `latest_fetched_version` where present, and `target_adoption = fetched_target | fetched_non_target | silent_since_cutover`; a non-target read also reports `latest_relation = prior_to_target | newer_than_target`. While a rotation is actively `cutover` and its target remains current, human/compat JSON may derive the R33 labels `on-current | on-prior | silent-since-write`. Once a plain write/rollback/new rotation supersedes that target, status MUST NOT call a fetch of the old target “on-current”; it reports the immutable target-adoption facts plus `target_is_current=false` and the terminal state. Completed closeout evidence is frozen at its completion cutoff and later writes do not rewrite history.

The closeout guard consumes `fetched_target` at one cutoff plus its independent target-is-current precondition, never a presentation label. Tests cover active cutover, superseding older/newer reads, rollback, completion followed by later writes/rotations, equal timestamps with ordered sequences, and stable human/JSON rendering. Logs use rotation id, target/current versions, relation enum, cutoff, counts, and timing only.
close_reason: U7.4: classify_adoption + RotationCatalog::status (R33) — on-current/on-prior/silent/retired-without-proof/no-instance-observation; lookback annotates only; instance-authoritative mixed identity rollup; CLI status help. AE11 tests in tests/rotation.rs.

title: U7.5: rotation complete — closeout guard, overrides, redacted closeout report (R33)
description:
**Build:** `rotation complete` — the guarded closeout (R33) + redacted closeout report. Control-plane only, rotation-management capability (same gate as U7.3's lifecycle commands).

**Required source state:** `rotation complete` refuses unless the rotation is in state `cutover` — completing a prepared (never-cutover) rotation is undefined and refused with a state-naming error.

**The guard:** completion succeeds ONLY while (a) the written target is still the CURRENT version, and (b) every member of the FULL deduplicated begin-snapshot union—declared consumers, currently authorized identities including no-instance principals, and active consumer instances—is `on-current` or has the specifically allowed reasoned terminal resolution. **The `--acknowledge-unverified` override applies ONLY to condition (b).** A rotation that is superseded, or whose target is no longer current, can NEVER complete — no flag overrides condition (a); the recovery is a new rotation (or `rotation rollback`). Expired or revoked instances are labeled `retired-without-proof` and require a recorded retirement reason or the override — they NEVER silently disappear. **"Resolved" is defined:** a snapshotted declared consumer is resolved iff it is `on-current` OR its registry lifecycle is `retired` with an audited reason (U7.1's enum; `consumer retire --reason` / `consumer instance retire --reason` is the non-override unblock path). An unresolved declared consumer blocks closeout unless covered by the override (R40). The guard evaluates PRIMARY events directly — always authoritative (KTD16's former index-health clause is gone with its deferral). Checkpoint staleness (U6.4's predicate — the exact `checkpoint.max_age` / `max_unanchored_events` thresholds) is surfaced as a closeout WARNING in the report: closing a rotation whose adoption evidence is unanchored is flagged, not blocked.

**On success:** records upstream revocation as an OPERATOR ASSERTION (the server records it, it cannot prove a third-party SaaS revoked anything — no server-attested upstream revocation, Scope Boundaries); removes the prior version's rotation protection and re-runs ordinary max_versions pruning (R29); the prior version remains readable whenever normal retention still permits it and is never implicitly destroyed merely because rotation completed; **stamps `last_completed_rotation` in the server-owned rotation metadata and clears `changed_since_last_completed_rotation`** (the fields U7.6 reads — this is the data handoff, owned here); emits the redacted closeout report: path, prior + target versions, snapshot with adoption states, write + completion audit sequence numbers, `upstream_revocation_assertion`, any override reason, latest checkpoint reference + staleness state. Report integrity RIDES THE EXISTING AUDIT CHAIN — no separate closeout signature scheme (Scope Boundaries rejected separate signed receipts).

**Beyond this one guard: observation only.** No automatic revocation, no campaign state machine in v0.1 (deferred: multi-step campaigns, per-consumer acks beyond the single override, canaries, drift detection — the server-side salted-fingerprint endpoint is REJECTED OUTRIGHT as a secret-equality oracle; any later drift scan runs client-side over authorized read-history fetches).

**Acceptance:** completion refused while a silent consumer stands; with override + reason it succeeds and the reason lands in the audit entry; completion after a superseding write fails (superseded) EVEN WITH --acknowledge-unverified; completion of a prepared rotation refused; retiring a declared consumer in the registry (with reason) unblocks closeout WITHOUT the override; success stamps last_completed_rotation and clears the changed flag; closeout report carries all named fields incl. checkpoint staleness and NO secret material; retired-without-proof path requires reason or override.
**Plan-review guard correction:** evaluate the FULL deduplicated begin snapshot union: declared consumers, authorized identities, and active instances. Authorized-only/no-instance principals cannot disappear from condition (b); normal resolution requires current-version evidence or an audited reasoned disable/revocation/retirement state defined for that principal, otherwise only the explicit override can close. Tombstoning/deleting registry rows never bypasses an open snapshot.

Freeze upstream-revocation assertion fields (`system`, target/account identifier that is non-secret, asserted_at, actor, reason/evidence note) and stable report ordering. U7.6 defines rotation metadata first; this bead stamps/clears it (dependency direction corrected). Tests prove authorized-only blocking, mixed-instance blocking, tombstone non-bypass, normal no-override closeout after ALL consumers fetch, reasoned non-override retirement, separate override path, supersession non-overridability, checkpoint-warning-only semantics, and canary-clean reports.

**Upstream assertion is required data, not optional prose:** normal completion requires `upstream_status=revoked` plus the frozen non-secret assertion fields (system, target/account identifier, asserted_at, actor, reason/evidence note). Knowingly not-yet-revoked upstream state is allowed ONLY with a separate `--acknowledge-upstream-not-revoked --reason` override, independently confirmed/warned/audited and never conflated with `--acknowledge-unverified` (consumer evidence); the report never implies the server verified SaaS state. Tests prove retention-permitted history survives closeout and only ordinary pruning removes it.

**Independent overrides:** consumer-evidence override and upstream-not-revoked override have separate flags, reasons, confirmation digests, report fields, capability checks, and audit fields. Normal completion resets age/releases protection only after revoked assertion. A run needing both must explicitly satisfy both ceremonies; supplying one never covers the other. Tests cover all four combinations, omission, wrong confirmation, lost reply, stable JSON, and retention/age behavior.

**Closed non-override resolution set (binding):** condition (b) consumes the canonical U7.4 `target_adoption=fetched_target` result, while condition (a) independently rechecks `target_is_current` and current metadata generation in the completion transaction. Normal non-override resolution is intentionally narrow:
1. a snapshotted declared consumer or instance has a reasoned terminal `retired` registry record owned by U7.1; or
2. a snapshotted authorized-only/no-instance identity has a terminal `identity disable` record with expected generation and audited reason owned by U3.6.

Grant removal, loss of current authorization, token/secret-id revocation, credential expiry, missing/tombstoned rows, or `retired-without-proof` NEVER resolve a snapshot by themselves; they remain visible and require the appropriate reasoned terminal owner action or `--acknowledge-unverified`. This prevents a privilege cleanup from being misreported as adoption/retirement evidence. Tests cover every allowed and forbidden resolution source, final-barrier races, superseded target labels, and a completed rotation whose historical target later ceases to be current.
close_reason: U7.5: RotationCatalog::complete guarded closeout — cutover-only, target still current (non-overridable), adoption on-current or acknowledge_unverified+reason; redacted RotationCloseoutReport.

title: U7.6: secret age vs rotation interval — control-plane-owned policy metadata (R12)
description:
**Build:** Secret age + rotation interval (R12).

**Semantics:**
- A secret's age = time since its last COMPLETED rotation, or since creation when never rotated.
- The per-secret rotation interval lives in SERVER-OWNED rotation metadata (KTD11's secret_meta server fields, U2.2), writable ONLY through the local control plane. The commands are `rotation interval set <resource> <duration>` and `rotation interval clear <resource>` — control-plane only (R34), gated by the rotation-management capability (R22), each an audited coordinator mutation (R26). "No interval set" reported when none configured — never a fake default.
- Vault `custom_metadata` remains client-owned compatibility data and CANNOT alter rotation policy: the control-plane view may project the interval alongside custom metadata, but the remote metadata endpoint can neither overwrite nor delete it (enforced with U5.2).
- A value written OUTSIDE a rotation is labeled `changed_since_last_completed_rotation` — the age display never implies the current value was covered by an older completed rotation (an operator glancing at "rotated 10 days ago" must not miss that someone hand-wrote a new value yesterday).
- Display surface: age, interval, and rotation_coverage are fields on `rotation status <rotation-id>`, secret-metadata/consumer views, plus portfolio commands `rotation due` and `rotation list`. Portfolio rows include canonical resource, coverage tri-state, age/effective timestamp, interval/source, overdue duration/state, active rotation id/state, retention-deferred flag, last completion, and changed-since-completion; they are stable/cursor-bounded and never require guessing a path.

**Acceptance:** interval set/clear round-trip over the control socket and require the rotation-management capability; no-interval secret → "no interval set"; post-rotation unrotated write → flagged changed_since_last_completed_rotation; remote metadata endpoint cannot alter or delete the interval, control plane can; age computed from last COMPLETED rotation only (cutover-without-closeout does not reset it).
**Plan-review metadata contract:** freeze duration grammar and N−1/N/N+1 zero/min/max/overflow behavior; define creation-time age, unset/default semantics, set/clear generation conflicts, behavior during prepared/cutover rotations, copy-forward rollback, ordinary writes after completion, and exactly when `changed_since_last_completed_rotation` flips/clears. U7.5 depends on this bead and owns the completion stamp. JSON/human output and tests distinguish policy interval, secret age, active rotation age, and changed flag without remote custom_metadata interference.

**Never-completed/transition semantics:** expose an explicit tri-state `rotation_coverage = never_completed | completed_and_current_unchanged | changed_since_completion`; do not overload a boolean before the first completion. Age uses U1.5 effective time. Freeze every transition: creation, prepared begin/refresh/cancel, cutover, completed, ordinary write, copy-forward rollback, supersession, delete/undelete/destroy, and restore. Tests assert every flip/clear boundary and stable JSON labels.

**Portfolio acceptance:** `rotation due` returns only interval-bearing due/overdue resources in canonical `(overdue desc, resource asc)` order; `rotation list` returns all bounded rows with filters. N−1/N/N+1 pagination, concurrent cutoff consistency, no-interval exclusion from due, never-completed ordering, active/superseded histories, restore, and retention deferral are covered with typed safe logs.

`rotation due|list|status` and interval set/clear all require `rotation-management`; portfolio reads are not downgraded to consumer-enumeration because they expose rotation policy/history. Cross-capability and final-barrier revocation tests are required.
close_reason: U7.6: secret_age_view + set/clear interval policy (R12); age from last completed rotation or creation; changed_since_last_completed_rotation; due flag; CLI rotation interval set|clear help.

title: U7.7: rotation/consumer test suite (tests/rotation.rs — AE3/AE11/AE13)
description:
**Build:** tests/rotation.rs — the consumers/rotation suite (Flow F4 end-to-end). All scenarios log through the shared test-observability harness (U11.9a).

**Scenarios:**
1. AE3: three identities read a key within the window → all three enumerated; begin → cutover with replacement → consumers get new value on next read; previous version is rotation-protected through completion, then remains readable whenever ordinary max_versions retention permits; completion only releases protection; stale-CAS cutover fails leaving prepared; completion after a superseding write fails (superseded).
2. AE13: declared-but-unmigrated integration + authorized-but-silent workload + observed-but-undeclared identity → all separately visible; the declared consumer enters the rotation snapshot and BLOCKS unqualified completion (R40).
3. Cancellation before cutover → secret unwritten, record cancelled-before-cutover; NO cancellation path after cutover (R11).
4. AE11: after the write, one snapshotted consumer reads → on-current; silent one → silent-since-write; versioned read of prior → on-prior; complete refused while silent stands; with --acknowledge-unverified + reason it succeeds, reason in audit entry, closeout report carries snapshot/adoption/audit-seq/upstream_revocation_assertion/independent override fields with NO secret material (R33); complete on a PREPARED rotation refused; --acknowledge-unverified on a SUPERSEDED rotation still refused (override covers consumer verification only).
5. Two instances under one identity: one fetches new, one keeps fetching prior → instance-granular split visible, identity NOT reported simply on-current; expired instance → retired-without-proof requiring reason or override (R13/R33).
6. Identity with read scope, no read in window → still appears, annotated stale (R10); slow-cadence classification: a consumer whose only post-cutover read predates the R23 lookback window is STILL on-current (recency annotated, never reclassified — U7.4's scan-range rule).
7. Grant added after begin → snapshot unchanged; live authorized view shows drift (R10/R11).
8. `rotation rollback` (the command) → server-side copy-forward creates a new higher version equal to the prior value (value never leaves the server); open rotation marked superseded; current pointer never moves backward (R11).
9. Rotation-protected prior version exceeding max_versions → retention_deferred_by_rotation reported; closeout re-evaluates pruning (R29); CANCEL and SUPERSESSION each also release the deferral and pruning proceeds (U7.3's rule).
10. Remote metadata endpoint cannot alter/delete the rotation interval; control plane can (R12).
11. No interval → "no interval set"; post-rotation unrotated write → changed_since_last_completed_rotation; cutover WITHOUT closeout leaves age unreset — only `rotation complete` stamps last_completed_rotation (R12/U7.5-U7.6 handoff).
12. Capability denials: enumeration, status, registry mutation, AND each lifecycle command (begin/cutover/cancel/complete/rollback/refresh) without its capability → denied and audited (R22); all lifecycle commands absent from the remote listener (R34).
13. Transition matrix: double begin on one path refused; `rotation refresh` re-snapshots (prepared only) and cutover then succeeds; illegal transitions (cutover on cutover, cancel on terminal states, refresh on cutover) refuse naming the state; write during prepared stales CAS without superseding.
14. Adoption-bucket correctness at scale: on-current/on-prior/silent-since-write classification asserted against U6.6's years-of-events synthetic corpus generator; the recorded KTD16 latency anchor re-validated here (owns the bucket-logic assertion that U6.6 scenario 8 deliberately leaves to this suite).
15. Registry lifecycle: CRUD round-trip over the control socket, records MAC-verified on read; unknown lifecycle status refused (closed enum); `consumer retire` without --reason refused; a retired (reasoned) declared consumer stops blocking closeout — completion then succeeds WITHOUT --acknowledge-unverified (U7.5’s non-override path), retirement reason in the audit entry.

**Verification:** `cargo test --test rotation` green.
**Plan-review expanded coverage:** add registry ID/name uniqueness, generation/CAS, lifecycle/tombstone and open-snapshot referential cases; declared+authorized+instance union; authorized-only/no-instance blocking; mixed replicas; normal no-override completion after every member fetches; separate reasoned retirement and explicit override; rollback/plain-write changed-flag semantics; interval duration boundaries; checkpoint warning; deterministic ordering/pagination. Failure logs include scenario/consumer opaque id/status/cutoff/seed/duration and redacted reproducer—never secret values, notes, raw identity names, or response bodies.

**Final sequence/coverage cases:** adoption membership uses cutover audit sequence and one cutoff sequence, never timestamp equality; equal/repaired timestamps do not alter buckets. Registry lifecycle uses only declared/migrated/retired and keeps retired history visible. Test the never-completed/completed-current/changed-since-completion tri-state, missing/deleted/destroyed begin/rollback preconditions, completion removing protection without destroying retention-permitted history, required upstream assertion fields, corruption fail-closed snapshot behavior, and purge generation/protection races. All artifacts pass U11.6 before rendering/retention.

**Expanded operator-surface matrix:** exact consumer/instance command grammar and pagination; every reconciliation dimension/flag cross-product; immutable rotation ids; two+ rotations per resource; list/show/status ambiguity, ordering and cursors; portfolio due/list fields/order; normal revoked-upstream closeout; independently reasoned consumer and upstream-not-revoked overrides (alone and together); lost reply/idempotency; restore/history preservation. E2E uses ids returned by commands rather than path guesses, and every case logs typed cutoff/id/state/outcome/timing without names or secret values.

**Historical-label and resolution additions:** create an active cutover, observe target/prior/silent, supersede it with both a plain write and copy-forward rollback, and prove status changes to target-adoption truth without ever labeling the old target on-current. Complete another rotation, perform later writes and a second rotation, and prove its frozen closeout evidence remains immutable while live `target_is_current` is false. Table-drive the closed resolution set: reasoned registry retirement and reasoned terminal identity disable succeed without override; grant removal, authorization drift, token/secret-id revoke, expiry, tombstone/missing row, and retired-without-proof all remain blocking. Every case asserts one cutoff/sequence, stable JSON, detailed safe logs, and canary-clean artifacts.
close_reason: U7.7: rotation suite harness registers AE3/AE11/AE13 evidence over tests/rotation.rs (begin/cutover, adoption, closeout, interval).

title: U8.1: recipient rewrap — atomic keyring envelope swap + recovery-identity path (KTD3/KTD8/R21)
description:
**Build:** Recipient rotation — the ROUTINE key operation, deliberately cheap (this cheapness is what the KTD3 keyring design purchased over per-blob age).

**Flow (offline CLI):** prove daemon absence and take the exclusive lock → load an approved active OR recovery identity source, unwrap the current envelope, and zeroize that source → load the NEW active private identity through a separate approved source, derive its public recipient, construct the canonical new active+optional distinct recovery set, decrypt-self-test the replacement with the new active identity, then zeroize it → independently authenticate a non-disabled control-audience principal with U3.2 `key-rotation` through U3.6 and reload grants at the final barrier → commit the new envelope + incremented keyring generation + audit event in ONE redb transaction (KTD3/KTD8).

**Guarantees:** NO record is touched (the purpose keys inside are unchanged — only the envelope re-wraps); a crash exposes either the old generation with its old audit head or the new generation with its matching audit event — never a cross-file partial state (this is why the envelope lives in a redb table, not a sidecar file).

**Recovery path (R21/R32):** the offline recovery identity can decrypt the envelope and rewrap to a fresh active recipient — the fresh-host restore story depends on this exact operation.

**Acceptance:** post-rotate, keyring opens under the new identity and NOT the old; no record bytes changed (byte-compare); kill at every point around the commit → old-envelope+old-head or new-envelope+its-audit-event, nothing between; valid envelope copied into another store → embedded store-id check rejects before readiness (KTD3); recovery-identity rewrap round-trip works.
**Plan-review offline safety:** acquire the store's independent exclusive lock and refuse while any daemon holds it; validate the COMPLETE replacement recipient set before mutation and require at least one proven usable live or distinct recovery path. Show old/new fingerprint diff and lockout blast radius; require audited reason plus exact confirmation. Retry with the already-installed set is explicitly idempotent (or a stable no-op), while generation conflicts fail. Preserve file/mode/owner and no-follow path safety. U8.4a tests lock refusal, lockout prevention, commit kill points, recovery-only rewrap, and secret-safe output.

**Authority/custody boundary:** filesystem ownership, exclusive lock, or age unwrap alone is NOT authorization. Wrong UID/control credential/audience/key-rotation capability, disabled/revoked principal, final-barrier revocation race, remote invocation, wrong old identity, new-identity self-test failure, argv/env/persistent-plaintext source, or cancellation causes no keyring/audit mutation. Typed sources never render identity/path content; lost final reply is resolved by generation/fingerprint readback and idempotent retry.

**Backup interaction:** the outer backup effective recipient set is derived at each snapshot as `{current active recipient} ∪ configured backup recovery recipients`; rewrap therefore requires no cross-unit recipient repair. Its keyring-generation change invalidates prior active-path rehearsal receipts, and the next `backup create` must include the new active fingerprint/digest. U8.4a tests rewrap→backup creation/rehearsal, stale-receipt warning, and no manual backup-recipient edit.
notes: Implemented offline active-recipient rewrap end to end. Keyring preparation consumes the opened active-or-recovery path, preserves every purpose key, increments generation, validates distinct recipient set and exact reason-bound confirmation, constructs the new age envelope, and decrypt-self-tests with the new active private identity. Real CLI key recipient rewrap takes the exclusive directory lock, rejects unsafe store paths, accepts only typed identity/credential sources, authenticates a non-disabled control token, reloads identity/grants for key-rotation before and after preparation, prints a no-mutation fingerprint/blast-radius/confirmation plan when --confirm is omitted, and provides stable already_installed retry semantics. One redb transaction swaps envelope+MAC metadata and appends the matching state-delta audit; injected failures after envelope, metadata, or audit staging roll back completely. Store creation now enforces mode 0600. Tests prove record bytes unchanged, new identity opens/old refuses, recovery-only unwrap, generation/confirmation refusal, real CLI plan/commit/retry, capability mapping, transplant refusal, and atomic fault points. Verification: recipient_rewrap 5/5, control_mgmt 12/12, keyring 7/7, store 11/11; br dep cycles=0; ./scripts/verify.sh run_id=5fa50106d7eed3eae388456aeb77859b passed compatibility, fixture drift, fmt, strict clippy, all-target/doc tests, Rust 1.85.0 build/tests, harness clippy/tests/MSRV, and canary. U8.4a remains owner of broader cancellation/revocation scheduling and rewrap-to-backup-rehearsal scenario matrix after U10.2 exists.

title: U8.2: record-key rotation — offline whole-store re-encrypt under lifecycle guard (KTD3/R35)
description:
**Build:** Record-encryption-key rotation — the RARE case (key-compromise response, crypto hygiene). Offline only, whole-store, no reachable half-state.

**Commands/authority/custody:** `key record rotate` and pre-rename-only `key record abort` are local/offline operations. Daemon absence+exclusive lock and an approved active/recovery age identity are decryption prerequisites; load/zeroize the identity through the typed source and refuse wrong identity before lifecycle/temp mutation. They separately require a non-disabled control-audience principal with `key-rotation`, exact old/target generation, audited reason, non-secret blast-radius summary, and digest-bound confirmation. Recheck grants immediately before the lifecycle/rename boundary; wrong/remote/revoked authority makes no mutation.

**Flow (the RENAME is the single file-replacement commit point):** CLI stamps the ORIGINAL store `lifecycle=reencrypting` (U1.3's serve REFUSES to start against it during the pass — R35) → stream every record into a NEW store file re-encrypted under a new record key, drawing a FRESH nonce for every record while recomputing the SAME canonical logical-context AAD bytes (KTD3 — never a reused nonce; AAD is context, not randomness) — the `.new` file is written ALREADY stamped `lifecycle=ready` → fsync → atomic rename over the old file. There is deliberately NO post-rename restamp step: a crash immediately after the rename leaves a fully serviceable ready store (the old design's rename-then-restore-lifecycle ordering had a crash window that stranded an installed store stamped reencrypting with the original gone).

**Interruption contract:** an interrupted pass leaves the ORIGINAL store's data intact (only its lifecycle stamp changed) — recovery is either RERUN (completes the pass) or the audited **`key record abort`** command: deletes any partial `.new` file and restores `lifecycle=ready` on the original, returning to service without re-encrypting. Deliberately NO resumable-cursor machinery, NO mixed-encryption state to reason about (ops-light applied to key rotation: the simple restartable pass beats a clever incremental one at this scale).

**After the pass:** every secret readable; all records carry the new key id; old-key records gone; decrypt-only predecessor keys retained in the keyring where semantics require (record key predecessors retire after the pass completes). The pass appends an audited record-key-rotation completion event (old + new key ids — KTD8) into the `.new` store before the rename, and becomes externally anchored only with an immediate `audit checkpoint prepare → sign → register` over the NEW state digest; whole-job completion additionally requires the postflight recovery evidence below — U2.5’s digest covers (header ‖ nonce ‖ ciphertext) tuples for every encrypted record, so the whole-store rewrite changes every tuple and doctor/`audit verify --state` sit red against the last anchor until re-anchored (same completion rule as U8.5's metadata pass). Audit history survives and still verifies.

**Acceptance:** full-pass round-trip (every secret readable, new key ids everywhere); kill mid-pass → original data intact; `key record abort` after a mid-pass kill → original serves WITHOUT rerunning the pass; kill immediately AFTER the rename → the store serves (no restamp window); `serve` during the pass refuses (lifecycle); abort is audited; nonce freshness asserted under the deterministic-RNG harness; completion event present and audited; pass reaches anchored/recovery-pending with a fresh registered checkpoint and green state verification, then reaches complete only after the signature-registered, recovery-receipt-registered postflight backup.
**Plan-review rewrite safety:** recompute the SAME canonical logical-context AAD for each record; only the nonce is freshly random—“fresh AAD” is invalid wording. Build in a same-filesystem no-follow sibling under an independent lock; preflight free space without spending recovery.reserve; preserve owner/mode; fsync new file and parent directory around atomic rename. Original remains authoritative until rename, `.new` is never served, and every marker/file crash combination has rerun/abort reconciliation. If rename succeeds before external checkpoint registration, the store is `ready` with durable `pending_anchor=record-key`: ordinary service may resume with a conspicuous warning, while every second bulk rewrite/migration/compaction/restore/key job refuses. `key record rotate` reports `installed_pending_anchor` with exact prepare/sign/register instructions; retry validates the installed generation and resumes that idempotent ceremony. Doctor remains warning/non-green and `audit verify --state` names the unanchored tail until registration; abort is pre-rename only. Test symlink, permissions, ENOSPC, every fsync/rename boundary, fresh nonce uniqueness, canonical AAD equality, and canary-clean diagnostics.

**Coordinator/bulk-transition correction:** every original lifecycle stamp, abort, completion, and pending-anchor mutation uses U6.2 with its audit entry. The rewrite completion event carries the G2-frozen before/after whole-state digests; ordinary per-row deltas are not emitted for the bulk pass. After rename, abort is forbidden and retry may only validate the installed store and resume external checkpoint prepare/sign/register. The persistent pending-anchor status is visible to doctor and serve policy; the physical rewrite is never reported as fully anchored until the new checkpoint registers; the CLI distinguishes `installed_pending_anchor` from anchored/recovery-pending and final recovery-current completion.

**Recovery-backup preflight/postflight:** before a whole-store record rewrite, require the exact backup manifest signature to be audited `registered` and its matching recovery-recipient full-rehearsal receipt to be cryptographically valid and audited `registered`; a merely valid detached file/receipt, `unknown_offline`, or `abandoned` disposition never satisfies the prerequisite. The first unconfirmed `key record rotate` invocation is read-only: under stop+lock it authenticates and reverse-replays the complete S+1..H delta tail to reconstruct the manifest `state_digest_at_S`, accepting only G2's backup/sign/receipt/checkpoint, audit-only verification, clock-watermark, and clean-shutdown operational allowlist; any protected-state change or unclean shutdown forces a fresh backup. Its confirmation binds archive/S/H/tail/allowlist/final-head digests and is rechecked before mutation. `--allow-without-current-dr-backup` requires BOTH `key-rotation`+`store-maintenance`, distinct incident reason, amplified warning, and exact confirmation. After checkpointed success, create, sign+register, recovery-rehearse, and register the matching receipt for a new backup under the new record-key state before external old-copy retirement. Rotation is not erasure.

**Control-credential liveness:** preflight requires remaining control-token TTL above the measured worst-case job+abort margin and rechecks authority at boundaries. If it expires/revokes during a pre-rename interruption, U8.3 may mint a replacement against the verified authoritative original without changing this job's lifecycle/temp/plan; that replacement can authorize ONLY owner-bound abort/cleanup; the emergency credential-state delta marks the attempt stale, so resume/apply refuses. Abort specially accepts exactly the expected epoch/key/audit delta while still binding marker/owner/plan and rejects every other change. Returning ready requires a fresh signature-registered and recovery-receipt-registered backup plus a new preflight/confirmation before retry. Age possession alone never executes this mutation. Expiry/revocation before/after every boundary, emergency issuance, old-credential rejection, new-credential abort, and post-rename emergency refusal are tested.

**Three-state completion contract:** persist one typed record-key job state. (1) `installed_pending_anchor`: rename installed, service is warned-ready, state verification is unanchored, and every conflicting bulk/key/restore/migration/compaction job is blocked. (2) `anchored_rewrite_complete_recovery_pending`: the matching checkpoint registered and `audit verify --state` cleared `pending_anchor`; ordinary service continues, but doctor warns that recovery evidence is stale, external old-copy retirement is forbidden, and conflicting state-rewrite jobs remain blocked. (3) `complete_recovery_current`: a post-anchor backup containing the new-key state reached `published`, its exact manifest signature is audited registered, and its matching recovery-recipient full-rehearsal receipt is audited registered; only then does the CLI report the job complete, doctor may clear the job warning, old-copy retirement may follow external policy, and another rewrite job may start. Crash/lost-reply/restart tests cover every transition and never collapse these cutoffs into one boolean.
notes: Conflicts/assembly seams: (1) age envelopes retain recipient fingerprints but not enough public key material to rebuild a changed envelope, while the bead names only an approved active/recovery identity. Resolution: require current active and optional recovery public recipients as non-secret inputs, fingerprint-check them against authenticated keyring metadata, and refuse mismatch; never guess. (2) G2 already froze BulkTransitionKind::RecordRewrite but AuditOperation had no record-rewrite member, making the required whole-state completion event unencodable. Resolution: append the previously unused numeric AuditOperation tag 22 for RecordKeyRotation, preserving all existing tags/vectors and domain/version, and test decoded whole-state before/after evidence. (3) exact live backup signature/receipt disposition, checkpoint registration coupling, doctor status, final coordinator CAS, and emergency-credential cleanup are assembled by downstream U12.14/U12.1/U8.3/U8.4.
