Resolution: implement the complete offline kernel, durable rewrite_jobs state, exact three-state transition validators, canonical CLI surface, and keep production CLI apply/abort fail-closed at live_recovery_evidence_catalog_pending rather than trust caller-supplied disposition; existing backup.live_control_catalog tail owns atomic live binding. Implementation: evidence/head/generation/owner/reason/TTL-bound read-only plan and digest confirmation; final-barrier authority flags; independently audited atomic ready→reencrypting enter and reencrypting→ready abort deltas; source-head continuity validation; same-filesystem safe-path/capacity checks; every encrypted secret version decrypted and re-encrypted under a new record key with identical canonical AAD and fresh deterministic-harness nonces; predecessor record keys removed only after full rewrite; current recipient envelope and MAC metadata regenerated; ready target receives one whole-state RecordKeyRotation completion event, pending_anchor=record-key, and MAC-authenticated durable installed rewrite job before fsync/atomic rename; no post-rename restamp; persisted installed→anchored/recovery-pending→recovery-current validators bind owner, checkpoint/state, exact published backup/signature/recovery receipt and generations; backup archives now include reserved codec-20 rewrite_jobs while old archives remain decodable because the frozen codec stays optional; docs and typed-source CLI help landed. Verification: reencrypt 3/3 covers round-trip all versions, new key ids, nonce uniqueness, active/recovery unwrap, canary absence, audited enter/completion/abort, original intact, partial cleanup, ready-after-rename, durable job and all three states, evidence/dual-authority plan. Key CLI 4/4 and backup/store/format regressions pass. Full local ./scripts/verify.sh run bde67f68f1a494f0be649cb0ff22f9da passed compat pins/capture, lock/MSRV, x86_64 crypto vectors, fmt, strict clippy, all-target tests/docs, Rust 1.85 build/tests, harness clippy/tests/MSRV, and canary scan. No hosted CI ran. ARM64 execution remains externally unavailable per user.

title: U8.3: credential-epoch rotation + shared emergency-control primitive (R41)
description:
**Build:** the `credential epoch rotate --reason <...>` incident command (R41) and the ONE shared epoch-bump-plus-emergency-control-credential primitive reused by restore (U10.3) and offline clock repair (U1.5). Metadata-integrity rotation moved intact to U8.5 and audit-payload generation rotation to U8.6; recipient rewrap remains U8.1 and record-key re-encryption remains U8.2. This split prevents recovery from waiting on unrelated whole-store key jobs.

**Primitive:** validate an R17-approved output sink → generate the replacement control credential and new credential-verifier key where applicable → write+flush disclosure → in one coordinator transaction increment `current_credential_epoch`, install verifier metadata, persist its audit event/reason, and make the replacement verifier authoritative. Every prior token and secret_id is then rejected via U4.1's mandatory `issue_epoch == current_epoch` check (an epoch-only bump can leave an old verifier cryptographically valid; MAC failure additionally applies only where the verifier key also rotated). A sink failure or crash before commit leaves old epoch authoritative and any orphan disclosure unusable; a successful activation discloses exactly one usable replacement.

**Command posture:** two explicit modes, never remote. Online uses owner SO_PEERCRED + the combined U3.6 control capabilities and the typed sink handshake; offline uses daemon absence, exclusive lock, service-owner filesystem authority, approved age unwrap, reason+confirmation as frozen in Recovery principal/authority below. Do not imply one mode's control credential/lock rule applies to the other. Before commit, show bounded counts of outstanding token/secret_id records, explain that the caller's control credential dies, require exact confirmation plus audited reason, and expose stable human/JSON output without raw credentials in argv/logs. Revocation/epoch linearizes through the coordinator.

**Shared ownership:** restore and clock repair call this exported implementation, never copy it. Clock repair wiring unignores startup scenario 11. Integration/shared-symbol tests prove all three callers resolve to this primitive; any future epoch-bumping operation must use it or fail review. Restore may supply an already-generated target verifier context only through the typed API; semantics remain identical.

**Tests:** U8.4a lands early focused coverage: every credential kind invalidated, concurrency before/after barrier, R17 sink/crash matrix, wrong capability/reason/confirmation, offline/control confinement, blast-radius output, orphan recovery, restore/clock-repair reuse, stable JSON, canary-clean detailed logs. U8.4 re-runs this slice within the full key suite.

**Acceptance:** incident command ends every remotely usable credential atomically and leaves one working emergency control credential; no mixed epoch/key state is servable; restore and clock repair cannot lock out the operator; tests/startup.rs scenario 11 and named auth integration tails are unignored by their owners; no generic “rotate all keys” command exists—U8's per-purpose registry remains binding through U8.6.

**Typed online disclosure handshake:** offline callers write+flush the approved TTY/pipe/socket/anonymous-memory sink directly. Online control rotation sends the raw credential only over the authenticated owner socket to the CLI; the CLI writes+flushes its approved sink and ACKs a request nonce plus credential digest before the daemon commits epoch+verifier+audit. Connection loss before commit leaves an unusable orphan; loss after commit leaves a usable value already delivered. Persistent regular/block-device raw sinks are refused.

**Recovery principal/authority:** the replacement credential is control-audience only, has a frozen short bootstrap TTL, and atomically targets a system-generated recovery principal carrying the versioned admin bundle needed for identity/grant and credential recovery—even if prior human admins were disabled. Online invocation therefore requires key-rotation PLUS identity+grant-management and credential-issue authority; a narrow key operator cannot silently become admin. For a healthy on-host store, offline epoch/clock emergency invocation requires service-owner filesystem authority, daemon absence + exclusive lock, exact reason/confirmation, and ANY approved age identity that successfully unwraps the CURRENT keyring—either the active identity or an enrolled recovery identity. The recovery recipient is optional, so active-only stores remain recoverable; a wrong identity refuses before mutation/disclosure. Archive restore is different: it uses a matching recovery-recipient identity from the backup recipient set and the restore ceremony. Tests execute the recovery commands, data-plane audience denial, TTL expiry, disabled-admin stores, cross-capability escalation attempts, active-only and recovery-only healthy-store emergency access, wrong identity, absent optional recovery recipient, lock races, and archive-recovery identity separation.

**Interrupted-job control recovery:** the offline emergency primitive may open a verified authoritative ORIGINAL stamped `reencrypting|migrating|compacting` only under its authenticated owned marker/plan and exclusive lock. With an approved unwrap identity plus reason/confirmation, it epoch-rotates/mints a finite replacement control credential but does NOT perform/resume/abort/rename the maintenance/key mutation or change its PHYSICAL lifecycle/temp. The necessary credential-epoch/key/audit mutation atomically marks the attempt `auth_recovery_stale`; the replacement credential may authorize only the owner-bound abort/cleanup. Resume/apply refuses. After abort returns the source ready, the operator must take a fresh signed+recovery-rehearsed backup and new preflight/plan before retry. Foreign/ambiguous/post-rename states refuse.
notes: Review required. Ambiguity 1: R41 says generate a new credential-verifier key where applicable, while recovery-only access may know only the enrolled recovery identity and the live keyring stores recipient fingerprints rather than reconstructible active public recipients. Rewrapping a verifier-rotated keyring from recovery-only authority would either drop the active recipient or invent recipient material. Safest resolution: this incident primitive performs an epoch-only invalidation under the existing verifier. U4.1 mandatory issue_epoch equality atomically rejects every old token and secret_id; tests prove both kinds die, while the one replacement is finite and control-audience only. No cryptographic-verifier-rotation claim is made. Ambiguity 2: online owner-socket handlers and a persisted live coordinator adapter do not yet exist (the router exposes health/status only), and clock repair must add its meta mutation to the same transaction. Resolution: exported one shared prepared/commit type plus incident, clock, and restore entry points; implemented real offline CLI, online nonce/digest ACK kernel, atomic redb epoch+recovery-identity+admin-grant+credential+audit commit, and routed live socket/clock/interrupted-marker assembly to scoped final-integration bead olss-charter-qul.12.14 in the integration-tail ledger rather than adding an unauthenticated bypass. ARM64 execution unavailable per user; x86_64 evidence only. Verification: ./scripts/verify.sh run 20aa0a72efd0bb1902ab5a128647f749 passed compatibility, frozen vectors, fmt, strict clippy, all-target tests, docs, Rust 1.85 build/tests, harness clippy/tests/MSRV, and canary on Linux x86_64. Focused results: 5 credential-epoch domain/store tests and 2 real CLI/help tests passed; br dep cycles returned zero.

title: U8.4: full key-rotation suite — recovery slice + record/metadata/audit key jobs
description:
**Build:** tests/reencrypt.rs — the key-rotation suite. Execution note: test the INTERRUPTION path explicitly — the original store must remain intact and no mixed-key state may ever be servable. All scenarios log through the shared test-observability harness (U11.9a).

**Scenarios:**
1. Rotate recipient → keyring opens under the new identity, not the old; no record bytes changed.
2. Kill at every point around the recipient-rewrap commit → after restart, either old envelope + old audit head, or new envelope + its matching audit event (KTD8).
3. Valid keyring envelope copied into another store → embedded store-id validation rejects it before readiness (KTD3).
4. Recover the keyring with the offline recovery identity and rewrap to a fresh recipient (R21/R32 recovery path).
5. Rotate record key → every secret readable; all records carry the new key id; old-key records gone; completion audit event present (old/new key ids); checkpoint prepare→sign→register makes state verification green, then the job remains recovery-pending until a newly published/signature-registered/recovery-receipt-registered backup advances it to recovery-current (mirror of scenario 9’s anchoring rule).
6. Interruption matrix for the record-key pass (U8.2's rename-commit flow): kill mid-pass → original data intact, `serve` against it refuses (lifecycle), RERUN completes; kill mid-pass then `key record abort` → partial .new deleted, original restored to ready and SERVES without re-encrypting; kill immediately AFTER the atomic rename → the new store serves (no restamp window); abort is audited.
7. Audit history survives BOTH rotations and still verifies (chain + checkpoints).
8. `credential epoch rotate` → all pre-rotate tokens/secret_ids rejected; emergency control credential authenticates; audit records the reason.
9. Rotate metadata-integrity MAC key (offline rewrite, U8.5) → every clear record re-MACs under the new key and U6.4's full-state verification passes; the pass ends with a fresh checkpoint prepare → sign → register anchoring the new state digest (U8.5's rule — `audit verify --state` green against the NEW anchor); same interruption matrix as scenario 6 (kill mid-pass → rerun or `key metadata abort`; kill after rename → serves); `serve` during the pass refuses (R39/R35) and the refusal names the metadata re-MAC operation (progress record).
10. Rotate audit-payload key (forward-only, U8.6) → events appended after the rotation encrypt under the new key id; historical events remain decryptable via `audit query` under the retained decrypt-only key; prior event ciphertext bytes unchanged (byte-compare); chain + registered checkpoints still verify (KTD4).

**Verification:** `cargo test --test reencrypt` green.
**Plan-review suite split/safety:** U8.4a owns and lands recipient/epoch scenarios early; this full suite depends on it and re-runs them, then adds record-key, metadata re-MAC, and audit-payload cases. Scenario 9 is owned by U8.5 and scenario 10 by U8.6 (neither is owned by U8.3). Add exclusive-lock/daemon-running refusal, recipient lockout prevention, blast-radius confirmation, offline/remote confinement, restore+clock-repair shared-primitive proof, symlink/ENOSPC/mode/fsync boundaries, post-rename/pre-checkpoint recovery, and artifact safety. Whole-store jobs log record counts/key ids/generations/point/timing only—never plaintext, keys, AAD containing paths, or raw credentials.

**Verification command contract:** this bead runs both `cargo test --test key_recovery` and `cargo test --test reencrypt`; claiming the recovery slice was “re-run” from the latter binary is invalid. Scenario 9 consumes U8.5 and scenario 10 consumes U8.6. Bulk rewrite cases assert before/after state-digest transitions and pending-anchor recovery; audit-key cases assert one coordinator generation boundary with unchanged historical bytes.

**Pending-anchor/limit additions:** scenarios 5 and 9 assert warned ready service, exact conflicting-job refusals, diagnostics/incident availability, idempotent checkpoint completion, abort-only-before-rename, doctor finding, and restart/lost-reply behavior. Scenario 10 freezes transition-event-first-under-new-generation and tests audit key counts 23/24/31/32/33, encoded-size limit, U6.5 reserve/capacity preflight, concurrent final-slot rotation, RNG-before-limit refusal, and historical verification.

**Completion-state matrix:** for record and metadata jobs, assert exact persisted/result/doctor behavior for `installed_pending_anchor`, `anchored_rewrite_complete_recovery_pending`, and `complete_recovery_current`. Inject checkpoint success with backup failure, publication pending, valid-but-unregistered signature/receipt, recipient-generation change, lost registration reply, restart, attempted conflicting job, ordinary reads/writes, and final idempotent completion. Old-copy retirement is never claimed before state 3.
close_reason: U8.4: key_rotation_suite registers recipient rewrap, epoch, reencrypt, metadata integrity, audit-payload, key_recovery producers.

title: U10.1: logical backup format + recovery-epoch rules — design & freeze (Gate G3, KTD17)
description:
**Design-and-freeze bead — Gate G3.** Design the logical backup format + restore/audit-epoch rules (KTD17), review, FREEZE. One of M0's four gates: the backup format is persistent format, so it freezes with the others. (Gate G2 depends on this bead for the recovery-family event types — the audit schema must freeze WITH them, not before.)

**Format principles (KTD17):**
- Owned by the APPLICATION and versioned INDEPENDENTLY from redb — one consistent read transaction (redb MVCC reads run beside the writer, proven by U2.1's spike) produces versioned canonical frames. NEVER a copy of the live redb file as the normative format. This keeps release-fixture restores stable across redb file-format changes (R35/R38).
- Frame set (R32): versioned canonical frames per table; encrypted record bytes UNCHANGED; authenticated clear-record bytes; the current keyring envelope; a recovery keyring rewrapped to configured backup recipients (their non-secret fingerprints recorded); ALL audit events; per-table counts and digests; a canonical manifest (store id, schema version, keyring generation, U2.5 non-audit state digest, chain head, latest included checkpoint, checksums) plus a DETACHED signature produced via KTD4's external flow over the domain-separated manifest+content digest.
- **Manifest signing is a TWO-STEP ceremony mirroring checkpoint prepare/sign/register (the daemon never holds the signing key — KTD4), which means an UNATTENDED nightly `backup create` publishes one container in unsigned-signature state; its encrypted payload contains the checksummed canonical recovery manifest.** The format defines that state explicitly; no unsigned-manifest sidecar exists; `backup manifest sign` (operator/timer, offline signing key) completes the signature like a checkpoint. Restore policy for unsigned-but-checksummed archives: absence refuses unless `--allow-unsigned-manifest`, an audited non-secret `--reason`, and exact digest-bound confirmation are all supplied; the accepted override emits a high-severity warning and doctor reports it. A present invalid/mismatched signature can NEVER be overridden. Restore verifies signer key id/domain against the operator-supplied authenticated checkpoint public trust-lineage bundle (root plus U6.8 transitions) from the recovery package—NEVER a key or lineage trusted only because it is carried inside the archive (an attacker who can rewrite the archive could rewrite an embedded key).
- The archive NEVER contains a private age identity or the checkpoint signing key. The documented recovery package = archive + offline recipient identities + signing-key custody where applicable + the authenticated checkpoint/manifest PUBLIC trust-lineage bundle for offline verification; a store backup without them is NOT a recovery, and the docs must say so plainly.

**Epoch rules (KTD17/R32):**
- Every restore increments `current_credential_epoch` (pre-restore bearer credentials die cryptographically).
- Every restore mints and discloses a replacement emergency CONTROL credential as part of activation (R41's primitive, owned by U8.3, R17 sink-validated disclosure ordering). This is a FROZEN rule, not an implementation nicety: the epoch bump kills every pre-restore credential including control ones, and no bootstrap route exists on any listener — without this rule a restored store is unauthenticatable by design.
- A normal restore continues the existing audit epoch only when its archived head/trust generation is consistent with ALL supplied authenticated recovery evidence: it is not behind a supplied checkpoint from the canonical source incarnation and no supplied effective signing-trust transition postdates the archive's lineage/head. Out-of-band lineage may verify the archive signature, but it never silently mutates the trust registry restored from the archive.
- A ROLLBACK recovery (restoring behind the newest checkpoint OR effective signing transition IN THE SUPPLIED SET — see U10.4 for the honest completeness limit) requires explicit `--start-recovery-epoch` + reason; increments credential AND audit epochs; and opens the new epoch with a fork genesis recording restored head, missing anchored range, supplied checkpoint-set digest, imported-lineage digest/current signer where present, archive digest, source-final-tail/RPO assertion digest, actor, and reason. Verification forever reports the historical anchored epoch and recovery fork SEPARATELY—never a continuous chain.

**Exit:** format document + frame/manifest schema committed (both manifest states + signing ceremony + restore policy included); recovery-family event types handed to G2; reviewed; frozen. Later changes are R35 forward-only migrations with upgrade notes. Feeds M0; blocks U10 implementation beads.
**Plan-review format precision:** archive content and canonical manifest are immutable once backup closes. `backup manifest sign` produces/registers a DETACHED signature over a domain-separated digest of the canonical manifest+content digest; it never rewrites archive payload or changes the backup/rehearsal identity. Freeze duplicate-frame rejection, canonical member/table order, exact length/count maxima, unknown-version policy, no trailing bytes, no paths/symlinks, and strict manifest/signature framing. Golden/negative vectors cover unsigned, signed, duplicate, reordered, truncated, oversized and signature-confusion cases.

**Authoritative archive registry (binding):** G3 freezes one table/domain/codec registry with stable ids, codec versions, required/optional cardinality, ordering, and owner bead. Backup enumerates this registry—not a hand-maintained table list—and a coverage test fails whenever a durable table/domain from U2/U3/U4/U6/U7/U8 exists without an archive codec/restore verifier. Unknown/duplicate/omitted required table frames fail closed.

**Detached-signature invariant:** archive content and canonical manifest close immutably in the unsigned/checksummed state. Signing stores/registers a detached signature over the frozen manifest+content digest and never rewrites an archive member or manifest bytes. Golden fixtures and docs use this terminology consistently.

**Recipient vocabulary/policy (G3-frozen):** the active recipient is the current daemon-keyring recipient; a recovery recipient is a distinct off-host recovery path; the configured **backup recovery-recipient set** contains 1..7 distinct off-host recovery recipients. At each backup snapshot, the effective outer set is derived—not separately stored—as `{current keyring active recipient} ∪ configured recovery set`, maximum 8, with canonical duplicate/equality rejection. Backup callers cannot override it per invocation. `backup recipient list|set` manages only the recovery set and requires `backup-recipient-manage`, generation/CAS, reason, blast-radius summary, and digest-bound confirmation. Manifest/receipts bind keyring generation, recovery-set generation, and effective-set digest. Active rewrap automatically changes the next effective set and invalidates prior active-path receipts; recovery-set changes invalidate affected DR receipts.

**Snapshot state commitment:** the canonical manifest includes U2.5/U6.4's full non-audit logical `state_digest`, computed inside the SAME consistent backup read snapshot as table counts/digests and bound into the detached manifest signature. Audit/checkpoint/descriptor tables remain excluded exactly as in the frozen state-digest algorithm; the manifest separately binds cutoff sequence/head/checkpoint. This field is the authoritative freshness comparator for U8.2/U8.5/U10.7/U10.8.

**Manifest signer eligibility:** backup close freezes creator audit epoch/sequence/head, U6.8 trust-lineage generation+transition digest, key id current at close, and backup domain. `backup manifest sign` must sign AND authenticate/register while that key remains current. Rollover refuses unless the manifest is registered or audited abandoned; an unsigned pre-transition backup cannot be newly signed after rollover and must be recreated or retained explicitly unsigned. Restore verifies historical signed artifacts through lineage, reports registration separately, and states that Ed25519 alone provides no trusted production time.

**Unsigned-manifest abandonment:** `backup manifest abandon <manifest-digest>` is local control, requires `backup`, current registry generation, non-secret reason and exact digest confirmation, and atomically records idempotent abandoned status. It never deletes archive bytes or converts the archive to signed; restore may still use the explicit unsigned override. Abandon merely permits signing-trust rollover and remains doctor/list visible.

**Offline registration policy:** a cryptographically valid detached manifest signature is sufficient for default offline restore even when registration proof is unavailable, but output says `registration_status=unknown_offline`. Registration remains required operationally for doctor/timers/rollover. If the recovery package includes the complete authenticated audit-chain segment from artifact cutoff through the registration/abandonment event to the covering external checkpoint head, with needed audit-payload generations to verify/decrypt the exact disposition binding, offline verify reports that authenticated disposition. Abandonment does not cryptographically revoke an already-created detached signature; without disposition proof an offline verifier cannot know it.

**Single-container publication unit (G3-frozen):** `backup create` publishes exactly ONE mode-0600 versioned container, never an archive plus independently renamed unsigned-manifest sidecar and never a bundle directory. Its bounded clear canonical `signing_header` contains only the framing/version, opaque archive/store-incarnation identifiers, signer key id/domain and lineage generation, recipient-set generations/fingerprints, encrypted-payload length+digest, and the digest of the full recovery manifest. One age-encrypted payload contains that canonical recovery manifest plus every table frame. Build payload first, hash it, then encode the header: there is no cyclic digest. Header and encrypted payload close immutably together; only the later signature is detached. `backup manifest sign` signs the domain-separated header+encrypted-payload digest without needing an age identity. Restore can enforce framing/limits, hash the ciphertext, and verify the detached signature/trust lineage BEFORE age decryption; after decrypt it verifies the recovery-manifest digest and every member checksum/state relation. Existing prose saying archive plus unsigned manifest means this single container in unsigned-signature state, not two publication units. Golden/fault fixtures reject header/payload swaps, manifest-digest mismatch, appended/trailing bytes, recipient-header confusion, a stray sidecar, and signature-before-decrypt order violations.

**Trust-aware restore/fork rule (G3-frozen):** a backup contains the public trust registry exactly as of snapshot S. A normal restore installs exactly that registry and its current signer; a recovery package's newer out-of-band lineage is verification evidence, not an in-place update. Therefore after A→B rollover, a normal restore intended to produce a B-signed first checkpoint uses a fresh B-created, B-signed, signature-registered, recovery-receipt-registered backup whose payload already contains B. Supplying an authenticated effective A→B transition newer than an older A archive is itself rollback evidence even when newer checkpoints are withheld. Only `--start-recovery-epoch` may import the complete verified root→B public lineage/current descriptor into the new fork; fork genesis binds its canonical digest/effective positions and labels it imported recovery trust. B then signs the first new-epoch checkpoint without any A private key. Missing, forked, replayed, unbounded, wrong-store/domain, or internally inconsistent lineage refuses before disclosure/install.

**Source-observation/RPO binding:** every activation assertion includes `claimed_decommissioned`, archive cutoff epoch/sequence/head, and `source_observation_status=barrier_confirmed|last_known|unavailable`. `barrier_confirmed` binds the exact final source incarnation/epoch/sequence/head after clean stop plus executor/store-lock barriers. `last_known` binds an optional observed tuple, observation time, and provenance but is explicitly non-final; `unavailable` may have no post-archive tuple. The assertion also binds any supplied archive→observed tail range/digest and `complete|partial|unavailable` status, plus `rpo_status=known_range|unknown` and explicit non-secret acknowledgment. A complete tail must descend from the archive head and is classified for later checkpoint/trust evidence. Activation/fork preserves these fields so verify explains known dropped events or unknown RPO honestly. Only `barrier_confirmed` permits/requires a final head recheck and makes a post-assertion append stale; last-known/unavailable remain operator claims and never masquerade as final. A supplied later checkpoint or effective lineage still forces fork semantics.
notes: Implemented and froze application-owned backup format v1. Added authoritative 24-entry archive registry with coverage against every current durable table; canonical frame/manifest/container codecs and strict bounds/order/duplicate/unknown/trailing-byte rejection; single mode-0600 publication-unit contract; detached domain-separated Ed25519 signature framing; 1..7 recovery-recipient/effective-set rules; unsigned override and invalid-signature refusal policy; recovery-family event IDs; trust-aware normal-versus-fork classification; credential/audit epoch activation plan; source-observation/RPO commitments; normative docs and golden/negative fixtures. Verification: ./scripts/verify.sh run_id=b5d177c354708c447201c666261ebe2c passed compatibility pins/capture, lock/MSRV policy, crypto vectors, fmt, strict clippy, all-target tests, Rust 1.85.0 build/tests, harness clippy/tests/MSRV tests, and canary gate on Linux x86_64. Focused backup_format tests: 6 passed. br dep cycles: 0.

title: U10.2: backup — consistent logical archive + recovery keyring rewrap + detached signature (R32)
description:
**Build:** `backup create` — produce KTD17's logical archive from ONE consistent redb read transaction while the server runs (R32); plus `backup manifest sign`—one CLI ceremony with two explicit phases. The local short-lived phase loads the offline signing key through an approved source, signs the immutable backup-domain digest, atomically writes/fsyncs a DETACHED signature, and zeroizes the key. The register phase uses service-owner SO_PEERCRED plus a non-disabled control-audience credential with `backup`, reloads grants at the reserved-lane final barrier, and atomically audits/registers that exact signature digest so doctor can report unsigned/unregistered manifests. If the daemon is unavailable or registration reply is lost, the detached file remains safe; retry verifies the identical archive/signature and registers idempotently without re-signing.

**Contents per the frozen G3 format:** versioned canonical table frames; encrypted record bytes unchanged; authenticated clear-record bytes; current keyring envelope; recovery keyring rewrapped to the G3 effective backup set derived atomically for the snapshot from the current active recipient plus the configured 1..7 recovery-recipient set with their non-secret fingerprints recorded in the manifest; all audit events; per-table counts + digests; the immutable canonical manifest (store id, schema version, keyring generation, U2.5 non-audit state digest, chain head, latest included checkpoint, checksums). The signature is NEVER embedded: it is a separate detached output and, after authenticated registration, a digest-addressed registry record.

**Why the recipient rewrap matters:** a fresh-host restore can use a recovery-recipient identity from the outer backup set and rewrap the recovered keyring to a NEW active recipient before activation—it does NOT require the failed host's active identity. That is the difference between a backup and a recovery story.

**Encryption/recipient authority:** `backup create` derives and binds the effective outer set from the snapshot's keyring active recipient plus configured recovery-set generation; a caller holding `backup` cannot add/override a recipient on the command line. `backup recipient list|set` is separately gated by `backup-recipient-manage`, generation/CAS, exact confirmation, and reason. The configured recovery set has 1..7 unique fingerprints; the derived effective set has 2..8 and rejects equality with active. Private keys never enter the archive. Backup creation requires its own management capability (R22); runs through the reserved control lane so it works even when the data plane has failed closed (KTD8/R27).

**Acceptance:** backup taken during concurrent writes restores to a transactionally consistent point (no torn record); manifest fields complete and checksummed (unattended `backup create` = unsigned state per U10.1); `backup manifest sign` completes+registers the signature, after which the manifest verifies by signer key id/domain against the out-of-band authenticated U6.8 public trust lineage; recovery-recipient fingerprints present; archive contains no private key material (scan); works via control socket while serving.
**Plan-review output/recipient protocol:** write the one mode-0600 no-follow same-filesystem temp container and fsync it; before the publication reservation, every error/cancel removes it, while after reservation the authenticated `publishing` recovery protocol below governs rename/cleanup rather than blind deletion. Outer age encryption includes the snapshot-derived current active recipient for active-recipient offline rehearsal AND at least one distinct configured recovery recipient for recovery-only rehearsal; fingerprints are in the manifest and an empty/unproven recovery set refuses. Detached signature registration is idempotent and cannot mutate content digest. Test output collision/symlink, short write/ENOSPC/fsync/rename, cancellation, recipient separation, and safe logs.

**Authorization/audit cutoff:** backup is a U6.2 coordinator-authorized control operation using U3.6's backup capability. The consistent archive snapshot ends at audit sequence S and its manifest names S/head. Durable archive-output, detached-signature, and rehearsal-receipt events occur AFTER S and reference the archive/content digest; they are never falsely claimed to be members of the archive they describe. Cancellation/revocation before the final authorization barrier yields no published archive; lost final reply leaves a digest-addressable artifact with an audited outcome.

**Recipient attack tests:** an identity with only `backup` attempts an attacker-chosen recipient flag/config override and is refused before snapshot/output; wrong `backup-recipient-manage`, stale recovery-set generation, duplicate/active=recovery, empty recovery, eighth configured recovery (ninth effective recipient), malformed fingerprint, and revocation race make no change. List reveals fingerprints/generation only. Rotation invalidates affected rehearsal receipts and forces a new signed backup plus both offline modes.

**Manifest registration tests:** missing/wrong control credential, UID/audience/backup capability, revoked/disabled principal, final-barrier revocation, daemon unavailable, duplicate register, lost reply, changed archive, wrong domain/key id/lineage, short-write/fsync/rename, and stale signature. Local signing never sends private key to daemon and registration never treats filesystem ownership or key possession as control authority.

**Active-rewrap consistency:** the backup read transaction captures keyring generation/active fingerprint and recovery-set generation together, derives one effective-set digest, and uses those exact values for envelope/manifest/receipt. Concurrent U8.1 rewrap linearizes before or after snapshot; no archive mixes generations. Rewrap→next backup automatically uses the new active identity and needs no manual recipient-set repair.

**Operational prerequisite status:** a maintenance/key-rewrite preflight accepts this backup only when the exact detached manifest signature has audited registry status `registered` (never `abandoned` or `unknown_offline`) and the matching recovery-recipient full-rehearsal receipt is cryptographically valid AND audited `registered`, with identical archive, manifest, keyring generation, recovery-set generation, and effective-set digest. A valid detached file or local receipt without disposition proof is adequate for offline cryptographic verification but not for an operational completion prerequisite. Tests cover unknown-offline, abandoned, wrong covering checkpoint/chain segment, stale generation, valid-but-unregistered, registered, and lost-reply/idempotent registration.

**State-digest snapshot test:** independently recompute the non-audit state digest from the captured frames and require exact manifest equality; concurrent writes linearize wholly before/after the read transaction. The signed manifest binds it. A table digest or state-digest mismatch refuses rehearsal/restore/maintenance even if outer checksums were recomputed.

**Rollover signing tests:** backup before transition signs+registers under A, then rollover to B preserves verification; unsigned/unregistered A backup blocks rollover until audited abandoned, and afterward cannot register under A. Backup after transition signed with retired A refuses; current B succeeds. Lost reply/idempotent retry finishes while A is current. Tests document that public-only offline verification cannot prove signing time or withstand later A-private-key compromise.

**Abandon tests:** wrong digest/generation/reason/confirmation/capability, already registered, duplicate/lost reply, revocation race, and remote route. Registered cannot become abandoned; abandoned cannot later register under any key. Rollover refuses outstanding unsigned until this owner-scoped command succeeds.

**Authenticated output publication state machine:** build the G3 single container at a private no-follow same-filesystem temp, scan it, fsync it, and compute its immutable digest. At the final U6.2 barrier, reload backup authority and atomically reserve an opaque output id plus header/content/target-identity digests, signer key+lineage generation, owner, and `publishing` registry/audit state. Only after that durable reservation may the CLI rename to the final path and fsync the parent; a second idempotent U6.2 transaction records `published`. These are deliberately two durability domains, never described as one atomic outcome. Cancel/revocation before reservation removes the temp and publishes nothing. After reservation U6.1 started-command semantics apply: retry verifies the exact owned temp/final digest and finishes publication even if authority later changes; it may never substitute bytes or target. Reservation-with-temp, reservation-with-final-file, missing/corrupt temp, collision/symlink, crash after rename, parent-fsync uncertainty, second-transaction audit/capacity failure, cancellation, and lost reply each have one inspect/resume or owner-abandon outcome. A visible final file always has a prior registry reservation; `publishing` remains doctor-visible and blocks signer rollover until it reaches `published` and is signed+registered or `backup manifest abandon <manifest-digest>` resolves it.

**Trust-generation publication ordering:** the private temp freezes signer key id/lineage generation, but the reservation barrier rechecks they are still current. If A→B activation commits first, stale A temp bytes are never published and creation must rebuild under B. If A reservation commits first, U6.8's serialized outstanding inventory sees the A descriptor and refuses activation until registration or abandonment. Long snapshot/temp/fsync and transition races prove both linearizations. The backup snapshot cutoff remains S; `publishing`, `published`, signature, receipt, and reconciliation events are post-S operational tail events and are never falsely included in the container.

**Canonical backup descriptor identity:** compute `artifact_digest = BLAKE3(backup-artifact-v1 || canonical signing_header || encrypted_payload_digest)` before reservation. This is the registry/descriptor identity and the value exposed by the CLI as `<manifest-digest>` for `backup manifest abandon`; the inner encrypted recovery-manifest digest is stored separately for diagnostics. `publishing` persists both, so missing temp/final bytes never make owner abandonment impossible. List/doctor/retry/abandon round trips use the stored artifact digest and exact output id.

**Artifact catalog and publication recovery (binding):** exact owner-scoped commands are part of this bead:
- `backup list` is stable, cursor-bounded, and filterable by publication, manifest-signature, rehearsal, and disposition state.
- `backup show <manifest-digest>` returns the canonical artifact digest, opaque output id, snapshot cutoff/state digest prefix, signer/lineage, keyring/recovery/effective-recipient generations, `publishing|published|registered|abandoned` facts, active/recovery rehearsal dispositions, safe file-presence/digest status, and one exact next action—never a raw target path, member data, private recipient material, or secret.
- `backup resume <manifest-digest>` may only reconcile the exact authenticated `publishing` reservation. It verifies owned temp/final bytes and target identity, finishes rename/parent-fsync/`published` idempotently, or returns a typed owner-abandon-required result when bytes are missing/corrupt. It never takes a new snapshot, substitutes bytes/targets, signs/registers, or treats a merely published archive as recovery-current.

List/show/resume require the concrete `backup` capability and final-barrier reauthorization, run through the reserved lane (or the already-defined authenticated offline mode), and are absent remotely. A backup-only timer/operator can recover its own lost reply without receiving `diagnostics`; doctor remains a cross-domain view, not the only discovery mechanism.

**Tests/logging:** unit cases cover filters/cursors/state transitions/authorization/idempotency; integration/fault cases cover lost create replies and every temp/reservation/rename/fsync/outcome state, signer-rollover races, missing/corrupt bytes, target substitution, duplicate resume and owner abandon. E2E forces a lost reply, discovers the digest through list/show, resumes, then signs/registers/rehearses. Typed logs expose command/state/output id/safe digest prefixes/point/outcome/timing only and pass U11.6 before rendering/retention.

**Resume authorization precision:** resume authenticates and reauthorizes the CURRENT caller for `backup`; it does not require the original creator identity/grant to remain active and does not reinterpret revocation as cancellation of an already authorized `publishing` reservation. A different currently authorized backup owner may finish the exact immutable bytes. With no currently authorized owner, the state remains safely visible to owner-capable recovery after authority is restored; it is never auto-substituted or silently discarded.
notes: Review required. Conflict 1: G3 marks archive table IDs 13-18 required, but current redb schema has no live tables for signing trust, AppRole roles/usage, reserve, consumers, or rotations. Omitting them violates the frozen manifest; fabricating rows is unsafe. Resolution: emit canonical authenticated empty required frames only for absent schema owners, while every live durable table is captured byte-for-byte; final owner additions automatically replace empties through the registry coverage gate. Conflict 2: this bead requires live owner-socket create/catalog/registration while serving, but current serve/control router exposes only health/status and has no store/coordinator persistence adapter. Resolution consistent with repository integration-tail convention: implemented the complete backup domain, exact CLI surface, functional offline detached signing, single-transaction real-redb snapshot, recipient/recovery crypto, publication/catalog/retry/registration/preflight kernels, and routed persistence/live control assembly to scoped final-integration bead olss-charter-qul.12.14 in docs/integration-tail-ledger.json rather than adding an unauthenticated bypass. ARM64 execution is unavailable per user; x86_64 and architecture-independent canonical encoding evidence only. Verification: ./scripts/verify.sh run f3389ce4019668cfc4a2a4a216bf7f57 passed compatibility, vectors, fmt, strict clippy, all-target tests, docs, Rust 1.85 build/tests, harness clippy/tests/MSRV, and canary on Linux x86_64. Focused backup tests: 7 domain/storage/crypto/publication cases and 2 CLI cases passed. br dep cycles reports zero cycles.

title: U10.3: restore — verify-everything temp-sibling build, atomic install, credential-epoch bump (R32)
description:
**Build:** `restore` — construct a NEW database from an archive; verify EVERYTHING before activation; make credential resurrection impossible (R32).

**Flow:** under `lifecycle=restoring` (R35), under the independent parent restore lock, build a new database in an owned TEMPORARY SIBLING while the final target path is ABSENT (any pre-existing target path refuses untouched) → first enforce outer framing/size/member-count/checksums and verify the detached signature/unsigned-override authorization; only after that gate may it decode/decrypt table frames, verify every record MAC/ciphertext/audit relationship/schema/count — verified by signer key id and backup domain against the operator-supplied authenticated public trust-lineage bundle from the recovery package (U10.1/U6.8); an absent signature proceeds only with `--allow-unsigned-manifest` + audited reason + exact digest-bound confirmation, while any present invalid/mismatched signature always refuses → fsync the database and directory → ATOMICALLY install only after ALL verification succeeds. A tampered or corrupt archive leaves the target directory inert (AE7 tamper case).

**Credential epoch (the resurrection defense):** restore atomically increments `current_credential_epoch`. Every restored credential record retains its ORIGINAL issue_epoch, so ALL pre-restore tokens and secret_ids fail epoch validation (R24's MAC domain) even where verifier and TTL would otherwise pass — a restored snapshot cannot revive credentials revoked after it was taken. Identities, grants, secrets, and audit history survive; bearer credentials are reissued. The restore is recorded as a recovery event in the first new audit entry.

**Post-restore access bootstrap (the lockout defense):** the epoch bump also kills every pre-restore CONTROL credential, and no bootstrap route exists on any listener — so restore mints and discloses a replacement emergency control credential during activation, invoking R41's primitive (owned by U8.3, bead olss-charter-qul.10.3 — an explicit dependency: restore CALLS that implementation, never rebuilds it) with R17's disclosure-before-commit ordering: validate the credential output sink → generate → write+flush to the sink → only then complete the atomic install whose transaction carries its verifier. A failed sink write aborts activation with the target inert. The first new audit entry records the recovery event and the emergency issuance. The operator uses this credential to reissue normal operator/workload credentials — without it, a restored store would be unauthenticatable (G3 frozen rule, U10.1).

**Restore-incarnation marker (the split-brain defense):** the recovery event records a fresh restore-incarnation nonce; all subsequent audit entries/checkpoints carry it, so verify can name divergent incarnations. Activation requires a `--source-decommissioned` assertion binding store id; archive cutoff/source incarnation; `claimed_decommissioned`; `source_observation_status=barrier_confirmed|last_known|unavailable`; optional observed source incarnation/epoch/sequence/head, observation time and provenance; supplied tail range/digest with complete|partial|unavailable status; `rpo_status=known_range|unknown` plus explicit acknowledgment; target id/path digest; new target-active and chosen recovery fingerprints; installed keyring generation; actor; reason; and confirmation. `barrier_confirmed` requires clean stop, executor/store-lock barriers, a complete descending tail, and immediate final-head recheck; a post-assertion append stales it. `last_known` is non-final and `unavailable` permits fresh-host DR with no post-archive tuple; both record unknown risk rather than claiming proof. Wrong/internally inconsistent observation, tail, RPO, target, or recipient bindings refuse before disclosure/install.

**Inputs/installed recipient set:** before restore, generate a NEW target active identity with `key age-identity generate --purpose active` into approved target custody. Restore consumes two distinct typed private inputs: (1) one matching backup recovery-recipient identity to open the outer archive/recovery envelope, and (2) the new target-active private identity, from which it derives the active recipient and decrypt-self-tests the installed keyring before disclosure/install. Neither identity is persisted/logged/accepted via argv/env/plaintext file. The installed keyring set is exactly new target active plus one explicitly confirmed distinct keyring-recovery public recipient; by default derive it from the recovery identity used, or accept another explicit public recipient. It never copies all outer backup recipients. Wrong/equal/swapped identities or self-test failure leave target inert.

**Acceptance:** AE7 core — fresh-host restore: every secret readable, identities/grants intact, chain verifies against the last off-host checkpoint, pre-restore bearer credentials rejected (the revoked token STAYS revoked); the emergency control credential is disclosed exactly once, authenticates, and is what issues the fresh working credential (which then works); a shared-symbol/integration test proves restore invokes U8.3's primitive; tampered member or any present-but-invalid/mismatched detached signature → verification fails before activation, target inert; an absent signature refuses unless the full allow-unsigned+reason+confirmation ceremony succeeds; restore into any pre-existing target path → refused, path untouched; broken credential sink → activation aborts, target inert; the recovery event carries the incarnation nonce and subsequent entries/checkpoints carry it; redb-version upgrade between backup and restore succeeds without opening an old DB file (KTD17); every verification failure names the failing member/frame/table and check kind, distinguishing archive corruption vs identity/unwrap failure vs manifest-signature mismatch — never a bare 'verification failed'.
**Plan-review activation precision:** missing signature refuses unless BOTH `--allow-unsigned-manifest` and an audited `--reason` plus explicit confirmation are supplied; then emit a high-severity warning and record the exact archive digest. Reject symlink/non-empty/foreign targets no-follow; fsync installed DB, target directory, and parent before success. “Disclosed exactly once” means once for a SUCCESSFUL activation: a crash after sink flush but before install may disclose an unusable orphan, and retry safely produces a new value. Tests cover every sink/install/fsync boundary and distinguish orphan disclosure from activated credential.

**Parent-level restore exclusion/install shape:** before constructing a temporary DB, acquire a no-follow exclusive restore lock/marker in the target parent directory. The frozen target rule is “path absent” (not ambiguously empty); only the owned lock/temporary sibling may exist. Validate parent owner/mode/filesystem, build and fsync the sibling file/directory, atomically rename into the absent target, and fsync the parent. Every marker/temp/install crash combination has one authoritative retry/cleanup rule.

**Activation event/state rules:** normal restore increments credential epoch exactly once and its first new audit event is `restore_activation`, containing the emergency-issuance facts and a G2 bulk transition from archived state digest to installed state digest. Rollback mode delegates first-event ownership to U10.4's fork genesis; it does NOT also prepend a normal restore event or double-increment the credential epoch. The shared U8.3 recovery principal is control-only, short-lived, and proven able to run identity/grant/credential recovery. Its online/offline disclosure uses the typed precommit sink handshake and refuses persistent plaintext regular/block sinks.

Activation is explicitly unanchored until the first post-restore external checkpoint registers; verification reports that fact and M2 evidence performs the checkpoint before calling the recovery verified.

**Trust-lineage verification vs installed state:** restore verifies archive/checkpoint signatures made by current or retired keys through the authenticated out-of-band lineage, rejecting missing/forked/replayed lineage, archive-embedded replacement roots, wrong domain, and unknown ids. But normal restore installs the trust registry/current signer contained inside the archive and never imports a post-archive transition. If supplied lineage shows an effective transition after the archive head/generation, normal mode refuses as rollback-required even with later checkpoints withheld. The normal post-rollover fixture therefore uses a fresh B backup whose payload already contains B; its first restore checkpoint is B-signed. The older A backup remains verifiable/rehearsable, but can acquire B only through U10.4 fork genesis.

**Post-activation anchor state:** normal restore installs `lifecycle=ready` plus durable `pending_anchor=normal-restore`; rollback mode installs `pending_anchor=rollback-fork` under U10.4. Ordinary service may resume with a conspicuous unanchored warning, but bulk rewrite, migration, compaction, second restore activation, and key jobs refuse. Diagnostics, backup/read-only verify, revocation/emergency control, and the exact checkpoint ceremony remain available. The first external `audit checkpoint prepare → sign → register` over the installed incarnation PLUS a passing state verify clears the matching marker through an audited U6.2 transition (register alone never clears — U10-root/U10.6 scenario-19 semantics); only then may recovery evidence/doctor call it externally anchored. A crash/lost reply before install leaves target inert; after install it leaves the ready+pending marker authoritative and retry resumes checkpoint completion, never reissues epochs/credentials or repeats activation.

**Verification-order tests:** malformed outer framing/limits/checksum, invalid signer/domain/lineage, and unauthorized unsigned override never enter member/table decode hooks. Valid signature then corrupt member reaches the exact frame/table verifier. This cheap-authentication-first order is part of the frozen restore threat boundary.

Restore recomputes the manifest's non-audit state digest from the reconstructed store before activation and compares exact bytes; the first activation/fork event then records archived and installed state commitments. A mismatch is corruption and is never covered by the unsigned override.

**Target-identity race defense:** immediately before credential disclosure/install, rederive/recheck the target-active identity, active+recovery fingerprints, keyring generation, and assertion/confirmation digest. Input swap, stale fingerprint, equality, using the archive recovery identity as routine active identity, or changed target refuses. Activation/fork event records only the fingerprints/generation/assertion digest. E2E boots the restored daemon using ONLY the new target-active identity while source-active and archive-recovery private identities are unavailable, then proves offline rewrap through the installed recovery path.

**Post-restore credential handoff:** use the emergency control credential to issue a labeled finite normal admin control token through U4.5, authenticate that token on a named command, revoke the emergency accessor, prove emergency rejection, and use the normal token thereafter. A restore is not operationally handed off merely because emergency auth succeeds; lost-response/orphan rules apply.

**Disposition reporting:** restore never calls a detached signature “registered” from archive/signature/lineage alone. It reports valid signature + unknown_offline disposition, or verifies an optional out-of-band complete authenticated chain segment from artifact cutoff through the registration/abandonment event to its covering checkpoint, including needed audit-payload generations. Default acceptance is signature validity; operational disposition is separate and never forged into certainty.

**Normal-activation source record:** `restore_activation` binds the assertion digest and records archive cutoff, observation status/provenance, optional observed tuple, unarchived range/tail digest/completeness, known|unknown RPO acknowledgment, and intentionally dropped-tail classification. Only barrier-confirmed evidence is labeled final; last-known/unavailable are explicitly non-final. Complete supplied evidence containing a newer checkpoint or effective signing transition delegates to U10.4 before any target/disclosure mutation; incomplete evidence remains an explicit limitation surfaced by verify/doctor.

**First-checkpoint signer possession:** before normal or fork activation, the local restore process loads the approved off-host private-key source matching the signer that will be current in the installed trust state, signs a domain-separated nonce challenge bound to archive/assertion/target, verifies it against that public descriptor, and zeroizes it; the key never enters the daemon. This proves usable custody but is not control authorization. Missing/wrong/unusable current-key custody refuses before emergency disclosure/temp install with exact fingerprint remediation. v0.1 has no implicit new root or degraded unanchorable activation mode.
notes:
Completion review (2026-07-17):
Conflict: U10.3 requires restore to verify/import complete signing-lineage and every ciphertext, but G3 archive registry reserves table IDs 13-24 while the current durable redb schema has only tables 1-12; U10.2 therefore emits canonical empty frames for the reserved tables. The authoritative integration-tail ledger assigns signing_trust.restore_and_fork_import to dependent olss-charter-qul.11.4. Also legacy SecretRecord rows and current EncryptedRecord rows share the secrets frame, so blindly AEAD-opening every row would reject valid retained-format archives.
Resolution: normal restore verifies the signed container before unwrap, exact frame summaries/state digest, complete encrypted audit chain, and all currently authenticated identity/grant/credential/epoch records; it never invents reserved rows. CLI requires an authenticated public-key candidate and proves possession of its matching off-host private signer with a domain-separated archive/assertion/target challenge. Full retained-lineage import, historical interval verification, post-archive-transition rollback delegation, and legacy/current secret-row format dispatch remain with olss-charter-qul.11.4 as the existing named integration tail. This is safer than treating empty reserved frames as trust state or guessing ciphertext codecs.
Implementation: fresh-host no-follow/private-parent lock; absent target; logical redb reconstruction; atomic new-active+recovery rewrap; shared U8.3 epoch preparation; disclosure-before-commit; one restore_activation whole-state event; fresh incarnation; ready+pending_anchor=normal-restore; mode-0600 sibling rename/fsync; typed-source functional CLI; operating procedure.
Verification: focused restore/shared-epoch/rewrap/store suites; cargo fmt; strict clippy; cargo test --locked --all-targets --all-features; complete local scripts/verify.sh CI matrix including self-test, compat, lock/MSRV, x86_64 crypto vectors, docs, native/MSRV tests, harness, and canary gate all passed. Native ARM64 crypto-vector execution unavailable by operator statement and remains externally unverified.

title: U10.4: rollback/fork recovery — supplied newer checkpoint or signing lineage
description:
**Build:** the rollback classifier and explicit recovery-fork activation for an archive behind authenticated evidence supplied from the canonical source incarnation (KTD17/R32). This bead never guesses that the operator supplied a complete mirror; it makes the evidence basis and limitation machine-visible.

**Rollback-required predicate:** validate the archive tuple `{store, source incarnation, audit epoch, sequence/head, trust-lineage generation/current signer}` against two independently supplied sets: (1) external checkpoints and (2) the authenticated public trust-lineage bundle. Rollback is required when a valid same-source checkpoint descends beyond the archive head OR a valid effective signing transition postdates the archive lineage/head, even if the newer checkpoint set is withheld. Every checkpoint/transition is checked for root, old-key signature, domain, store/incarnation/epoch, ordering, descendant/effective position, generation, bounds, replay, and fork consistency before it can influence classification. Malformed, forked, unrelated, or mutually inconsistent supplied evidence refuses; it is never silently ignored.

**Honest completeness limit:** the tool can evaluate only supplied evidence. If BOTH all later checkpoints and all post-archive effective lineage transitions are withheld, an older archive can look normal; a later `audit verify` given the complete mirror/lineage exposes the divergence or omitted anchored range. Output and docs state `evidence_completeness=operator_supplied`, never “latest globally.” A complete authenticated source tail, when available, is also scanned for checkpoint/transition events. Ordinary unanchored source-tail loss is recorded by U10.3's explicit RPO assertion; later checkpoint or trust evidence changes the mode to fork.

**Behavior and authority:** without `--start-recovery-epoch`, rollback-required input refuses before credential disclosure, temp build, or target mutation and names the archive tuple, triggering evidence digests, and exact remedy. The flag requires a non-secret reason, source-decommission/RPO assertion, exact digest-bound confirmation, and the normal restore authority/recipient/target contract. With it, activation increments credential and audit epochs exactly once. Credential/audit epoch overflow, stale confirmation, authorization revocation, wrong target/recipient, or changed evidence refuses before disclosure/install.

**Explicit trust import:** normal restore never updates archive trust. Fork mode may import the complete verified public lineage from the archive's trusted root through the selected current signer B, including every ordered A→…→B transition and effective position. The fork genesis labels this an imported recovery-trust snapshot and binds its canonical digest, source generation/current signer, evidence-set digest, and verification result; it does not splice those post-archive transitions into a supposedly continuous old chain. Missing/forked/replayed/oversized/wrong-store lineage, a root not anchored by the archive/recovery package, or an ambiguous current signer refuses. No private A key is needed: after import, the independently protected current B private key signs the first new-epoch checkpoint.

**First-event ownership:** rollback activation's first new-epoch event is one fork genesis—there is no preceding normal-restore event and no double epoch increment. It records archived and installed state digests, restored tuple, missing anchored checkpoint range, supplied checkpoint-set digest, imported-lineage digest/current signer, archive/container/signature digests, complete U10.3 assertion digest (archive cutoff; observation status/provenance and optional observed tuple; tail range/digest/completeness; known|unknown RPO acknowledgment; target and recipient bindings), emergency recovery-principal issuance, actor, and reason. Future verify always reports the historical anchored epoch, dropped source tail, and recovery fork separately.

**Evidence/branch isolation:** each supplied checkpoint set must be internally consistent for ONE canonical source incarnation. The normal-restored branch and rollback-fork branch are isolated descendants of the same decommissioned original source; a checkpoint created on the normal-restored incarnation is recorded separately and must not be mixed into the original-source set used to justify the fork. Mixing divergent incarnations fails consistency rather than being treated as a newer checkpoint. Only a barrier-confirmed source observation is final-head rechecked and staled by a post-assertion append; last-known/unavailable evidence remains explicitly non-final/unknown.

**Fork pending anchor:** the installed target is `lifecycle=ready` with durable `pending_anchor=rollback-fork`. Ordinary service may resume with the documented warning, while conflicting bulk rewrite/migration/compaction/restore/key jobs refuse. The emergency credential issues/tests a finite normal admin token and is revoked. Using that normal token, the first B checkpoint prepare→sign→register binds fork genesis, imported-lineage digest, incarnation, and state; `audit verify --state` then clears the exact marker idempotently. Until this succeeds doctor is non-green and the fork is not recovery-verified. Before activation, U10.3's typed possession challenge must succeed for imported current key B; total current-key loss refuses activation and never triggers implicit re-enrollment or a degraded hidden mode.

**Tests:** checkpoint-only, lineage-only with checkpoints withheld, both evidence types, both withheld limitation, later full-mirror detection, malformed/forked/replayed transition, wrong root/domain/store/incarnation/effective position, lineage count/size limits, transition/checkpoint disagreement, and current-B-with-no-A-private-key. Cover missing flag/reason/confirmation, evidence/epoch overflows, observation-status/provenance/optional-head/tail/RPO mismatch, post-assertion append, target identity races, source-set vs divergent-normal-branch mixing, every disclosure/install/pending-anchor kill, lost reply/idempotency, emergency→normal handoff, first B checkpoint, and canary-clean typed logs.
notes:
needs-user-review — conflict/resolution/evidence (2026-07-17):
Conflicts: U10.4 requires checking checkpoint incarnation, but frozen CheckpointDescriptor v2 has no incarnation field. It also asks the rollback owner to reverify every old-key transition signature, while the canonical SigningLineage stores activated transition digests/public entries rather than detached SignedSigningTransition objects. Finally the existing restore installer implements only normal restore; splicing a second partial filesystem installer here would duplicate its credential disclosure/atomic install authority.
Chosen resolution: SuppliedCheckpoint binds an authenticated evidence-package source_incarnation alongside the signed checkpoint and includes both in the evidence digest; mixed incarnations fail. Checkpoints are cryptographically verified against CheckpointTrust. SigningLineage is accepted only as an out-of-band authenticated canonical bundle whose transitions were verified during activation; its root, ordered generations, current signer, epoch/effective positions, bounds and source-incarnation binding are revalidated. The module produces a complete digest-bound fork genesis and RollbackFork PendingAnchor for the common restore installer rather than duplicating install/disclosure code.
Implementation: strict checkpoint/lineage classifier, operator_supplied completeness, checkpoint-only and lineage-only rollback triggers, withheld-both honest limitation, inconsistent/malformed/mixed-source refusal, evidence/import/genesis digests, explicit --start-recovery-epoch semantics, full authority/possession/RPO/target confirmation, one-time credential/audit epoch increments with overflow refusal, one fork genesis and installed pending anchor.
Verification: cargo test --test recovery_fork --test restore --test checkpoint --test signing_trust => 24/24 passed. cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings passed. Tests cover checkpoint-only, lineage-only, withheld limitation, tampered signature, mixed incarnation, flag/authority/current-signer-possession/epoch overflow, exact increment and pending anchor. Durable filesystem activation, emergency-token handoff and first-B checkpoint integration remain dependent on the common restore/control assembly and later U10.6 acceptance suite; no claim of installed-daemon E2E is made.

title: U10.5: backup verify + --full disaster-recovery rehearsal with recorded age (R32)
description:
**Build:** `backup verify` (manifest/frame check without restoring) + `backup verify --full` — the STANDING DISASTER-RECOVERY REHEARSAL (R32).

**--full flow:** restore into an isolated temporary directory → validate schema and tables → authenticate and DECRYPT EVERY ENCRYPTED RECORD without emitting any plaintext → verify the audit chain and checkpoint relationship → tear the temporary store down → record the result; the rehearsal work dir is operator-selectable and defaults OFF the store filesystem; the offline verifier refuses when projected temp usage crosses the chosen filesystem's documented safety threshold; control-lane use is limited to authenticated receipt registration, and docs warn that a same-filesystem verifier can still pressure live capacity.

**Two offline result kinds — the distinction IS the DR guarantee:** U2.3 zeroizes the boot age identity after opening the live keyring, so the running daemon is never assumed to retain it. The isolated verifier receives either the active-recipient identity through an approved FD and records **"integrity-verified (active-recipient path)"**, or receives ONLY a distinct recovery-recipient identity and records **"DR-rehearsed (recovery path)"**. Only the second proves fresh-host disaster recovery. Doctor reports both ages and invalidates receipts when the corresponding recipient set changes.

**Rehearsal record:** every `backup verify --full` run is offline and binds the exact archive manifest/content digest (last month's rehearsal proves nothing about last night's). It emits a detached signed receipt containing result, mode, recipient-set generation, tool version, and timestamp under the distinct rehearsal domain. `backup rehearsal record` is the SOLE authenticated control-lane registration step: it verifies signer key id/domain against the stored U6.8 public trust lineage, checks archive/recipient generation, and atomically audits/stores the receipt. There is no control-lane verification mode. Doctor flags when the LATEST backup's digest has no rehearsal of either kind.

**Posture (docs must say it plainly):** a backup that has never survived a rehearsal is UNPROVEN; one never rehearsed through the recovery recipient is unproven as DR. Ordinary corruption is caught by manifest/member checksums. Full rehearsal additionally proves recipient unwrap, AEAD/MAC validity, schema relationships, and defeats a recomputed-checksum unsigned/forged container.

**Acceptance:** --full on a good archive → passes, temp store removed, NO plaintext emitted or logged (canary scan), recorded rehearsal age queryable; offline --full with the distinct recovery-recipient identity unwraps via the RECOVERY envelope and records DR-rehearsed; offline --full with a MISMATCHED recovery-recipient identity → fails naming the recovery envelope (not a generic decrypt error); ordinary corrupted ciphertext → manifest verification fails its checksum; a separate recomputed-checksum unsigned/forged fixture reaches full verification and fails AEAD naming the record; active-recipient rehearsal records integrity-verified, distinct from DR-rehearsed; `backup rehearsal record` registers an offline receipt; doctor distinguishes both ages and flags an unrehearsed latest backup.
**Plan-review rehearsal precision:** active-recipient mode runs in the offline verifier with that identity supplied through an approved FD; recovery mode deliberately withholds it and succeeds only via the distinct recovery identity. Ordinary ciphertext corruption must first fail manifest checksum; a separate forged/unsigned fixture recomputes checksums and must then fail AEAD authentication, proving defense in depth. Cleanup, secret-temp deletion, and U11.6 scan run on every success/failure/signal. Rehearsal receipt binds archive content digest, manifest/signature state, identity-kind (active-recipient/recovery-recipient), tool version, result and timestamp without plaintext.

**No-activation reuse:** both modes call the exact restore archive parser, table registry, MAC/AEAD/audit/state verifiers, and temporary-build code in a non-activating rehearsal mode. They never install a target, bump either epoch, open a recovery fork, or issue a credential. Only a signed receipt or locally authenticated control registration records the outcome; raw identity material never reaches the daemon/logs.

**Trust rollover:** the offline verifier consumes the recovery package's authenticated public trust-lineage bundle, never a root supplied only by the archive. Receipts/manifests/checkpoints signed by retired verification keys remain valid when their key id follows that lineage; wrong/forked/unknown/domain-confused lineage refuses. Tests rehearse a pre-rollover archive after rollover and register its receipt safely.

**Receipt sign/register authority:** every offline full verifier optionally loads the U6.8 signing key through a separate approved typed source only after archive verification, signs the immutable receipt under the rehearsal domain, writes/fsyncs it detached, and zeroizes the key; the age identity and signing key are distinct inputs/roles. `backup rehearsal record` requires service-owner SO_PEERCRED plus a non-disabled control-audience credential with concrete `backup`, reloads grants at the reserved-lane final barrier, then atomically audits/registers by receipt digest. Wrong UID/audience/capability, revoked/disabled principal, revocation race, daemon unavailable, duplicate/lost reply, signer lineage/domain mismatch, and changed recipient generation are tested; retry is idempotent.

**Receipt signer eligibility:** because the receipt originates offline, `backup rehearsal record` accepts only a receipt signed by the key CURRENT at registration under the rehearsal domain; a rollover between offline sign and registration requires re-signing with the new current key. Already registered historical receipts remain verifiable through lineage. Tests reject new registration under a retired/stolen key while preserving old registered receipts.

Receipt offline verification likewise does not infer a trusted signature time. Registration while the signer is current is the operational cutoff; after rollover an unregistered old-key receipt must be re-signed with the new current key. Registered historical receipts remain lineage-verifiable subject to the documented retired-private-key compromise limitation.

Offline receipt verification likewise separates valid signature from server registration. Without a complete authenticated chain segment from receipt/archive cutoff through registration to the covering checkpoint, with needed audit-payload generations it reports unknown_offline; doctor registration state comes from the live store. No tool infers disposition from the detached receipt alone.

**Receipt time semantics:** the detached receipt carries signer-claimed `performed_at` for operator context, authenticated by the signature but not a trusted clock or cryptographic signing time. The server registration event supplies effective-time `registered_at`; doctor warning/expiry age uses `registered_at` for the exact latest archive/recipient generations and labels it as evidence-registration freshness, not proof of execution time. Offline-only verification reports claimed time plus `registration_status=unknown_offline` and no operational freshness age. Future/backdated claimed times never extend freshness. Tests cover old receipt newly registered, future/backdated claims, clock repair/high-water behavior, rollover, and offline display.
notes: Conflict/assembly seams: U10.5 requires live authenticated receipt registration and doctor freshness reporting, but the daemon control catalog/adapter and doctor are explicitly owned by downstream U12.14 and U12.1; it also requires authenticated public trust-lineage import from recovery packages, while that durable restore/fork import is owned by U10.4. Implementing fake in-memory daemon persistence or trusting archive-supplied roots here would violate the charter. Resolution: implement the complete offline verifier/rehearsal kernel and canonical CLI surfaces now; keep backup rehearsal record fail-closed through the existing live-control adapter pending boundary, expose a deterministic authority-complete/idempotent registration kernel and evidence-age query for the downstream adapters, and retain the existing unique tails backup.live_control_catalog and signing_trust.restore_and_fork_import. Implementation: backup verify and --full typed-source CLI; strict outer/signature gate; active vs recovery envelope result; shared non-activating restore parser; isolated mode-0700 temp store with capacity refusal and unconditional cleanup; every secret AEAD plus metadata MAC, identities/grants/credentials/epoch, state digest, audit chain/payload, and checkpoint relationship verification; detached domain-separated signed/fsynced mode-0600 receipt with exact archive/manifest/generations/mode/tool/claimed time and private-key zeroization; current-signer/exact-generation/service-owner/control-audience/backup-capability/final-barrier registration kernel with idempotent retry and effective registration age; operator posture/capacity/time semantics docs. Ordinary outer corruption fails before unwrap; a recomputed-checksum, re-encrypted, re-signed forged record reaches reconstruction and fails AEAD without canary disclosure. Verification: focused backup_verify 4/4 and restore 4/4; all-target/all-feature tests passed; strict all-target/all-feature clippy passed after correction; full local ./scripts/verify.sh run d75328abfb74d946589644f9467f307d passed compat pins/capture, lock/MSRV, x86_64 crypto vectors, fmt, clippy, all-target tests, docs, Rust 1.85 build/tests, harness clippy/tests/MSRV, and canary scan. No hosted CI ran. ARM64 execution remains externally unavailable per user.

title: U10.6: backup/restore test suite (tests/backup.rs — AE7, epoch, fork, rehearsal)
description:
**Build:** tests/backup.rs — the backup/restore suite. All scenarios log through the shared test-observability harness (U11.9a).

**Scenarios:**
1. AE7: backup a running server; restore onto a fresh data directory → every secret readable, identities + grants intact, audit chain verifies against the last off-host checkpoint, pre-restore bearer credentials rejected (a token revoked AFTER the backup stays revoked — epoch, not luck); the replacement emergency control credential disclosed during restore authenticates and is what issues the fresh credential, which then authenticates (no other issuance path exists — every pre-restore credential is dead).
2. Backup during concurrent writes → restores to a transactionally consistent point, no torn record.
3. Restore a backup predating a token revocation → that token stays invalid (credential epoch).
4. Tamper with one archive member → verification fails BEFORE activation; target directory stays inert.
5. Archive behind the newest supplied checkpoint without --start-recovery-epoch → refused; with it → verifier reports old anchored history, missing range, and the new audit epoch — never a continuous chain (KTD17).
6. Upgrade redb while the logical archive version stays supported → restore succeeds into the new redb version without opening an old database file (KTD17 — this test is what keeps R38's prior-release smoke fixture viable).
7. Restore without a usable age identity → fails, no partial initialization.
